description: An elevated Korean dining experience in the Flatiron area that treats Korean cuisine with the reverence and refinement it deserves. The banchan spreads are beautiful, the proteins are prepared with serious technique, and the cocktail program incorporates Korean flavors seamlessly. Korean food for a special occasion.

name: Rezdôra
description: One Michelin star for Emilia-Romagna Italian that has Flatiron pasta lovers in a permanent state of devotion. Stefano Secchi's handmade tortellini in brodo, the tagliatelle al ragu, and the prosciutto di Parma are all extraordinary. The wait for a table is notorious — Resy releases go fast. The bartender's choice pasta at the bar is the insider move. One of the best Italian restaurants in New York.
bookingInfo: Opens 30 days ahead at midnight ET on Resy. Midnight releases go fast.

name: Kosaka
description: A serene West Village omakase counter from chef Yoshihiko Kousaka that is one of the city most underrated high-end sushi experiences. The rice is exceptional -- perfectly seasoned and at the ideal temperature -- and the fish selection reflects decades of expertise. Intimate, quiet, and focused entirely on the craft.

name: Shmoné
description: One Michelin star for Israeli cooking in Greenwich Village from Eyal Shani, the man behind a global empire of vibrant Mediterranean restaurants. The whole cauliflower is the showstopper — roasted until charred and caramelized, served whole. The lamb shoulder and the warm pita bread are both outstanding. The energy is festive, the portions are generous, and the cooking is joyful.

name: Frevo
description: One Michelin star for a 14-seat counter hidden behind an art gallery in Greenwich Village. Brazilian chef Franco Sampogna serves a $245 tasting menu that fuses South American soul with French technique. The intimacy is the point — you're watching every dish come together inches from your face. One of the most unique dining experiences in New York. Book on Resy months ahead.

name: Jeju Noodle Bar
description: One Michelin star for Korean ramyun in the West Village — the first noodle shop in history to earn a star, and it earned it honestly. Chef Douglas Kim's bowls are deeply flavored, the broth is complex, and the handmade noodles have a chew that's addictive. The space is tiny, the wait is real, and every bowl feels like it was made specifically for you. Resy reservations.

name: Family Meal at Blue Hill
description: One Michelin star for Dan Barber's farm-to-table vision in Greenwich Village. Family Meal at Blue Hill serves a $145 four-course family-style dinner with ingredients sourced from Stone Barns farm. The cooking is deceptively simple — seasonal vegetables that taste more like themselves than you thought possible. Weekend lunch ($85) is the accessible entry point. Resy reservations.
address: 75 W Washington Pl, New York, NY 10011

name: Tsukimi
description: One Michelin star for East Village kaiseki from chef Takanori Akiyama. The $250 dinner tasting menu unfolds with the quiet precision of a Japanese tea ceremony — each course seasonal, each plate beautiful. The space is intimate and serene. This is not the flashy sushi spots of Midtown. This is Japanese cooking at its most meditative and precise. Book on Resy. Allow yourself to slow down.

name: Yoshino
description: One Michelin star for a 20-course omakase in NoHo at $500 per person. Tadashi Yoshida's restaurant, named for the Japanese town where his father was born, is one of the most ambitious sushi experiences in the city. The aged fish program is extraordinary. The rice is perfect. The counter seats 10. If you're going to spend $500 on sushi, this is where you should spend it.

name: Torien
description: One Michelin star for a 13-course yakitori tasting menu ($185) from chef Yoshiteru Ikegawa, who runs the legendary Torishiki in Tokyo. Every part of the chicken is grilled over binchōtan charcoal with reverence — heart, liver, skin, thigh, tail. The seasonal vegetable courses are equally impressive. NoHo location. Book on Resy. The most refined chicken dinner of your life.

name: Tuome
description: One Michelin star for Asian-inflected American small plates in the East Village. Thomas Chen's Tuome has been quietly excellent for years — the Berkshire pork for two ($69) with crisp skin and spicy peanut noodle is the dish that defines the restaurant. The duck confit dumplings and the tuna tartare are both outstanding. Book on Resy. Underrated and underhyped.

name: Bar Miller
description: One Michelin star for a sustainable omakase in the East Village. Bar Miller, sibling to Rosella, serves a 15-course $250 menu focused on responsibly sourced seafood. The philosophy is admirable and the cooking backs it up — every course reflects careful sourcing and skilled technique. The space is intimate and modern. A Michelin star earned the right way.

name: Restaurant Yuu
description: One Michelin star for a French-Japanese tasting menu in Greenpoint. Chef Yuu Shimano and executive chef Shuji Furukawa serve a $300 dinner that fuses Japanese precision with French luxury. The result is quietly stunning — dishes that look minimal and taste extraordinary. One of the most interesting new Michelin additions. Brooklyn fine dining at its most refined.

name: L'Abeille
description: One Michelin star for French-Japanese fusion in Tribeca. Chef Mitsunobu Nagae serves a $255 tasting menu and $190 shorter option, plus a la carte. The Japanese inflections on classic French technique create dishes that feel both familiar and surprising. The Tribeca townhouse setting is elegant. OpenTable reservations. The kind of restaurant that serious food people whisper about.

name: Muku
description: One Michelin star earned at just 70 days old — the youngest restaurant ever to receive the honor. Chef Manabu Asanuma's kaiseki features soba made with flour from his parents' farm in Japan. The 10-12 course $295 tasting menu in Tribeca is personal, seasonal, and deeply connected to its roots. A beautiful story told through extraordinary food. Book on Resy.

name: 63 Clinton
description: One Michelin star for a seven-course American tasting menu ($112) on the Lower East Side. Chefs Sam Clonts and Raymond Trinh created one of the most affordable Michelin-starred experiences in the city. The cooking is creative, seasonal, and unpretentious. The LES location gives it a neighborhood energy that most tasting menu spots lack. Resy reservations. An absolute steal.

name: Shion 69 Leonard
description: One Michelin star for Edomae-style sushi at a Tribeca counter. Founding chef Shion Uino departed in late 2025 and the restaurant was temporarily closed after a fire; check current status before booking. When open, the $400 omakase is focused and elegant — pristine fish, warm rice, and a quiet intensity. The aged tuna and the kohada are consistently extraordinary. The space at 69 Leonard is intimate and serious. Resy reservations. For sushi purists only.

name: Huso
description: One Michelin star for Top Chef winner Buddha Lo's Tribeca restaurant inside a Marky's Caviar shop. The 12-course $285 tasting menu features caviar in nearly every course, which sounds excessive until you taste it — each application is different and justified. Lo earned his star by being fearless and precise. The location inside a caviar shop is peak New York absurdity. Resy.

name: Corima
description: One Michelin star for Mexican fine dining in Chinatown from chef Fidel Caballero, who evolved his acclaimed pop-ups into a permanent restaurant. The $125 tasting menu features creative mole courses, duck taquiza, and a la carte options. The cooking is deeply personal and rooted in Mexican tradition. One of the most original restaurants in the city. Resy reservations.

name: Icca
description: One Michelin star for a Financial District omakase that offers both a $495 sushi bar experience and a $195 Italian-inspired Kappo at tables. Chef Kazushige Suzuki trained in both traditions, and the dual-concept approach is genuinely unique. The sushi is pristine. The Italian Kappo is playful. Choose your adventure. One of the most creative restaurant concepts in downtown Manhattan.

name: Bridges
description: A creative New American spot tucked into Chinatown that draws on the neighborhood multicultural energy for a menu that is inventive and soulful. The space has a cool, slightly moody vibe that matches the food ambition, and the cocktails are thoughtfully composed. Exactly the kind of chef-driven restaurant that makes Chinatown exciting.

name: Shota Omakase
description: One Michelin star for Edomae sushi in Williamsburg. Chef Cheng Lin serves two omakase options — a premium 18-course ($195) and the Gentei 19-course ($255). The fish is pristine, the rice is warm, and the intimate counter setting is pure focus. One of the best sushi values in Brooklyn at the Michelin level. Resy reservations. A quiet alternative to Manhattan's sushi temples.

name: The Four Horsemen
description: One Michelin star for a natural wine bar that happens to serve some of the best food in Williamsburg. Chef Nick Curtola's constantly changing menu pairs creative small plates with an extraordinary selection of minimal-intervention wines. The Four Horsemen is a decade old and still commands lines. LCD Soundsystem's James Murphy is a co-owner. The most effortlessly cool restaurant in Brooklyn.

name: Meju
description: One Michelin star for a Korean tasting menu hidden in the back of a Long Island City provisions store. Hooni Kim's Meju serves a $235 menu that's intimate, creative, and deeply Korean. The banchan alone is worth the trip. Per Se alum Dae Kim consults. The strangest, most wonderful dining location in Queens — behind a shelf of kimchi jars. Resy reservations.

name: Le Veau d'Or
description: A legendary French restaurant on the Upper East Side that was revived after its original 1937 incarnation closed, bringing back old-school French bistro charm with a modern sensibility. The room drips with nostalgia -- red banquettes, brass fixtures -- and the menu delivers faithful renditions of French classics. A love letter to a bygone era of New York dining.

name: Claud
description: Below-street-level East Village wine bar that became one of the most desirable casual spots in the city without ever raising its voice. The red shrimp sizzling in garlic oil is the signature, the half chicken is a sleeper hit, and the chocolate cake slab is non-negotiable dessert. The wine list is eclectic and affordable. The table under the stairs is the coveted people-watching seat. Book on Resy.

name: Cafe Commerce
description: Harold Moore brought his beloved Commerce back from the dead, this time on the Upper East Side. The sweet potato tortelloni with hazelnuts is the hit that followed him, and the Parker House rolls in the bread basket are dangerous. The coconut cake is legendary — do not leave without it. Cinnamon rolls available for weekend pickup. The kind of chef-owned neighborhood spot every block deserves.

name: Grand Central Oyster Bar
description: Grand Central Oyster Bar has occupied this vaulted, tiled basement since 1913, and the architecture alone is worth the trip. Twenty-five varieties of oysters on the half shell, pan roasts that warm your soul, and a New England clam chowder that sets the standard. Closed weekends, which is your loss and also the point — this is a power-lunch destination. The bar is the best seat in the house.

name: Txikito
description: Alex Raij and Eder Montero's Chelsea Basque restaurant captures everything that makes San Sebastian dining magical. The bomba rice with shrimp, uni, and mushrooms is extraordinary. The octopus carpaccio, the boquerones, and the wood-fired peppers are all textbook pintxo bar perfection. The wine list is Iberian and goes deep. Ask about specials — gooseneck barnacles when in season are a revelation.

name: Chama Mama
description: Georgian cuisine's breakout moment in New York, and Chama Mama delivers. The khachapuri — a boat of bread filled with molten cheese, butter, and a runny egg — is the dish that made this place famous. The charcoal kebabs and dumpling-like khinkali are both excellent. The Georgian wine list features varietals you've never heard of and will immediately want to drink more of. Three locations now.

name: Adda
description: The Unapologetic Foods team relocated Adda from Long Island City to the East Village, doubled the space, and won Eater's Best Comeback Award. The goat biryani is a sleeper hit, the butter chicken experience requires advance ordering and is worth the planning, and the vegetarian menu is genuinely exceptional. Chef Chintan Pandya doesn't do timid at any of his restaurants, and Adda is his most accessible.

name: Sunn's
description: Sunny Lee's Chinatown wine bar won Eater's Best Wine Bar award, and the banchan alone is worth the trip. Every dish is vegetable-forward and creative — even the house salad is extraordinary in her hands. The natural wine list is fun and affordable. After a glowing NYT review, Lee set aside more walk-in spots. The space is tiny, the flavors are huge, and the hospitality is genuinely warm.

name: Sailor
description: April Bloomfield's triumphant return to New York after a complicated departure. The Fort Greene restaurant with Gabriel Stulman is her at the top of her game — the herb-butter roasted half chicken with Parmesan potatoes is reason enough to go to Brooklyn. The chicken liver mousse toast is rich and perfect. The spicy ginger cake for dessert is aggressively good. House wine is $9 a glass — a rare value.

name: Red Hook Tavern
description: Billy Durney's Red Hook tavern is the neighborhood restaurant everyone wishes was in their neighborhood. The burger is one of the best in Brooklyn — a thick, juicy, perfectly seared patty with melted American cheese. The oysters are fresh, the roast chicken is excellent, and the vibe is polished but unpretentious. Walk-ins welcome. The kind of place where a random Tuesday becomes special.

name: Bong
description: One of NYC's hottest new restaurants, and earning it honestly. Chef Chakriya Un and partner Alexander Chaparro transformed their Kreung Cambodia pop-up into a Crown Heights gem celebrating Khmer cuisine. The coconut corn, the fried squid with salted duck egg, and the crispy whole fish are all extraordinary. The space is tiny and colorful — every detail handmade by their community. Resy reservations are brutal.

name: Rolo's
description: A red-sauce Italian-American joint in Bushwick that has become one of Brooklyn most beloved neighborhood restaurants. The pasta is made with love, the portions are honest, and the whole experience feels like eating at your coolest friend Italian grandmother house. No pretension, just great food in a fun room.

name: Al Badawi
description: A groundbreaking Palestinian restaurant in Brooklyn Heights from chef Ayat Masoud that puts Palestinian cuisine on the fine-dining map with extraordinary grace. The musakhan is transcendent, the hummus is the platonic ideal, and every dish tells a story rooted in tradition but elevated with real technique. Essential eating.

name: Noz Market
description: The retail and casual dining arm of the Sushi Noz universe on the Upper East Side, offering pristine Japanese ingredients and prepared foods to take home. The chirashi bowls are gorgeous and the curated selection of Japanese pantry items is unmatched in the city. Your neighborhood Japanese gourmet shop -- if your neighborhood has impeccable taste.

name: One If By Land, Two If By Sea
description: The most romantic restaurant in New York, and it has been for decades. A converted 1767 carriage house in the West Village with a fireplace, candlelight, and live piano that make every dinner feel like a proposal. The tasting menu is refined American, the beef Wellington is the showpiece, and the chocolate souffle requires a 20-minute wait that builds anticipation perfectly. Resy reservations essential.

name: Minetta Tavern
description: Keith McNally's Greenwich Village bistro is home to the Black Label Burger — a dry-aged blend that is, by most accounts, the best burger in New York City. The bone marrow appetizer, the steak frites, and the dark mahogany interior all scream old-money downtown. Getting a table is still an exercise in persistence. The bar seats are first-come, first-served and worth the gamble. Brunch is excellent.

name: Il Buco
description: This NoHo Italian in a candlelit former antiques shop has been a date-night institution for over 25 years. The seasonal market-driven menu changes constantly, the whole branzino is outstanding, and the award-winning wine cellar in the basement holds rare bottles worth exploring. The rustic farmhouse atmosphere feels transported from Umbria. Reservations on Resy. The kind of restaurant that makes you fall in love.

name: Manhatta
description: Danny Meyer's 60th-floor restaurant at 28 Liberty Street has the most dramatic views in New York dining. Looking out at the Manhattan skyline from the Financial District while eating refined seasonal American food is a cinematic experience. The tasting menu is strong, the cocktails are excellent, and sunset through those windows will stop your conversation mid-sentence. Resy reservations.
address: 28 Liberty St 60th floor, New York, NY 10005

name: La Mercerie
description: Marie-Aude Rose all-day French cafe inside the Roman and Williams Guild showroom in SoHo is as beautiful as you would expect, but the food more than holds its own against the design-magazine setting. The croque madame might be the best in the city, and the pastry program is genuinely world-class. A scene with substance.

name: Dame
description: A tiny West Village seafood spot from chef Ed Szymanski that punches miles above its weight class with an obsessive focus on British and European seafood traditions. The fish and chips elevated to fine-dining quality is a flex, and the Dover sole is immaculate. Getting a reservation is a blood sport, but the payoff is extraordinary.

name: Bad Roman
description: Major Food Group's Columbus Circle Italian is pure theater — tableside Caesar salads, a rigatoni that arrives in a cloud of steam, and a dining room designed to make everyone look important. The food is crowd-pleasing rather than revolutionary, but when you're splitting a wagyu carpaccio with friends at 10 PM and the music is loud, that's exactly what you want. The scene is the point. Resy reservations.

name: The Polo Bar
description: Ralph Lauren's private clubhouse on East 55th Street is the most aspirational restaurant in Midtown. The Polo Burger is one of the best in the city, the Cobb salad is impeccable, and the equestrian-themed room makes you feel like you're dining inside a Ralph Lauren ad — which is, of course, entirely the point. Getting a table requires patience and a Resy alarm. The bar is slightly more accessible.
bookingInfo: Phone-only reservations released monthly — no online link.

name: Le Rock
description: A sleek French brasserie in Rockefeller Center that proves Midtown can have genuinely cool restaurants. The raw bar is stellar, the steak tartare is textbook, and the room makes you feel like a stylish extra in a Wes Anderson film. The rare Midtown spot that locals actually seek out rather than stumble into.

name: Pastis
description: Keith McNally brought Pastis back from the dead in 2019, and the Meatpacking classic picked up exactly where it left off. The moules frites, the steak tartare, and the brunch French toast are all perfectly executed French bistro fare. The sidewalk tables are the best people-watching in the Meatpacking District. The fact that it feels unchanged after a decade away is either comforting or uncanny. Both.

name: Locanda Verde
description: Andrew Carmellini's Tribeca Italian inside the Greenwich Hotel is the kind of restaurant that makes you want to move downtown. The sheep's milk ricotta with honey is the iconic opener, the lamb Bolognese is rich and satisfying, and the brunch is one of the best in lower Manhattan. The bar is walk-in friendly and lively. Robert De Niro's hotel gets a lot right, and this restaurant is Exhibit A.

name: Nobu Downtown
description: Nobu Matsuhisa's Financial District outpost serves the Japanese-Peruvian fusion that launched a global empire. The black cod miso is the dish that made Nobu famous — buttery, sweet, and caramelized to perfection. The yellowtail jalapeno and the rock shrimp tempura are equally essential. The space is massive and buzzy. It's not cheap, it's not quiet, and it's still one of the best splurges downtown.

name: The Grill
description: Major Food Group's Midtown power restaurant in the iconic Seagram Building is American grandeur at its most theatrical. The tableside preparations — Dover sole, steak Diane, baked Alaska — are dinner as performance. The room is stunning, the cocktails are precise, and the clientele looks like a casting call for a prestige drama. Jackets suggested. Resy reservations. This is where grown-ups dine.

name: Laser Wolf
description: Michael Solomonov brought his Philly Israeli grill to a Williamsburg rooftop, and the result is one of the most fun restaurants in Brooklyn. The meal starts with a mezze spread that would be a complete dinner anywhere else, then moves to wood-fired meats and vegetables. The lamb skewers are outstanding. The rooftop setting with Manhattan views is electric on summer evenings. Resy.

name: Charlie Bird
description: Charlie Bird in SoHo set the template for the modern NYC wine bar — great pastas, a killer natural wine list, and a soundtrack curated like a DJ set. The farro salad with radishes and mint became iconic. The cacio e pepe is outstanding. The room is perpetually buzzy. It opened the door for a generation of food-and-wine spots that followed. The roast chicken for two is an under-ordered masterpiece.

name: King
description: Clare de Boer and Jess Shadbolt's SoHo townhouse restaurant is quietly one of the best in the city. The cooking is French-Italian with a restraint that lets perfect ingredients speak. The chicken for two and the trout amandine are both extraordinary. The seasonal vegetables are treated with more respect than most restaurants give their proteins. No flash, just substance. Resy.

name: Pasquale Jones
description: The copper oven at Pasquale Jones produces some of the most blistered, beautiful pies in Manhattan — the clam pizza is worth canceling plans for. Nolita doesn't need another Italian spot, but this one justifies its existence with handmade pastas that rival places twice the price. Come hungry. The cacio e pepe pizza shouldn't work, but it absolutely does. Walk-ins at the bar.

name: Buvette
description: Jody Williams's tiny West Village gastrothèque is the most charming restaurant in New York. French small plates, natural wines, and a candlelit space so intimate that strangers become friends by dessert. The croque monsieur is perfect, the steamed mussels are briny and delicious, and the chocolate mousse is a commitment worth making. No reservations — walk in and wait. Cash only.

name: Morandi
description: Keith McNally's West Village Italian has a Tuscan farmhouse warmth that makes every visit feel like a family dinner in Florence. The brick chicken, the cacio e pepe, and the lemon ricotta pancakes at brunch are all outstanding. The patio is one of the best in the Village. The wine list is Italian and affordable. Sunday morning brunch here is one of the most pleasant rituals in New York.

name: River Cafe
description: The most iconic waterfront restaurant in New York. Dining at River Cafe under the Brooklyn Bridge with the Manhattan skyline blazing across the water is a cinematic experience that no photograph can capture. The tasting menu is refined American, the wine list is encyclopedic, and the chocolate marquise is legendary. Jacket required. Book for sunset. This is where you celebrate the big moments.

name: Celestine
description: A stunning Mediterranean restaurant perched on the DUMBO waterfront with jaw-dropping views of the Manhattan Bridge and skyline. The food is actually great, which is borderline miraculous for a restaurant with views this good. Wood-fired dishes, excellent pastas, and a wine list deep on natural and Mediterranean selections.

name: Mắm
description: Jerald Head and Nhung Dao's Lower East Side Vietnamese restaurant started as a pop-up and became one of the most exciting restaurants downtown. The menu changes frequently — one night it's pho, another it's bun dau — and that unpredictability is the magic. The sister wine bar Lai Rai is next door. Plastic stools, uncomfortable seating, and food that makes none of that matter. Walk-in only.

name: Quality Meats
description: Quality Branded's Midtown steakhouse does everything you want a modern steakhouse to do — great cuts, creative appetizers, and over-the-top desserts. The tuna tartare stacked like a tower is the showpiece starter. The prime rib is outstanding. The ice cream sundae for dessert is absurd in the best way. Buzzy, fun, and designed for groups who want to eat well without the stuffy white-tablecloth energy.

name: Buddakan
description: Stephen Starr turned a Chelsea warehouse into a 16,000-square-foot Asian temple with a 16-foot Buddha, communal tables, and a menu that spans dumplings to crispy calamari salad to lobster fried rice. It's theatrical, it's loud, and it's been packed since 2006. The edamame dumplings are the gateway, the short rib is the commitment, and the space itself is the star. Great for large groups.

name: Catch
description: The Meatpacking seafood restaurant with a rooftop, sushi bar, and a scene that operates more like a nightclub than a restaurant after 10 PM. The truffle sashimi is the Instagram hit, the Cantonese lobster is genuinely good, and the crowd is beautiful and knows it. Not the place for a quiet dinner. Absolutely the place for a birthday, a celebration, or a Tuesday night that becomes a story. Resy.

name: Tao Downtown
description: Tao's downtown Chelsea location is an 11,000-square-foot Asian mega-restaurant centered around a 16-foot Buddha and a menu that hits every Asian cuisine from sushi to Peking duck. The food is better than it has any right to be for a restaurant this size and this scene-driven. The miso black cod competes with Nobu. Late night the energy shifts to full nightclub. An experience more than a restaurant.

name: Lavo Italian Restaurant
description: Lavo's Midtown Italian is where upscale dining meets nightlife. The one-pound meatball is the signature — an Instagram phenomenon before Instagram existed. The skirt steak is excellent, the shareable desserts are enormous, and after 10 PM on weekends, the dining room becomes a party. Not subtle, not quiet, and not apologizing for any of it. The brunch party on Saturdays is legendary.

name: Cecconi's Dumbo
description: The DUMBO outpost of the global Italian restaurant chain, set inside the gorgeous 1 Hotel Brooklyn Bridge with unbeatable waterfront views. The pastas are reliable, the cicchetti are fun for sharing, and eating on that terrace on a summer evening is one of the great pleasures of living in this city. Classic Italian in a setting hard to beat.

name: Santina
description: Major Food Group's coastal Italian under the High Line is a glass-enclosed greenhouse serving Italian Riviera-inspired dishes with a garden party energy. The crudite tower is a showpiece, the lobster fra diavolo is rich and spicy, and the seasonal crudo changes daily. The brunch is lively. The connection to the High Line above makes it a perfect pit stop on a West Side walk.

name: Kafana
description: A candlelit Serbian cafe in the East Village that feels like a secret from Belgrade. The cevapi (grilled sausages), the burek (flaky pastry), and the grilled lamb are all hearty, soulful, and cheap. The rakia selection is the most extensive in the city. The atmosphere is dark, romantic, and slightly bohemian. Open late, perfect for a date that starts weird and ends wonderfully. Resy.

name: Raf's
description: The buzziest Italian-American in SoHo from the Nolita Hospitality crew. Rigatoni alla vodka, chicken parm, tiramisu — all the classics done with polish and energy. The sidewalk tables on Crosby Street are prime people-watching. The vibe is perpetually festive. Getting a table requires Resy speed and luck. When you're in, the experience delivers. It's the restaurant that makes SoHo feel alive.

name: El Quijote
description: The Torrisi team resurrected El Quijote inside the Chelsea Hotel, and the result drips with vintage glamour. Lobster, paella, garlic shrimp — Spanish classics served in a room that feels like a 1970s fever dream in the best way. The cocktails are strong, the energy is electric, and the late-night crowd makes this one of the most interesting scenes in Chelsea. Open late. Resy.

name: Sky Pavilion
description: A sprawling, lavish Chinese restaurant in Midtown West that goes big on Cantonese and Sichuan classics with luxe presentation. The dim sum is a highlight, and the Peking duck is carved tableside with proper ceremony. The kind of grand Chinese dining room that feels like an event -- perfect for large groups and celebrations.

name: Falansai
description: Chef Eric Tran's Vietnamese in Greenpoint celebrates French and Chaozhou influences with dishes like duck Vietnamese tamales in banana leaf and savory pork skewers. Operating inside the Pan Pan Vino Vino bakery since 2025. The Dac Biet tasting menu is creative and personal. This is immigrant cooking elevated with technique but never stripped of soul. Book on Resy.

name: Kelang
description: Malaysian food from the Hainan Chicken House team, and the parathas alone are worth the trip to Greenpoint. Impossibly fluffy, layered, and perfect for tearing and dipping. The oxtail rendang mixes Haitian and Malaysian rice — a reflection of owner Chris Low's multicultural childhood. Weekend brunch available. The abacus seeds are a must. Walk-in.

name: Border Town
description: Tortilla master Jorge Aguilar and Amanda Rosa brought their cult pop-up Border Town to a permanent Greenpoint home. Northern Mexican flour tortillas handmade to order, tacos de guisados, and cocktails in a bright, casual space. The tortillas are the star — warm, pillowy, and better than 99% of what you'll find in NYC. Dinner only for now. Walk-in.

name: Confidant
description: A refined New American restaurant in Brooklyn Heights that brings real culinary ambition to a neighborhood that desperately needed it. The seasonal menu showcases smart, produce-driven cooking with enough technique to impress without feeling fussy. A go-to for Brooklyn Heights locals who want a proper dinner out without crossing the bridge.

name: Gertie
description: The beloved Jewish diner Gertie reopened in Prospect Heights after closing its Williamsburg location. Counter-service format with a latke bar, hand-sliced bagels, and classic deli sandwiches. The matzo ball soup is deeply comforting. The space is cozy and nostalgic. Come for breakfast, stay for the egg cream. Everything feels handmade because it is.

name: Il Leone
description: Cult Maine pizzamaker Ben Wexler-Waite brought his naturally leavened Neapolitan pies to Park Slope. The sourcing is meticulous — Maine and Italian ingredients, wood-fired with genuine care. The margherita is textbook, and the Isola lobster pizza nods to southern Italian seafood pastas. The wine list has surprising picks from Campania. Walk-in only.

name: Vato
description: A fun, lively Mexican spot in Park Slope that brings Korean-Mexican fusion with creative tacos and killer margaritas. The vibe is casual and energetic, perfect for groups, and the menu has enough range to satisfy everyone from the adventurous eater to the kid who just wants a quesadilla. A neighborhood staple for good reason.

name: Babbo
description: Stephen Starr bought Babbo from the Batali era and revived it with Mark Ladner (formerly of Del Posto) running the kitchen. The love letters pasta survived the transition, the 100-layer lasagna from Del Posto is now here, and the Greenwich Village townhouse has a renewed energy. This is a restaurant with history finding its second act. Book on Resy.

name: Ho Foods
description: East Village Taiwanese that does three things at an elite level: beef noodle soup, chile wontons, and radish cakes. The broth is deeply beefy, the wontons are coated in fiery chili oil, and the radish cakes are crispy perfection. Breakfast hours bring soy milk, youtiao, and scallion pancakes. The beef noodle set for two ($69) is the power order. Walk-in.

name: Golden Diner
description: The TikTok-famous Chinatown diner where the egg and cheese sando on a sesame-scallion milk bun became a viral sensation. But the honey butter pancakes and the Korean wings are equally good. Breakfast is served until 10 PM, which is genius. Cheap cocktails by NYC standards. The aesthetic is retro-diner with an Asian twist. Resy reservations. Expect a line on weekends.
bookingInfo: Opens 30 days ahead at midnight ET on Resy.

name: Hamburger America
description: Burger scholar George Motz opened his love letter to the American hamburger in SoHo. Three burger styles — smash, fried onion, and a rotating regional special — all executed with academic precision. The Key lime pie is excellent. The hot ham sandwich is the sleeper hit. Two lines: fast and slow (counter seating). The monthly regional special is always worth trying.
address: 155 W Houston St, New York, NY 10012

name: Nepali Bhanchha Ghar
description: Jackson Heights Nepali momo champion — five-time winner of the Momo Crawl, and not by accident. The steamed and fried dumplings in a glistening tomato sauce are extraordinary. Potato, paneer, goat, shrimp, chicken — every filling has its devotees. The thali options are an excellent way to explore the full menu. Cash preferred. Roosevelt Avenue's best-kept secret.

name: Hyderabadi Zaiqa
description: An Eater Award-winning biryani specialist in Murray Hill with nearly 30 regional Indian biryanis — a map on the wall shows where each one originates. The bone-in goat dum biryani is the essential order. The gongura chicken biryani with sourleaf and the avakaya mango pickle biryani are both revelations. The tomato-pepper shorba is liquid gold. Affordable and deeply authentic.

name: Zaab Zaab
description: The original Zaab Zaab in Elmhurst is the most fun Thai restaurant in Queens. Isan-focused cooking from Bryan Chunton — duck larb, roasted whole tilapia, and gai yang chicken served with papaya salad. The mayor is a fan. Multiple locations now, but the Elmhurst original has the most character. The Zaab Burger stand on the LES serves Thai smash burgers. Resy.

name: Pho Bang
description: No-frills Chinatown pho in its purest form. Huge bowls of fragrant beef broth with rice noodles, rare beef, and all the fixings for under $10. The spring rolls are crispy and honest. The bun bo hue is fiery. This is the pho shop that doesn't need to reinvent the wheel because the wheel was already perfect. Cash only, no ambiance, and exactly what you need.

name: Llama San
description: Creative Nikkei cooking in the West Village that fuses Peruvian and Japanese flavors in ways that surprise and delight. The ceviche, the tiradito, and the lomo saltado are all vibrant. The cocktail program leans pisco-heavy and goes deep. The space is lively and colorful. One of the most interesting fusion restaurants in the city — neither cuisine compromises for the other.

name: Thai Villa
description: Reliable East Village Thai with generous portions, fiery curries, and pad thai that hits every note. Not trying to reinvent the genre — just executing classics with care and consistency. The green curry is properly spicy, the mango sticky rice is a perfect finish, and the prices are shockingly fair for Manhattan. The lunch special is one of the best deals downtown.

name: John's of Bleecker Street
description: Coal-fired whole pies on Bleecker Street since 1929 in a vintage brick-walled setting with stained glass and worn wooden booths. No slices — whole pies only. The char on the crust is the signature, and the simplicity of the toppings lets the coal-fired flavor dominate. BYOB-friendly. Cash only. One of the oldest pizzerias in New York and still among the best.

name: Hanco's
description: Beloved Brooklyn banh mi that's been feeding the borough crispy baguette sandwiches since before banh mi was trendy. The bread is crusty, the fillings are generous, and the Vietnamese coffee is strong enough to restart your heart. Multiple locations. The prices have inched up but still represent one of the best sandwich values in Brooklyn. Quick, delicious, no-nonsense.

name: Lil' Frankie's
description: Cozy East Village Italian with wood-fired pizzas, handmade pastas, and a back garden that's one of the most romantic outdoor dining spots in the neighborhood. Open until 2 AM, which makes it essential late-night fuel. The rigatoni alla vodka, the margherita pizza, and the tiramisu are all honest and satisfying. Walk-in only, cash only, and charming as hell.

name: Spicy Moon
description: Vegan Sichuan that actually delivers on the spice. The mapo tofu, the dan dan noodles, and the chili wontons are all fiercely flavored and entirely plant-based. If you didn't know it was vegan, you wouldn't guess. The West Village location is cute, the menu is tight, and the food proves that Sichuan peppercorns don't need meat to create magic. Multiple locations now.

name: Casa Enrique
description: The only Michelin-starred Mexican restaurant in NYC, and it absolutely earns that star with deeply soulful, traditional Mexican cooking in Long Island City. Chef Cosme Aguilar mole is a masterwork -- complex, layered, and worth the trip alone. The space is modest, the prices are fair, and every dish tastes like it was made by someone who genuinely loves this cuisine.

name: Pho Hoai
description: Williamsburg pho shop with rich, deeply flavorful beef broth and portions generous enough to get you through a Brooklyn winter. No-frills setting, honest cooking, and a bowl of pho dac biet that warms you from the inside out. The bun cha is also excellent. Nothing fancy, nothing Instagram-worthy, and nothing you'd trade for any pho in Manhattan. Cash only.

name: Pepe Rosso To Go
description: SoHo Italian counter spot that's been feeding the neighborhood generous portions of handmade pasta, paninis, and tiramisu at honest prices since the 1990s. The pasta of the day changes and is always solid. The panini with fresh mozzarella is a perfect SoHo lunch. No ambiance to speak of — just good Italian food at prices that feel like a time warp.

name: SriPraPhai
description: Widely considered the best Thai restaurant in New York City, and it's in Woodside, Queens, where it should be. The green papaya salad, the pad see ew, and the crispy watercress salad are all extraordinary. The garden patio in summer is gorgeous and huge. The menu is encyclopedic. Take the 7 train and eat like you're in Bangkok. Worth the trip from any borough.

name: Osakana
description: Approachable West Village sushi with quality fish at prices that don't require a second mortgage. The chirashi bowl is the value play, the salmon sashimi is pristine, and the miso soup is a comforting start. Cozy space, friendly service, and the kind of neighborhood Japanese spot that makes the Village feel like a real neighborhood. Resy.

name: Flushing Food Court at New World Mall
description: The sprawling basement food court in Flushing's New World Mall is the most authentic Chinese food experience in NYC outside of an actual trip to China. Hand-pulled noodles, lamb skewers, xiao long bao, Sichuan snacks — dozens of stalls serving regional specialties at prices that embarrass Manhattan. Come hungry, bring cash, and prepare to eat your way through every province.

name: La Gran Uruguaya
description: A Jackson Heights South American institution famous for its empanadas, milanesas, and Uruguayan pastries. The dulce de leche pastries are addictive. Open insanely early and late, serving the neighborhood's Latin American community with warmth and consistency. Cash-friendly, counter-service, and the kind of place where every regular has a standing order.

name: Arthur Avenue Retail Market
description: The beating heart of the Bronx Little Italy -- an indoor market stuffed with family-run vendors selling everything from hand-pulled mozzarella to imported olive oil to fresh pasta. This is not a tourist trap; it is where real Italians from the neighborhood still shop. Arthur Avenue makes Manhattan Little Italy look like a theme park.

name: Roberto's
description: A Bronx institution on Arthur Avenue serving some of the most genuinely soulful Italian food in all of New York City. Chef Roberto Paciullo runs the room with charismatic energy that makes every table feel like a celebration. The pastas are handmade, the specials are always worth ordering, and the experience is a reminder of what Italian dining should feel like.

name: Sahadi's
description: Century-old Brooklyn Heights Middle Eastern grocery and deli that's been the definitive source for hummus, tabbouleh, and prepared foods since 1895. The falafel is crispy, the baklava is flaky, and the prepared foods counter could stock an entire dinner party. Saturday morning is peak energy. One of the most important food institutions in Brooklyn. If you only visit one deli in the Heights, it's this one.

name: Emily
description: The Clinton Hill pizzeria famous for the Emmy Burger and creative wood-fired pies with quality cocktails. The Colony pizza — pepperoni, pickled chili, honey — is the cult favorite. The vodka grandma slice is a close second. The cocktail program is surprisingly good for a pizza spot. Multiple locations, but the Clinton Hill original has the magic. Resy.

name: Frankel's Delicatessen
description: Modern Greenpoint deli serving classic Jewish fare — hand-sliced smoked fish, bagels, egg creams — in a bright corner shop. The smoked fish platter is beautiful and generous. The pastrami sandwich is excellent. The egg cream is made old-school. A worthy addition to the Brooklyn Jewish deli renaissance. Cash only at the counter.

name: The Commodore
description: Williamsburg dive bar meets fried chicken joint, and both halves are excellent. The Nashville hot chicken sandwich is fiery and addictive, the frozen margarita is dangerously easy, and the late-night energy is pure Brooklyn. The vibe is unpretentious, the crowd is local, and the $5 margarita is one of the best deals in Williamsburg. Open late. No reservations. Just show up.

name: Samosa Shack
description: A beloved Jackson Heights street food stall turned brick-and-mortar serving some of the crispiest, most flavorful samosas in the city. The fillings rotate and get creative -- think beyond standard potato to pulled pork and other wild combos -- but the classic veggie samosa is still king. Cheap, fast, and exactly the kind of place that makes Jackson Heights the greatest food neighborhood.

name: Oxalis
description: Prospect Heights tasting menu that punches way above its neighborhood weight class. Seasonal, creative, and genuinely thoughtful cooking in an intimate dining room. The tasting menu changes constantly and always surprises. The wine list is natural-leaning and well-curated. One of the best value tasting menus in Brooklyn. Resy. Come for the food, stay for the warmth.

name: Lolo's Seafood Shack
description: Colorful Harlem Caribbean seafood with grilled lobster, jerk chicken, and rum cocktails in a beach shack vibe that transports you to the islands. The coconut shrimp is excellent, the plantains are perfectly fried, and the rum punch flows freely. The outdoor space in summer is festive and loud. Come hungry, leave happy, and don't skip the key lime pie.

name: Madcap Cafe
description: Laid-back Harlem all-day cafe with excellent brunch, avocado toast, and seasonal bowls in a plant-filled space. The pancakes are fluffy, the grain bowls are genuinely healthy, and the coffee is above average. Dog-friendly vibes. The kind of neighborhood cafe that makes you want to move to Harlem for the mornings alone.
address: 2244 Frederick Douglass Blvd, New York, NY 10027

name: La Cabañita
description: Sunset Park taqueria on 5th Avenue's Mexican restaurant row serving authentic tacos, cemitas, and fresh aguas frescas at prices that make the trip to Brooklyn's Latin quarter worth it. The tacos al pastor are excellent, the cemita poblana is massive and satisfying, and the horchata is freshly made. Cash friendly. No frills. Real Mexican food for real prices.

name: Maison Kayser
description: French baker Eric Kayser's NYC outposts serve crusty baguettes, flaky croissants, and refined lunch options that transport you to a Paris sidewalk. The eclair is exceptional, the croque monsieur is textbook, and the bread basket at any table is better than most bakeries' entire output. Multiple locations. The UES flagship is the most charming.

name: Nom Wah Nolita
description: The hip Nolita sibling of the legendary Chinatown dim sum parlor, serving a curated menu of dim sum classics and creative cocktails in a sleek, modern space. The original egg rolls and dumplings translate beautifully, and the late-night scene is surprisingly fun. Dim sum for the downtown crowd -- same quality, cooler vibe.

name: La Morada
description: Oaxacan family restaurant in the Bronx with complex moles that take days to prepare, tlayudas the size of tabletops, and tamales that would make a grandmother proud. The family's immigration story is woven into every dish, and the community mission makes dining here feel meaningful. The mole negro is the most ambitious dish on the menu. Cash friendly. Worth the trip to Mott Haven.

name: Achilles Heel
description: Greenpoint waterfront bar and restaurant with craft cocktails, oysters, and New American small plates in a space that feels like a well-designed living room. The outdoor area facing the East River is magical on summer evenings. The burger is excellent, the oysters are fresh, and the cocktails are serious without being pretentious. Walk-in. Perfect for a casual date.

name: Spice Symphony
description: Popular Midtown East Indian with a comprehensive menu spanning North and South Indian dishes. The chicken tikka masala is reliable, the dosas are crispy, and the lamb biryani is fragrant. Not trying to reinvent Indian food — just executing the classics at a fair price in a neighborhood that needs more options like this. The lunch buffet is an excellent deal.

name: Ops
description: Bushwick natural wine bar and pizzeria where the pies are naturally leavened and the orange wine selection is one of the best in Brooklyn. The seasonal toppings change with the market and are always creative. The burrata appetizer is excellent. The vibe is Bushwick at its most charming — industrial space, good music, interesting people. Resy. Date-night energy without the date-night prices.

name: Tanoreen
description: Rawia Bishara's Bay Ridge Palestinian restaurant is one of the most celebrated Middle Eastern restaurants in the five boroughs. The mussakhan is extraordinary — roasted chicken with sumac and onion on taboon bread. The cauliflower is somehow life-changing. The lamb shank falls off the bone. Bay Ridge is a trek from Manhattan, but Tanoreen makes it absolutely worth the journey.

name: Enoteca Maria
description: The Staten Island restaurant where real nonnas from around the world rotate as guest chefs, cooking authentic home recipes from their own kitchens. Every visit is different because every nonna is different. Italian, Algerian, Syrian, Colombian — the menu changes with the chef. The concept is genius and the food is deeply personal. Worth the ferry ride.

name: Christos Steakhouse
description: Long-standing Astoria Greek steakhouse serving prime cuts, lamp chops, and Greek specialties in a classic neighborhood setting. The porterhouse is beautifully charred, the Greek salad is fresh and honest, and the lamb chops are the kind that make you pound the table. Old-school service, white tablecloths, and the energy of a neighborhood that takes its meat seriously. OpenTable.

name: Don Antonio
description: Midtown Neapolitan pizzeria from Roberto Caporuscio featuring the signature fried-then-baked montanara pizza — a technique so clever it makes you wonder why everyone doesn't do it. The crust puffs up light and crispy, the toppings melt into the dough, and the result is unlike any other pizza in the city. The Margherita DOC is also excellent. Walk-in. A pre-theater essential.

name: La Piraña Lechonera
description: A Bronx food trailer with lines down quiet Mott Haven streets every weekend for classic Puerto Rican lechon asado — slow-roasted pork with crackling skin that shatters. Saturday from noon, Sunday from 12:30. Go before noon to skip the line. The rice and beans and mofongo are stellar sides. Eater 38 pick. One of the most important food experiences in the Bronx. Cash only.

name: Charles Pan-Fried Chicken
description: Charles Gabriel has been pan-frying chicken in cast-iron skillets on 125th Street for years, and the result is a perfectly browned crust and impossibly moist interior that has no business being this good. The combo with pulled pork or ribs is the power order. The black-eyed peas, grits, and mac & cheese are all outstanding. Eater 38. Harlem soul food at its purest. Takeout available online.

name: Carnitas Ramirez
description: The pop-up that became permanent on East 3rd Street, and the carnitas are life-altering. A glass case labeled with every part of the pig — shank, ribs, belly, cheeks, tongue, skin — and you point at what you want for $6 a pop. Dress everything at the salsa bar. Robert Sietsema called it game-changing, and he wasn't exaggerating. Eater 38 pick. Cash only. Wednesday through Sunday.

name: A&A Bake Doubles and Roti
description: James Beard America's Classics Award winner in Bed-Stuy, and the doubles — deep-fried flatbreads stuffed with curried chickpeas — are under $3 and worth traveling boroughs for. The roti is also exceptional. A&A has been here since 2002, a Trinidadian mainstay for locals who know. The tamarind and pepper sauces are the finishing touch. Cash only. One of the most important cheap eats in NYC.

name: Diljān
description: Afghan bakery in Brooklyn Heights from the team behind Little Flower Cafe and baker Bryan Ford. Huge rippable Afghan naan with accompanying dips, halwa sticky buns, and sheer pira pastries that are unlike anything else in the neighborhood. Get there early — the naan sells out. No real dine-in space, but perch on the ledge inside. Halal. A gorgeous addition to the Heights.

name: Gigi Curry & Noodle Bar
description: Fast-casual Thai noodle shop in Bushwick where you build your own bowl — curried noodles, peanut massaman, dry crab egg noodles — with varying spice levels and protein options. Counter service, takeout, delivery. The kind of quick, affordable, customizable lunch that Bushwick was missing. Simple concept, well-executed, and the prices are right.

name: Barker Cafeteria
description: Crown Heights daytime sandwich spot from Gracie Gardner and Henry Wright that excels at honest, well-made food. The roast beef with horseradish on focaccia is excellent. The chicken noodle soup is creamy and comforting. The apple fritters are a revelation. The Dad Plate — smoked ham, vegetables, bread, and spicy Hungarian cheese — is weirdly perfect. Open mornings and afternoons only.

name: Third Time's the Charm
description: The first physical restaurant from the Bad Cholesterol pizza pop-up, and the sourdough pies live up to the cult hype. Wood-fired in a Red Hook space with supper club vibes — chicken nuggets with gochujang dip, a blooming onion homage, and cocktails that don't take themselves seriously. Backyard for warm weather. Resy reservations, but walk-ins welcome. The most fun pizza spot in Red Hook.

name: Dollar Slice NYC
description: The quintessential New York survival food. A hot cheese slice for a dollar or two from a window on a Midtown street — it's not the best pizza, it's not trying to be, and it's the most democratic meal in the city. Available at 3 AM when you need it most. Multiple joints, all identical, all reliable. The cheese pull is respectable. The paper plate is part of the experience.

name: Papaya King
description: Iconic UES hot dog stand since 1932 serving snappy all-beef frankfurters with tropical fruit drinks that shouldn't pair well and absolutely do. The papaya drink is sweet, cold, and refreshing against a salty, snappy dog. The sauerkraut is tangy. Cash only, no seats, eat on the sidewalk. A New York institution that predates most of the buildings around it.

name: Taqueria Ramirez
description: This tiny Greenpoint taqueria caused an absolute frenzy when it opened, with lines snaking down the block for good reason. The focus is dead simple -- a tight menu of tacos done with precision and soul, using handmade tortillas that put 90% of NYC Mexican spots to shame. The suadero taco alone is worth the wait.

name: Tasty Hand-Pulled Noodles
description: Watch the noodles being hand-pulled to order through the window on Doyers Street, then eat them in a rich broth that's deeply satisfying. The beef hand-pulled noodle soup is the essential order. The wonton soup is excellent. The scallion pancakes are crispy. Tiny space, no decor, and some of the most honest food in Chinatown. Cash only. Under $10 for a filling meal.

name: Taco Mix
description: East Harlem taqueria on the original Taco Row strip — 116th Street between 2nd and 3rd — serving late-night carne asada, al pastor, and lengua tacos to a crowd that knows real tacos from fake ones. Open until 3 AM. The salsa verde is fiery. The al pastor has proper pineapple char. Cash only, fluorescent lights, and the kind of authenticity that East Harlem is built on.

name: Punjabi Grocery & Deli
description: The 24-hour East Village institution serving samosas, rice and dal, and roti wraps to cab drivers and night owls at prices that haven't caught up with inflation. Open around the clock. Vegetarian and halal. The samosa is crispy and properly spiced. The roti wrap is filling and under $5. At 4 AM after a night out, this place is a temple. Cash only. Life-sustaining.

name: Beto's Carnitas & Guisados
description: Beto's Carnitas turned from an NYC pop-up into a permanent LES fast-casual spot. The carnitas and guisados — mushrooms, chicken tinga, pork — are paired with flour tortillas or tortas. Counter service, walk-in only, and the kind of unpretentious Mexican food that Clinton Street was missing. The tortillas are made in-house. Simple, authentic, and exactly what the LES needed.

name: Ramen Danbo
description: Fukuoka-style tonkotsu ramen in Williamsburg where you customize everything — noodle firmness, broth richness, toppings. The pork broth is milky, rich, and properly porky. The ajitama egg is perfectly soft-boiled. The gyoza are crispy. No frills, no fusion, just ramen done the Fukuoka way. Multiple locations now. The Williamsburg original is the coziest.

name: King of Falafel & Shawarma
description: Vendy Award-winning falafel in Astoria that graduated from cart to restaurant. The falafel is crispy and herb-packed, the shawarma is juicy and perfectly spiced, and the hummus platter is generous and creamy. Under $10 for a feast. The Astoria location has seating. One of the best cheap meals in Queens. The garlic sauce is dangerously good. Halal.

name: Absolute Bagels
description: Upper West Side bagel institution hand-rolling and kettle-boiling since 1972. The everything bagel is the default order, and the cream cheese is applied with the generosity that only a real New York bagel shop understands. The line is constant, it moves fast, and every bagel comes out warm. No fancy flavors, no avocado toast, just perfect bagels. Cash only.

name: Ess-a-Bagel
description: Massive, fluffy bagels baked in Midtown since 1976 with an array of cream cheese flavors that ranges from classic to creative. The sesame bagel with scallion cream cheese is the standard order. The nova lox sandwich is piled high. Multiple locations now, but the 3rd Avenue flagship has the most character. The bagel is bigger than your head and twice as satisfying.

name: Cheeky Sandwiches
description: Tiny LES shop serving New Orleans-inspired po' boys, biscuit sandwiches, and sweet tea. The shrimp po'boy is excellent — crispy, creamy, and properly dressed. The biscuit sandwich for breakfast is Southern comfort in NYC form. The sweet tea is dangerously good. Small, quirky, and the kind of sandwich shop that makes the Lower East Side feel like a neighborhood.

name: Dosa Man
description: Legendary Washington Square Park cart with crispy, paper-thin dosas filled with masala potato, served with sambar and chutneys. A Vendy Award winner operating from a cart that most people walk past without realizing they're missing one of the best street food experiences in Manhattan. The plain dosa is extraordinary. The masala dosa is essential. Under $10. Cash only.
address: 50 Washington Square S, New York, NY 10012

name: Amy's Bread
description: Beloved Hell's Kitchen bakery baking crusty artisan breads, flaky scones, and rich brownies from scratch since 1992. The black olive twist is the signature bread. The scones are buttery and crumbly. The brownies are fudgy and dense. Everything is made by hand, and you can taste the care. Multiple locations, but the 9th Avenue original is the one that started it all.

name: The Original Chinatown Ice Cream Factory
description: Family-run Chinatown ice cream shop since 1978 with Asian-inspired flavors that were exotic before exotic was trendy. Lychee, taro, black sesame, ginger, and red bean — each one is made in small batches with real ingredients. The black sesame is the cult favorite. The taro is vivid purple and delicious. Cash only. A Chinatown institution that refuses to change and is better for it.

name: Wooly's Ice
description: LES shaved ice shop with Taiwanese-style snow ice in creative flavors — matcha, mango, ube — topped with mochi, boba, and condensed milk. The texture is feathery light, almost like eating a flavored cloud. The ube is visually stunning. The matcha is bittersweet perfection. A small, cheerful space on Doyers Street. The perfect Chinatown dessert stop.

name: Tortillería Mexicana Los Hermanos
description: Bushwick tortilleria grinding fresh masa in-house for the best corn tortillas in the borough. The tacos are built on tortillas so fresh they're still warm, and the tamales are wrapped with care. The difference between machine-made tortillas and these is the difference between wonder bread and sourdough. Cash only. A neighborhood treasure that deserves national recognition.

name: Doughnut Plant
description: Pioneering LES doughnut shop that proved doughnuts could be a craft. The creme brulee doughnut — torched to order — is the signature. The peanut butter and jelly is nostalgic genius. The tres leches is soaking wet in the best way. Seasonal specials rotate and are always creative. Multiple locations. The Grand Street original is the most atmospheric.

name: Mei Lai Wah
description: No-frills Chinatown bakery where the BBQ pork buns come out of the oven fresh, fluffy, and for a couple dollars each. The line forms at dawn and doesn't stop. The egg custard tarts are flaky and custardy. The coconut buns are sweet and pillowy. Cash only, no English needed, and the kind of Chinatown bakery that makes $4 feel like a feast.

name: El Atoradero
description: Washington Heights Mexican gem with complex Pueblan moles that take days to prepare, hearty cemitas, and fresh-pressed juices. The mole poblano is a masterwork — rich, chocolatey, and layered with dozens of ingredients. The tlayuda is enormous. The aguachile is fiery and bright. One of the most authentic regional Mexican restaurants in Manhattan, hiding way uptown.

name: Bunna Cafe
description: All-vegan Ethiopian restaurant in Bushwick with communal injera platters, rich lentil stews, and a coffee ceremony that's a genuine cultural experience. Everything is plant-based, and nothing feels like it's missing. The misir wot is earthy and spiced perfectly. The injera is spongy and sour in the best way. Saturday brunch available. A beautiful, mission-driven restaurant.

name: Tortazo
description: Rick Bayless's fast-casual Mexican in Midtown with tortas, tacos, and bowls using quality ingredients at reasonable prices. The torta is the signature — crusty bread, flavorful fillings, proper Mexican technique. The guacamole is excellent. The prices are fair for Midtown. Not the most authentic Mexican in the city, but the best quick Mexican near Times Square by a wide margin.

name: Please Don't Tell
description: The speakeasy that started the speakeasy craze. Enter through a phone booth inside Crif Dogs, dial a number, and step into a dimly lit bar where the cocktails are world-class and the hot dogs are a phone call away. PDT has been copied a thousand times and never equaled. The bartenders know their craft deeply. No reservations — show up and hope. The Benton's Old Fashioned is legendary.
address: 113 St Marks Pl, New York, NY 10009

name: Employees Only
description: West Village cocktail institution hidden behind a psychic's neon sign since 2004. The bartenders in white coats shake and stir with the precision of surgeons. The kitchen stays open until 3:30 AM — bone marrow poppers and steak frites at 2 AM is a lifestyle. The cocktails are spirit-forward and serious. One of the most influential bars of the modern cocktail era.

name: Brooklyn Brewery
description: The brewery that put Brooklyn craft beer on the map. The Williamsburg tasting room pours flagship Brooklyn Lager alongside rotating seasonal experiments. Tours are available and genuinely fun. Friday and weekend hours draw a lively crowd. The outdoor space is dog-friendly. If you only visit one brewery in Brooklyn, this is the legacy choice.

name: Other Half Brewing
description: Brooklyn's premier hazy IPA brewery with a massive Gowanus taproom, cult-favorite double IPAs that sell out on release day, and an imperial stout program that rivals the best in the country. The space is huge, the tap list rotates constantly, and the beer geek crowd is passionate. If you care about craft beer, Other Half is a pilgrimage site. Dog-friendly.

name: THREES Brewing
description: Gowanus brewery and event space with house-brewed beers, a rotating food pop-up kitchen, and regular live music and DJ nights. The space is industrial-chic and massive. The lager is excellent, the seasonal IPAs are creative, and the events calendar is packed. Dog-friendly, family-friendly during the day, and party-friendly at night. The most versatile brewery in Brooklyn.

name: Bemelmans Bar
description: The most iconic hotel bar in America. Art Deco interiors, Madeline murals on the walls, a live jazz pianist, and cocktails served with old-money elegance at The Carlyle. A classic martini here costs $30 and is worth every cent for the atmosphere alone. Open to non-guests — arrive before 9 PM to skip the line. Bemelmans is not a bar, it's a portal to a more elegant era.

name: Nowadays
description: Massive outdoor day-party space on the Ridgewood/Bushwick border with DJ sets, seasonal events, and a 5,000-square-foot indoor venue added recently. The garden is the draw — string lights, open air, and the kind of weekend energy that makes you forget you have a Monday. The crowd is diverse, the music leans electronic, and the vibes are immaculate. Seasonal.

name: Smalls Jazz Club
description: Intimate basement jazz club in the West Village with nightly live sets that range from emerging talent to legends. The cover is reasonable, the BYOB policy is friendly, and the room is small enough that you can hear the musicians breathe. This is jazz at its most intimate. No talking during sets — the audience takes the music as seriously as the performers. A sacred space.

name: Village Vanguard
description: The most legendary jazz club in the world, operating since 1935 in a triangular basement on 7th Avenue South. The greatest jazz musicians who ever lived have played this room — Coltrane, Monk, Bill Evans, Sonny Rollins. Shows are ticketed, the room is small, and the sound is perfect. If you see one live jazz show in New York, make it a Monday night at the Vanguard.

name: House of Yes
description: Bushwick's wildest nightlife venue — circus performances, immersive dance parties, themed costume nights, and a creative energy that makes every event feel like an art installation you can drink in. Costumes are often required — check Instagram before going. The cocktails are creative. The crowd is fearless. The most memorable night out you'll have in Brooklyn.

name: Upright Citizens Brigade
description: The improv theater that launched Amy Poehler, Tina Fey, and a thousand comedy careers. Nightly shows feature sketch, improv, and stand-up from some of the funniest up-and-coming performers in the city. Ticket prices are reasonable. The energy is electric. BYOB at some shows. If you want to see the next SNL star before they're famous, this is where it happens.

name: Brooklyn Bowl
description: Williamsburg bowling alley, live music venue, and restaurant in one glorious multi-use space. Blue Ribbon fried chicken is served while you bowl, and the concert calendar features mid-tier touring acts with surprisingly great sound. The nachos are better than bowling alley nachos have any right to be. Perfect for groups, birthday parties, and people who want entertainment with their entertainment.

name: Pier 17 Rooftop
description: The Seaport District crown jewel is this massive rooftop concert venue and hangout space perched above the East River with jaw-dropping Brooklyn Bridge views. More of an events and entertainment destination than a restaurant, hosting big-name concerts in summer and transforming into a winter wonderland in colder months. One of the best open-air spaces in lower Manhattan.

name: Bohemian Hall & Beer Garden
description: Astoria's massive Czech beer garden has been pouring pints since 1910, and the picnic tables under the trees are one of the best warm-weather drinking experiences in the city. Pilsner Urquell on draft, kielbasa, pierogi, and a crowd that spans every age and background in Queens. Free admission, huge outdoor space, and the vibe of a European village festival. Dog-friendly.

name: 230 Fifth Rooftop
description: Massive Flatiron rooftop with panoramic Empire State Building views, seasonal igloos in winter, and frozen cocktails in summer. The views are genuinely spectacular — one of the best Empire State vantage points in the city. The cocktails are fine, the crowd is touristy, and the experience is about the view rather than the drinks. But what a view. No cover most nights.

name: Radegast Hall & Biergarten
description: Williamsburg Bavarian beer hall with a retractable roof, communal tables, German beers, and live music on weekends. The bratwurst is snappy, the schnitzel is golden, and the sauerkraut is tangy. The weekend brunch with pretzels and weisswurst is a sleeper hit. When the roof opens on a summer evening, there are few better places to drink a liter of beer in Brooklyn.

name: Rockwood Music Hall
description: Intimate LES live music venue with multiple stages, nightly shows spanning rock, folk, jazz, and singer-songwriter sets. The rooms are tiny enough that every performance feels like a private concert. The back room has hosted artists before they blew up. No cover for many shows. The bar is cheap. One of the most important grassroots music venues in New York.

name: Mercury Lounge
description: LES rock club that's been a launching pad for indie bands since 1993. The Strokes, Interpol, and The National all played early shows here. The 250-capacity room has outstanding sound and sightlines from every angle. The booking is consistently excellent. Cheap drinks, no pretension, and the legacy of 30 years of discovering great music.
address: 217 E Houston St, New York, NY 10002

name: Le Bain
description: Perched atop The Standard Hotel in the Meatpacking District, Le Bain is the kind of rooftop bar where fashion people, Europeans, and runway models converge. There is a plunge pool on the roof, panoramic Hudson views, and DJs spinning until the early hours. A scene, unapologetically -- dress up, show up late, and embrace the chaos.

name: Pete's Candy Store
description: Williamsburg institution in a former candy store with a tiny back room for live music, trivia nights, spelling bees, and bingo. Free shows, cheap drinks, and a neighborhood crowd that's been coming here for 20 years. Pete's doesn't try to be trendy — it just is, by virtue of being genuine. The backyard opens in summer. One of the last truly un-corporate bars in Williamsburg.

name: Breads Bakery
description: Israeli bakery near Union Square whose chocolate babka is widely considered the best in New York City. The layers of chocolate and dough spiral into something transcendent. The rugelach, the challah, and the croissants are all outstanding. The holiday season brings lines out the door. Multiple locations, but the Union Square flagship is the one. Do not leave New York without trying this babka.

name: Patisserie Chanson
description: Elegant Flatiron patisserie with French pastries that look like jewelry and taste like Paris. The macarons are perfectly crispy-chewy, the eclairs are works of art, and the seasonal tarts are extraordinary. The hidden dessert bar behind the bakery is a date-night gem. The attention to detail is obsessive. One of the most refined pastry experiences in Manhattan.

name: Cafe Grumpy
description: Greenpoint-born specialty coffee roaster that became famous from Girls on HBO, but the coffee earned its reputation honestly. Meticulous sourcing, light-medium roasts, and a minimalist aesthetic. The pour-over is exceptional. The flat white is velvety. Multiple locations, but the Meserole Ave original in Greenpoint is the pilgrimage site. No flavored lattes. Just coffee, done right.

name: Sunday in Brooklyn
description: Williamsburg brunch destination that built its cult on malted pancakes — thick, fluffy, and drenched in hazelnut-maple praline butter. The hasselback potato at dinner is also outstanding. The three-level space is designed for lingering. Saturday and Sunday brunch has significant waits. The cocktail program is better than most brunch spots bother with. Resy for dinner, walk-in for brunch.

name: Clinton St. Baking Co.
description: The LES brunch spot that started it all. The buttermilk pancakes with warm maple butter have drawn lines every weekend since 2001 and show no signs of stopping. The BLT is excellent. The eggs Benedict is textbook. Dinner is underrated. Walk-in only, and the weekend wait can hit 90 minutes — but the pancakes really are that good. A genuine NYC institution.

name: Supermoon Bakehouse
description: Instagram-famous LES bakery with outrageously layered croissants, cruffins, and seasonal stuffed pastries in flavors that push the boundaries of what pastry can be. The supreme croissant — filled, glazed, and topped — is an engineering marvel. The cookie is excellent. Seasonal specials sell out early. Arrive before 10 AM on weekends or accept disappointment.

name: Baked
description: Red Hook bakery with a cult following for its sweet and salty brownie — the dessert that put the neighborhood on the map. The banana cake with cream cheese frosting is equally legendary. The scones are buttery. The space is cozy and smells incredible. Everything is baked from scratch with visible love. The kind of bakery that makes Red Hook worth the detour.

name: Cha Cha Matcha
description: The pink-hued matcha cafe that helped launch the matcha latte movement in NYC. Ceremonial-grade matcha, oat milk lattes, and matcha soft serve in a space designed to be photographed. The matcha is genuinely good — bright, earthy, and properly whisked. Multiple locations. The Nolita original is the cutest. Not just Instagram bait — the drinks actually deliver.

name: Black Seed Bagels
description: Montreal-meets-NYC bagels — wood-fired, honey-sweetened, and smaller and chewier than the classic New York bagel. The tobiko cream cheese is the signature schmear. The BEC on a black seed bagel is outstanding. Multiple locations across the city. A different bagel philosophy than the big fluffy NY-style, and both approaches deserve respect.

name: Budin
description: Greenpoint Scandinavian-inspired specialty coffee shop with light-roasted beans, cardamom buns, and a minimalist Nordic aesthetic. The pour-over is clean and bright. The kanelbulle (cinnamon bun) is the pastry to get. The space is calm and beautiful. One of the best third-wave coffee shops in Brooklyn for people who want their coffee experience to feel intentional.

name: Partners Coffee
description: Brooklyn-based specialty coffee roaster with seasonal blends, single origins, and a focus on direct-trade sourcing. The drip coffee is clean and well-balanced. The cold brew is smooth. Multiple Williamsburg locations. The branding is beautiful. The commitment to the supply chain is genuine. A step above the average coffee shop without the pretension of the hardcore specialty spots.

name: Win Son
description: Taiwanese-American brunch and dinner destination in Williamsburg that makes fly's head, lu rou fan, and scallion pancakes that honor tradition while feeling completely modern. The brunch is outstanding — the congee and the breakfast sandwich are both excellent. The cocktails are creative and lean into Taiwanese flavors. Resy. One of the most important Taiwanese restaurants in NYC.

name: Dough
description: Bed-Stuy doughnut shop with oversized, yeast-raised doughnuts in creative glazes — hibiscus, dulce de leche, blood orange, cafe au lait. Each doughnut is the size of a softball and rich enough to split. The hibiscus glaze is the signature — floral, sweet, and a stunning shade of pink. Multiple locations. The Franklin Ave original is the pilgrimage site.

name: Grimaldi's Pizzeria
description: Coal-fired DUMBO pizzeria under the Brooklyn Bridge with thin-crust pies in a former bank building. The margherita is well-charred and the mozzarella is fresh. Cash only, no slices, whole pies only. Touristy? Yes. Still legitimately good? Also yes. The location alone — steps from the Brooklyn Bridge with Manhattan glowing across the water — makes it worth the visit.

name: Joe & Pat's
description: Staten Island thin-crust legend since 1960 with crackling crispy pies and old-school Italian-American dishes that make the ferry ride worth it. The vodka pie is the signature — tangy, creamy, and addictive. The chicken parm is a heavyweight. Joe & Pat's doesn't need Manhattan's approval and never asked for it. SI pride in every slice.

name: Hometown Bar-B-Que
description: Red Hook Texas-style BBQ that rivals anything in Austin. Billy Durney smokes brisket low and slow until the bark cracks and the fat renders into silk. The pulled pork and lamb belly are equally outstanding. The outdoor space is massive and dog-friendly. Lines form early on weekends. Durney went from bouncer to pitmaster to Eater 38 — that's a New York story.

name: Mighty Quinn's BBQ
description: Hugh Mangum brought serious Texas-style BBQ to the East Village and proved that NYC can absolutely hang in the smoker game. The brisket is the star -- beautifully smoked with a legit bark and buttery interior that rivals what you would find in Austin. Started as a Smorgasburg stall and grew into a mini-empire, but the original still has the most soul.

name: Murray's Bagels
description: Greenwich Village hand-rolled bagels baked in a brick oven since the 1990s. The everything bagel with scallion cream cheese is the default — warm, chewy, and generously schmeared. The lox sandwich is piled high. Cash only, tiny shop, and a line that wraps around 6th Ave on weekend mornings. No fancy flavors. Just a perfect New York bagel, every time.

name: H&H Bagels
description: Iconic Upper West Side bagel factory making hand-rolled, kettle-boiled bagels since 1972. Massive, chewy, and everything a NYC bagel should be. The plain bagel with a thick layer of cream cheese is a simple joy that H&H has been perfecting for over 50 years. The sesame is the sleeper pick. Multiple locations now, but the Columbus Ave original is the pilgrimage.

name: Jing Fong
description: Massive Chinatown dim sum hall where rolling carts bring dumplings, steamed buns, and turnip cakes to your table in a chaotic, joyful circus of food. The har gow is translucent and shrimpy, the siu mai is meaty, and the egg custard tarts are warm and flaky. Seat 300+ people. Weekend mornings are insane. Tuesday lunch is the insider move. A true Chinatown spectacle.

name: Shanghai You Garden
description: Flushing soup dumpling specialist with some of the best xiao long bao in New York. Thin skins that barely hold the scalding broth inside, bursting with savory pork filling. The pan-fried dumplings are crispy-bottomed and excellent. The scallion pancakes are a must-add. Lines are a constant — arrive at 11 AM or bring a book. Order at least two baskets. Cash preferred.

name: ABCV
description: Jean-Georges Vongerichten vegetable-forward restaurant in the ABC Carpet and Home building proves that plant-based eating can be genuinely luxurious. The space is gorgeous -- all earthy tones, natural light, and wellness energy without being preachy about it. The cooking is inventive and globally inspired, making even committed carnivores forget they are eating vegetables.

name: Avant Garden
description: East Village vegan fine dining with candlelit romance and creative plant-based dishes that make you forget protein exists. The beet tartare is gorgeous, the mushroom risotto is rich, and the natural wine list goes deep into organic and biodynamic bottles. One of the best date-night vegans in the city. Resy. Proof that vegan food doesn't have to be boring or beige.

name: Ladybird
description: East Village vegan tapas bar with creative plant-based small plates, craft cocktails, and a buzzy downtown energy. The jackfruit tacos are inventive, the mushroom ceviche is bright, and the cocktail program is more ambitious than most non-vegan bars. The space is dark and moody. Perfect for a date where you want to seem cultured and compassionate simultaneously.

name: By Chloe
description: The fast-casual vegan chain that helped normalize plant-based eating in NYC. The guac burger is the signature — a black bean patty with guacamole that actually tastes like something. The mac and cheese is vegan and convincing. The kale Caesar is reliable. Multiple locations. Not revolutionary, but dependable, affordable, and exactly what a quick vegan lunch should be.

name: Nish Nush
description: FiDi falafel shop with crispy, herb-packed falafel balls, creamy hummus, and vibrant Mediterranean bowls at quick-service prices. The shakshuka is a solid brunch option. The sabich — fried eggplant, egg, and tahini in pita — is the insider order. Quick, affordable, and genuine. One of the best cheap lunches in the Financial District. Vegetarian and halal friendly.

name: Gazala's
description: The only Druze restaurant in New York, and one of the most unique culinary experiences in Hell's Kitchen. The kibbeh, the lamb chops, and the hummus are all deeply flavorful and rooted in a tradition that few diners have encountered.
