Chef Gazala Halabi brings a culture to the table that deserves far more attention. The Middle Eastern small plates are generous and shareable.

name: Manousheh
description: Greenwich Village Lebanese flatbread shop baking fresh manakeesh topped with za'atar, cheese, and labneh. The za'atar manakeesh is the essential order — warm, crispy, and herbaceous. Perfect for a quick breakfast or late-night snack. The labneh with olive oil is creamy and addictive. Open late. Cash friendly. One of the best cheap eats in the Village.

name: Mu Ramen
description: Long Island City ramen from chef Joshua Smookler with handmade noodles and a tonkotsu broth that's been simmered to rich, porky perfection. The spicy miso ramen brings real heat. The pork buns are excellent. The space is small and intimate. One of the best ramen shops in Queens, hiding in an LIC side street where most tourists never wander.

name: Ippudo
description: The NYC outpost of Shigemi Kawahara legendary Hakata-style ramen chain that has been slinging tonkotsu since 1985. The East Village location still draws crowds for its rich, porky broth and perfectly springy thin noodles. The hirata buns are a must-order starter, and the Akamaru Modern is the bowl that put them on the map here.

name: Atoboy
description: Junghyun Park's Korean small plates restaurant where you pick three dishes and the kitchen builds a prix fixe around them. The format is brilliantly simple — choose your adventure, and every adventure is excellent. The rice cakes are outstanding, the seasonal courses surprise, and the wine program goes deep. One of the most creative dining formats in the city. Resy.

name: Her Name is Han
description: Modern Korean in NoMad with an approachable menu spanning bibimbap, japchae, and Korean fried chicken. The execution is consistent and the prices are fair. The fried chicken is crispy and well-seasoned. The japchae is silky. Nothing revolutionary, but everything is done well, and sometimes that's exactly what you need. OpenTable. Good for groups.

name: Soothr
description: East Village Thai with bold Isan flavors and some of the spiciest food in the neighborhood. The crispy catfish salad is fiery and addictive, the green curry is properly hot, and the pad krapow is textbook. Not trying to be trendy — just executing authentic Thai with care. The lunch special is one of the best deals downtown.

name: Han Dynasty
description: Sichuan restaurant in the East Village with authentic dan dan noodles, dry pot, and mapo tofu that brings genuine mala numbness. The dan dan noodles are rich with sesame and chili. The dry pot is fiery and customizable. The mapo tofu is silky and electric with Sichuan peppercorn. Multiple locations. The East Village spot has the best energy. Affordable and fiercely flavored.

name: Taverna Kyclades
description: The Astoria Greek taverna with a legendary grilled octopus and lines that wrap around the block every single night. The whole grilled fish is outstanding — perfectly charred and simply dressed. The fried calamari is textbook crispy. No reservations, cash only, and a wait that can hit 90 minutes on weekends. Arrive at 5 PM opening for the best shot. Worth every minute.

name: S&P Lunch
description: Hole-in-the-wall Thai lunch spot in Chinatown with outstanding khao soi, pad see ew, and curries at prices that make Midtown weep. The khao soi is the must-order — rich coconut curry over egg noodles with crispy noodle topping. The pad see ew is wok-charred and excellent. Cash only, tiny space, counter service. One of the best cheap lunches below Canal Street.

name: Taim
description: West Village falafel pioneer with three varieties of crispy falafel — traditional, harissa, and red pepper — that are herb-packed and perfectly fried. The sabich is excellent. The smoothies are refreshing. The Mediterranean bowls are colorful and satisfying. Multiple locations. The Waverly Place original is the one that started it all. Quick, fresh, and genuinely healthy.

name: Mission Chinese Food
description: Danny Bowien's LES Chinese-American that rewrote the rules of what Chinese food in America could be. The Kung Pao pastrami is a collision of Jewish deli and Sichuan cooking that somehow works perfectly. The thrice-cooked bacon is insane. The mapo tofu is fiery and electric. The neon-lit space is chaotic and loud. Not for a quiet dinner. Perfect for an unforgettable one.

name: Miss Lily's
description: Vibrant Greenwich Village Jamaican restaurant with jerk chicken, oxtail, and rum cocktails in a colorful, music-filled space. The jerk seasoning is properly spicy and smoky. The oxtail is tender and rich. The rum punch flows freely and dangerously. Live music and DJ nights make this more than a restaurant — it's a party. Open late. The Village's Caribbean heartbeat.

name: Totonno's Pizzeria Napolitana
description: Operating since 1924, Totonno is one of New York true pizza legends -- a no-frills Coney Island institution charring pies in the same coal-fired oven for a century. The pizza is magnificently simple: thin, blistered crust with high-quality mozzarella and a bright tomato sauce. Cash only, limited hours, occasionally closed on a whim -- old New York at its best and most frustrating.

name: Maison Pickle
description: UWS comfort food with a famous 30-layer French onion dip that arrives at the table still bubbling. The Pickle Burger is outstanding — garlicky, tangy, and perfectly messy. The fried chicken is crispy and juicy. Brunch draws a lively neighborhood crowd. Happy hour 4-6 PM weekdays is a steal. The kind of restaurant that makes the Upper West Side feel young again.

name: Baohaus
description: Eddie Huang's East Village bao shop — juicy pork belly in fluffy steamed buns that put Taiwanese street food on the NYC map. The Chairman Bao is the original and still the best. The taro fries are addictive. The space is tiny and loud. Huang's personality is baked into every bite — bold, unapologetic, and impossible to ignore. Walk-in only.
address: 97 St Marks Pl, New York, NY 10009

name: Rahi
description: Greenwich Village Indian from chef Chintan Pandya with innovative regional dishes and a refined cocktail program that goes deep into Indian spirits. The lamb rogan josh is rich and complex. The black truffle naan is an indulgence worth ordering. The paneer tikka is smoky and well-spiced. More polished than Dhamaka, more adventurous than most uptown Indian spots. Resy.

name: Dekalb Market Hall
description: Downtown Brooklyn sprawling underground food hall packs in around 40 vendors and is a legit destination for serious eating. The anchor tenant is a Katz Deli outpost slinging the same pastrami as the LES original, but the real move is exploring the global lineup. Chaotic, loud, and perfect for groups who can never agree on what to eat.

name: Patsy's Pizzeria
description: East Harlem coal-fired pizzeria since 1933 — Frank Sinatra's favorite, and the thin-crust pies with a proper char justify the legend. The first pizza to be sold by the slice in New York, and the concept caught on. The sausage roll is a hidden gem. Multiple locations now, but the 1st Avenue original in East Harlem is the one with the history and the soul.

name: Spot Dessert Bar
description: East Village Asian-inspired dessert restaurant with theatrical plated desserts that look like modern art and taste like a sugar rush from the future. The Golden Toast — a honey-soaked bread topped with ice cream — is the showstopper. The matcha lava cake oozes green. The Harvest is a feast for two. The perfect post-dinner destination for people with a sweet tooth and a camera.
address: 13 St Marks Pl, New York, NY 10003

name: Arepa Lady
description: Maria Piedad Cano started selling arepas from a street cart in Jackson Heights in the 90s and built a devoted following that turned into a brick-and-mortar empire. The arepas de choclo -- sweet corn cakes stuffed with molten cheese -- are absolutely iconic. This is Colombian comfort food at its purest, and Jackson Heights is the only place it feels right eating it.

name: Mokyo
description: Cozy East Village izakaya with creative Japanese small plates, sake flights, and a lively atmosphere that makes every visit feel like a celebration. The karaage is crispy and well-seasoned. The okonomiyaki is savory and satisfying. The sake selection goes deep into regions most NYC spots ignore. Walk-in friendly. The perfect neighborhood Japanese spot.
address: 109 St Marks Pl, New York, NY 10009

name: Tim Ho Wan
description: Hong Kong's famous Michelin-starred dim sum chain made the leap to NYC, and the BBQ pork buns — with their golden, crackly top — are as good as advertised. The cheung fun is silky, the congee is comforting, and the sheer efficiency of the operation is impressive. The most affordable Michelin-connected dining experience in the city. Walk-in. East Village location.

name: Don Ceviche
description: Midtown Peruvian cevicheria with fresh daily ceviches, lomo saltado, and pisco sours in a bright, cheerful space. The classic ceviche is bright and citrusy, the lomo saltado is hearty and wok-fired, and the pisco sours are dangerously smooth. One of the better quick-lunch options near Times Square. The anticuchos are a solid appetizer. Walk-in friendly.

name: Jollibee Times Square
description: Filipino fast-food phenomenon in Times Square serving Chickenjoy fried chicken that's crispier and juicier than it has any right to be. The sweet spaghetti is an acquired taste that becomes an addiction. The halo-halo is a refreshing dessert that confuses and delights in equal measure. A cultural institution for Filipino New Yorkers and a gateway for everyone else.

name: Teranga
description: Harlem fast-casual West African restaurant serving grain bowls, jollof rice, plantains, and Senegalese stews at accessible prices. The mafe bowl is rich and peanutty. The jollof rice is fragrant. The fried plantains are perfectly caramelized. Healthy, affordable, and rooted in genuine West African tradition. The Museum District location inside the Africa Center is the flagship.

name: Thai Market
description: UWS Thai standby with a comprehensive menu of curries, stir-fries, and noodle dishes that's been feeding the neighborhood for years. The pad thai is reliable, the green curry is properly coconutty, and the tom yum soup is warming and bright. Not pushing boundaries — just executing the classics at a fair price in a neighborhood that counts on it.

name: Huertas
description: East Village Basque-inspired pintxos and wine bar with an outstanding outdoor garden and approachable Spanish plates. The pintxos are creative and well-executed. The txuleta steak is a showpiece for sharing. The croquetas are creamy and crispy. The sherry selection is deep. Happy hour 5-7 PM is a legitimate deal. Resy. One of the best patios in the East Village.

name: Okonomi
description: Williamsburg Japanese breakfast spot serving traditional morning sets with grilled fish, miso soup, and rice — a quiet, meditative meal in a neighborhood that's usually screaming for your attention. The breakfast set is beautiful and balanced. The grilled mackerel is perfectly charred. Open mornings only. Cash preferred. The most peaceful meal in Williamsburg.

name: Los Hermanos
description: East Harlem Dominican restaurant serving hearty plates of pernil, mofongo, and mangu with a loyal neighborhood following that spans generations. The pernil is slow-roasted and falling apart. The mofongo is garlicky and satisfying. The mangu with onions is breakfast perfection. Cash only. Open early and late. Dominican home cooking at its most genuine.

name: Bua Thai
description: Cash-only East Village Thai with some of the best pad see ew and massaman curry in the neighborhood at prices that haven't caught up with inflation. The massaman is rich, peanutty, and comforting. The mango sticky rice is the perfect finish. Open late, always reliable, and the kind of neighborhood Thai spot that you come back to weekly without even thinking about it.

name: Theodora
description: Acclaimed Upper East Side Greek with elevated Aegean cuisine, wood-fired dishes, and a garden that feels transported from a Cycladic island. The grilled octopus is exceptional, the lamb chops are perfectly charred, and the spanakopita is flaky and authentic. One of the most talked-about UES openings in years. Resy. The kind of Greek food that makes Astoria nervous.

name: Roscioli NYC
description: Rome's legendary Roscioli deli made its NYC debut in SoHo with imported salumi, cheeses, and refined Roman pastas that taste like a first-class flight to Trastevere. The cacio e pepe is textbook — simple, peppery, and executed with Roman precision. The carbonara is equally flawless. The salumi board is an education in Italian cured meats. Resy.

name: Mama's Too
description: UWS pizza shop with supremely thick, cheesy square slices that went viral for a reason — the spicy spring square with hot honey is aggressively good. The pepperoni square has crispy cups pooling with beautiful orange grease. The line wraps around Broadway. Not Neapolitan, not New York-style — something proudly its own. Cash friendly.

name: Hawksmoor
description: London import steakhouse in Gramercy serving premium British beef that rivals any American steakhouse. The bone-in ribeye is extraordinary — dry-aged and deeply beefy. The bone marrow is rich and decadent. The sticky toffee pudding is mandatory. The gorgeous room feels like a London club. Resy. The most civilized steakhouse experience in New York.

name: K'Far
description: Michael Solomonov's all-day Israeli bakery in NoMad with pastries and dishes that prove Philadelphia's best export isn't cheesesteaks. The shakshuka is spiced and perfect. The kubaneh (Yemeni pull-apart bread) is addictive. The chocolate babka rivals Breads Bakery. Breakfast through dinner, and every hour has its charms. Resy.

name: Delmonico's
description: America first fine dining restaurant, opened in 1837, still serving steaks in the Financial District to power-lunch brokers and tourists who appreciate a side of history with their porterhouse. The Delmonico steak was literally invented here, and the old-school wood-paneled dining room feels like stepping into a Wall Street time capsule. The gravitas is unmatched.

name: Place des Fêtes
description: Clinton Hill French-West African wine bar with creative natural wines, inventive small plates, and a vibrant neighborhood energy. The suya lamb ribs and the jollof arancini fuse West African flavors with French technique in ways that feel effortless and exciting. The wine list is eclectic and affordable. One of Brooklyn's most original restaurants. Resy.

name: Dept of Culture
description: Williamsburg Caribbean restaurant with jerk chicken, oxtail, and rum cocktails that bring genuine island energy to South 3rd Street. The jerk seasoning has real scotch bonnet heat. The oxtail is braised until it falls apart. The rum punch is tropical and dangerous. The reggae soundtrack and colorful space make every night feel like a vacation. Resy.

name: Zou Zou's
description: Madeline Sperling's vibrant Mediterranean at Hudson Yards with flatbreads, grilled meats, and a scene that buzzes from lunch through late dinner. The halloumi flatbread is the hit — salty, crispy, and shareable. The lamb kebabs are tender and well-spiced. The mezze spread is generous. The best restaurant at Hudson Yards by a considerable margin. Resy.

name: Ha's Snack Bar
description: The toughest reservation on the Lower East Side, and earning it. This intimate Vietnamese snack bar serves creative cocktails and refined Vietnamese small plates in a space so tiny and buzzy that every night feels like a private party. The team expanded into Bistrot Ha nearby. If you can get in, order everything and let the bartender guide the cocktails. Resy.

name: Rule of Thirds
description: Stylish Williamsburg izakaya in a converted warehouse with creative Japanese dishes, excellent sake, and DJ nights that turn dinner into a full evening. The karaage is crispy and addictive. The wagyu tataki is silky. The sake flights are well-curated. Saturday brunch adds a daytime dimension. Resy. The coolest Japanese restaurant in Brooklyn.

name: Coqodaq
description: Korean fried chicken specialist in Flatiron with double-fried crispy chicken, creative sauces, and sides worth ordering. The gochujang wings are fiery and addictive. The pickled daikon is the perfect cooling counterpoint. The fried chicken sandwich went viral for good reason. Walk-in, counter service, and a line that forms at lunch. One of the best casual Korean spots in Manhattan.

name: Gupshup
description: Modern Indian street food and cocktail bar near Gramercy with inventive chaat, creative dosas, and Indian-inspired cocktails that take familiar flavors somewhere unexpected. The lamb seekh kebab is smoky and well-spiced. The chaat is crunchy, tangy, and complex. The cocktail program uses Indian spices in genuinely creative ways. Resy.

name: Bangkok Supper Club
description: Upscale Meatpacking Thai supper club with late-night energy, Isan-inspired dishes, and a DJ-fueled atmosphere that turns dinner into a party. The papaya salad has real heat. The pad krapow is wok-fired and fragrant. The Thai cocktails are creative and strong. After 10 PM the energy shifts from restaurant to nightlife. Resy. Dress up.

name: Nami Nori
description: This West Village spot from former Masa chefs takes the hand roll -- temaki -- and elevates it into a full dining experience that is both fun and ridiculously delicious. The nori is always impeccably crispy, the rice is warm, and the fillings range from classic tuna to creative specials. A genuinely unique concept and the counter seating makes for a great date night.

name: Shuka
description: SoHo Mediterranean from the Balaboosta team with lamb kebabs, hummus, and a stunning brunch menu. The shakshuka is vibrant and properly spiced. The lamb kebab plate is generous and perfectly grilled. The space is beautiful — warm wood, natural light, and a downtown crowd. Brunch draws serious lines. Resy. The best Mediterranean in SoHo.

name: Cafe Spaghetti
description: Charming Williamsburg Italian with fresh pasta, classic red sauce dishes, and a cozy neighborhood feel that makes you want to come back every Tuesday. The spaghetti pomodoro is simple and perfect. The meatballs are grandma-approved. The tiramisu is honest. Walk-in friendly on weeknights. Resy for weekends. The kind of Italian spot every neighborhood deserves.

name: Chez Ma Tante
description: French-inspired Greenpoint restaurant with NYC's most Instagrammed pancakes and seasonal dinners that serious food people love. The pancakes at brunch are genuinely extraordinary — thick, fluffy, and served with cultured butter. The dinner tasting menu is creative and seasonal. The natural wine list is fun. Resy. One of Brooklyn's most charming restaurants.

name: Bar Blondeau
description: Sitting on the roof of the building adjacent to the Wythe Hotel in Williamsburg, Bar Blondeau is the kind of effortlessly cool rooftop that makes you feel like you are in a French film set in Brooklyn. The Manhattan skyline views are stunning, the wine list is well-curated with a French lean, and the vibe is relaxed enough that you do not feel like you are at a club. Perfect summer sunset spot.

name: Westlight
description: 22nd-floor rooftop at The William Vale with 360-degree views that rival helicopter tours. The craft cocktails are creative and well-made. The oysters are fresh. The small plates are solid. Sunset is magical — the skyline wraps around you from every angle. Resy. The most dramatic rooftop bar in Brooklyn. Dress code enforced.

name: Ophelia
description: Glamorous Midtown rooftop at the Beekman Tower with Chrysler Building views and Art Deco design that makes every cocktail feel like a scene from a Gatsby party. The drinks are creative and strong. The space is intimate compared to mega-rooftops. Resy. Come at golden hour for the most dramatic Chrysler Building photos you'll ever take.

name: Brooklyn Crab
description: Massive Red Hook waterfront seafood restaurant with three levels, mini golf, and views of the Statue of Liberty that make you wonder why this place isn't more famous. The crab bucket is messy and satisfying. The lobster roll is buttery. The fried clams are crispy. Come with a group, order a bucket of crabs, and stay all afternoon. The most fun seafood experience in Brooklyn.

name: St. Anselm
description: This Williamsburg gem does the impossible -- serves some of the best steak in NYC at prices that will not require a second mortgage. The small, rustic space with its open grill is all about fire-kissed meats, with the butcher steak being one of the best deals in the city. Do not sleep on the grilled vegetables either; the whole carrots off that grill are transcendent.

name: Bien Cuit
description: Zachary Golper Carroll Gardens bakery is where NYC bread nerds go to worship -- the sourdough is dark, deeply caramelized, and unlike anything else in the city. Bien cuit literally means well done in French, and these loaves take that philosophy to glorious extremes with long fermentations and bold crusts. You are coming here for bread that will ruin you for all other bread.

name: Hart's
description: Bed-Stuy neighborhood gem with seasonal New American dishes, natural wines, and a charming backyard garden. The cooking is thoughtful without being pretentious. The heirloom salad changes with the market. The pasta is handmade. The brunch on weekends is one of Brooklyn's best-kept secrets. Resy. The restaurant that made Hart Street a dining destination.

name: Frankies 457 Spuntino
description: Carroll Gardens Italian institution since 2004 with handmade pastas, meatballs, and a gorgeous backyard garden that's one of the most romantic outdoor dining spaces in Brooklyn. The cavatelli with hot sausage is the cult dish. The meatballs are light and herby. The seasonal salad is always perfect. BYOB-friendly. Resy. A Brooklyn classic that only gets better.

name: L&B Spumoni Gardens
description: Brooklyn institution since 1939 famous for the Sicilian square slice — sauce on top, cheese on bottom — that's unlike any other pizza in the city. The crust is thick, crispy, and slightly sweet. The spumoni ice cream is the required dessert. Gravesend is a trek, but L&B is a pilgrimage for serious pizza lovers. Cash only. Outdoor seating in summer.

name: Hanoi House
description: East Village Vietnamese with Hanoi street food elevated — the bone marrow pho is the showstopper, with marrow melting into rich beef broth. The crispy spring rolls are shatteringly crisp. The bun cha is fragrant and perfectly grilled. The intimate two-floor space is charming. Resy. One of the best Vietnamese restaurants in Manhattan.
address: 119 St Marks Pl, New York, NY 10009

name: Somtum Der
description: Bangkok import specializing in fiery Isan papaya salads — a dozen varieties ranging from merely spicy to face-meltingly hot. The larb is bright and herbaceous. The grilled pork neck is smoky and addictive. The sticky rice is the essential accompaniment. Michelin Bib Gourmand. Walk-in. The most authentic Isan food in Manhattan, and the spice levels don't lie.

name: Eataly NYC Flatiron
description: The Italian food emporium that is part market, part restaurant complex, part sensory overload in the best way. The Flatiron flagship is a sprawling temple to Italian gastronomy with multiple restaurants, a rooftop beer garden, and enough imported products to fill a warehouse. Come hungry, come with a plan, or just wander and eat your way through.

name: Eileen's Special Cheesecake
description: Nolita institution since 1978 making miniature New York-style cheesecakes in dozens of flavors. Rich, creamy, and small enough to justify ordering three — the plain original is perfection, the strawberry is the crowd-pleaser, and the seasonal specials are always worth trying. The display case is overwhelming in the best way. Cash friendly.

name: Mokbar
description: Esther Choi Korean ramen fusion spot in Chelsea Market is a tiny, mighty operation that smashes together Korean flavors with ramen technique and makes it all sing. The kimchi ramen and the rice cakes are the move, and everything hits with authentic Korean depth. A quick, casual counter situation -- grab a stool and slurp.

name: Saigon Social
description: LES Vietnamese cocktail bar with pho cocktails (yes, really), banh mi sliders, and a vibrant downtown atmosphere that turns dinner into a night out. The Vietnamese coffee cocktail is creative and delicious. The banh mi sliders are fun shareable starters. The energy is festive and loud. Resy. Date-night energy with Vietnamese flair.

name: Blue Ribbon Sushi
description: The SoHo sushi spot where chefs and industry people go after their own shifts close — open until 2 AM, which is the entire point. The omakase is excellent, the yellowtail jalapeno is a classic, and the late-night crowd gives it an energy that daytime sushi can't match. The Bromberg brothers built a late-night institution. Walk-in at the bar.

name: Margot
description: Charming Carroll Gardens French bistro with a gorgeous garden patio, seasonal prix fixe, and a weekend brunch that's one of the best in south Brooklyn. The steak frites is perfectly executed. The duck confit is rich and tender. The creme brulee is textbook. The garden in summer is romantic and leafy. Resy. The restaurant that makes Carroll Gardens feel like Paris.

name: Borgo
description: NoHo Italian from the Major Food Group team with handmade pastas, elegant cocktails, and a moody downtown atmosphere. The cacio e pepe is silky and peppery. The lamb ragu is rich and deeply flavored. The tiramisu is the proper finish. The space is dark and sexy — candlelight and exposed brick. One of the more intimate MFG experiences. Resy. Trending.

name: Leland Eating and Drinking House
description: Brooklyn Heights neighborhood restaurant with seasonal New American fare, craft cocktails, and a warm hospitality approach. The menu changes with the market. The burger is excellent. The cocktails are creative without being overwrought. Weekend brunch has a loyal neighborhood following. Resy. The kind of place that makes you glad you live in the Heights.

name: La Vara
description: Alex Raij and Eder Montero Carroll Gardens spot is a deeply personal love letter to Spanish and Moorish cooking that goes way beyond tapas-bar cliches. The menu digs into the Jewish and Arabic influences on Iberian cuisine, producing dishes that feel both ancient and totally fresh. Cozy, romantic, and perfect for sharing small plates with sherry.

name: Luke's Lobster
description: Sustainable lobster shack born in the East Village with butter-drenched Maine lobster rolls, crab rolls, and clam chowder that taste like a New England fishing village. The lobster is traceable to the boat. Multiple locations across Manhattan. The original East Village shop is tiny and always has a line. The crab roll is the sleeper hit.

name: Planta Queen
description: Plant-based Asian restaurant in Flatiron with vegan dim sum, sushi, and noodle dishes that would fool most carnivores. The cauliflower tempura is crispy and addictive. The vegan dim sum is creative and well-executed. The space is chic and Instagram-friendly. Resy. Proof that plant-based Asian food can be genuinely exciting and not just virtuous.

name: Paulie Gee's
description: Greenpoint wood-fired pizzeria with creative pies and a BYOB policy that makes it one of the best values in Brooklyn. The Hellboy — sopressata with hot honey — is the cult favorite. The cherry jones (cherry tomato, basil, fresh mozz) is perfect simplicity. The dough is naturally leavened and perfectly charred. Walk-in only. The most beloved pizzeria in Greenpoint.

name: Ci Siamo
description: Danny Meyer and chef Hillary Sterling's wood-fired Italian at Hudson Yards is the restaurant that justifies the entire development. The rigatoni alla vodka is silky and smoky. The olive oil cake is exceptional. The open kitchen with its massive hearth is mesmerizing. The cocktails are polished. Michelin recommended. SevenRooms reservations. One of the best new Italians in the city.
address: 440 W 33rd St Suite #100, New York, NY 10001

name: Café Carmellini
description: Andrew Carmellini's newest SoHo Italian cafe is refined but never fussy — handmade pastas, seasonal plates, and an all-day neighborhood energy. The branzino is pristine. The pasta changes daily and is always excellent. The tiramisu is classic. The space is beautiful without trying too hard. Resy. The kind of Italian cafe that SoHo was built for.

name: Yellow Rose
description: Tex-Mex in the East Village from a Texan team that actually knows what queso should taste like. The breakfast tacos are authentic — flour tortillas, scrambled eggs, and proper salsa. The queso is creamy and addictive. The margaritas are strong. Michelin recommended, which is wild for a Tex-Mex spot. Resy. Austin energy on Avenue A.

name: CheLi
description: Shanghai-born restaurant in the East Village with refined Shanghainese dishes, pristine xiao long bao, and a curated Chinese wine list. The lion's head meatball is enormous and tender. The Shanghainese noodles are silky. The xiao long bao rival Flushing's best. Michelin recommended. Resy. The most sophisticated Shanghainese food in Manhattan.
address: 19 St Marks Pl, New York, NY 10003

name: Pinch Chinese
description: Modern Chinese in SoHo with handmade dumplings, crispy scallion pancakes, and dim sum cocktail pairings. The soup dumplings are well-crafted. The Peking duck is a showpiece. The scallion pancakes are flaky and fragrant. Multiple locations. The Prince Street SoHo flagship has the best atmosphere. Resy. Accessible Chinese that doesn't dumb anything down.

name: Little Alley
description: Upscale Midtown Sichuan with high-end Chinese dishes, mapo tofu, and dan dan noodles in a polished setting. The mapo tofu is electric with peppercorn. The dan dan noodles are rich and sesame-forward. The kung pao chicken is crispy and well-balanced. More refined than Chinatown, less stuffy than most Midtown Chinese. Resy.

name: Laliko
description: Georgian food finally gets its proper NYC moment at this West Village charmer that showcases one of the world most underrated cuisines. Khachapuri -- that glorious cheese-filled bread -- is the star, but the whole menu of dumplings, walnut-laced salads, and grilled meats deserves attention. The natural wine list pairs beautifully with the bold, herbaceous flavors.

name: Jazba
description: East Village Pakistani with refined biryani, seekh kebabs, and an impressive cocktail program that blends South Asian spices with modern mixology. The biryani is fragrant and layered. The seekh kebab is charred and juicy. The nihari is rich and slow-cooked. Halal. Resy. One of the most exciting Pakistani restaurants to open in NYC in years.

name: Uluh
description: East Village Indonesian serving rendang, satay, and nasi goreng with creative cocktails. The rendang is deeply spiced and coconutty. The satay is charred and served with proper peanut sauce. The nasi goreng is wok-fried with the right amount of sweetness. Resy. One of the very few Indonesian restaurants in Manhattan, and it delivers.

name: B&H Dairy
description: Tiny East Village counter serving challah French toast, matzo ball soup, and pierogies since 1938. Kosher-vegetarian, cash only, and operating with the quiet dignity of a restaurant that's survived nine decades of New York. The challah French toast is legendary. The matzo ball soup heals everything. Open daily. One of the last authentic luncheonettes in the city.

name: Ayat
description: Abdul Elenani Palestinian restaurant has become one of the most celebrated spots in southern Brooklyn, serving soulful, vibrant mezze and grilled meats that tell the story of Palestinian home cooking. The musakhan -- sumac-spiced chicken over taboon bread -- is a revelation, and the hummus is silky perfection. Worth the trek for food this honest and delicious.

name: The Corner Store
description: Trendy SoHo all-day cafe with excellent egg sandwiches, acai bowls, and a buzzy brunch scene on Lafayette Street. The egg sandwich on a house-made roll is the go-to. The avocado toast is well-executed. The sidewalk tables are prime people-watching in SoHo. Resy. The kind of spot where you see someone famous and pretend you didn't.

name: Barbuto
description: Jonathan Waxman's iconic West Village Italian with the famous roast chicken — herb butter, crispy skin, and a simplicity that most chefs can't achieve. The kale salad is excellent. The seasonal pastas change with the market. The garage-door dining room opens onto Jane Street in warm weather. A decade-old institution that keeps earning its reputation. Resy.

name: Leo
description: From the people behind Lilia, Leo brings the same fresh pasta mastery to a more expansive Williamsburg space inside the old Domino Sugar factory. The pizza and pasta are both outstanding -- Italian-American cooking through a fine-dining lens that still manages to feel fun and approachable. Big, buzzy, and the waterfront setting is gorgeous.

name: Brasserie Fouquet
description: Parisian import from the Champs-Elysees now in Tribeca, serving French brasserie classics in a grand, glamorous setting. The steak tartare is properly seasoned. The Dover sole is buttery and precise. The creme brulee is textbook. The room feels like being transported to Paris's 8th arrondissement. Resy. The most authentically Parisian restaurant in downtown Manhattan.

name: Twin Tails
description: SoHo seafood with fresh crudo, lobster rolls, and a sleek raw bar perfect for date night. The crudo changes daily with the freshest catch. The lobster roll is buttery and generous. The oyster selection is well-curated. The space is stylish without being intimidating. Resy. Clean flavors and beautiful plating in a neighborhood that appreciates both.

name: Hearth
description: Marco Canora's East Village Italian-American with seasonal farm-to-table cooking and a bone broth window next door that launched a wellness trend. The cooking is honest and ingredient-driven. The brick chicken is the signature. The pasta changes seasonally. The wine list is thoughtful. Resy. One of the most quietly excellent restaurants in the East Village.

name: Crif Dogs
description: East Village hot dog joint famous for bacon-wrapped deep-fried dogs with creative toppings — and for hiding speakeasy PDT behind a phone booth in the back. The Spicy Redneck is wrapped in bacon and covered in chili and coleslaw. The Chihuahua has avocado and sour cream. Open until 2 AM. Cash only. The gateway drug to PDT.
address: 113 St Marks Pl, New York, NY 10009

name: Nyonya
description: Chinatown Malaysian with fragrant laksa, roti canai, and Hainanese chicken rice at great value. The laksa broth is coconutty and complex. The roti canai is flaky and served with proper curry dip. The Hainanese chicken is poached to silky perfection. The prices are Chinatown-friendly. Cash only. One of the best Southeast Asian meals below Canal Street.

name: Taiyaki NYC
description: Instagram-famous Chinatown dessert spot with fish-shaped waffle cones filled with soft serve in matcha, black sesame, and custard. The taiyaki cone is crispy, warm, and photogenic. The matcha soft serve is earthy and bittersweet. The mochi doughnuts are chewy and creative. A fun, quick dessert stop on Baxter Street. Cash friendly.

name: Taiwan Pork Chop House
description: No-frills Chinatown Taiwanese with the famous crispy pork chop over rice — breaded, fried golden, and served over steaming white rice with pickled vegetables. Simple, generous, and deeply satisfying. The beef noodle soup is also excellent. Cash only, point-and-order system, and some of the most honest food on Doyers Street. Under $10.

name: Mile End Delicatessen
description: Montreal-style Jewish deli in NoHo with house-smoked meat, poutine, and bagels flown in from Montreal. The smoked meat sandwich is thick-cut, juicy, and properly spiced. The poutine with gravy and cheese curds is authentic. The Montreal bagel is smaller and sweeter than its NYC cousin. A taste of Quebec on Bond Street.

name: Nubeluz
description: Sexy rooftop bar atop the Ritz-Carlton NoMad with panoramic views, creative cocktails, and a nightlife energy that makes it one of the hottest spots in Midtown after dark. The cocktails are theatrical and strong. The space is designed for atmosphere first, and it delivers. Resy. Come after 10 PM on weekends for the full effect. Dress code enforced.

name: Dear Irving on Hudson
description: Glamorous rooftop cocktail bar at the Aliz Hotel with sweeping Hudson River views and era-themed cocktail menus that feel like time travel in a glass. The Art Deco design is stunning. The cocktails are precise and creative. The views at sunset are extraordinary. Resy. The most sophisticated rooftop bar in Hell's Kitchen.

name: Magic Hour Rooftop
description: Whimsical Midtown rooftop at the Moxy with a topiary garden, carnival games, mini putt-putt, and Empire State Building views. The cocktails are fun and frozen. The atmosphere is more party than lounge. The mini golf with the Empire State glowing behind you is a pure New York moment. Walk-in. The most playful rooftop in the city.

name: Alma
description: Carroll Gardens Mexican with a stunning rooftop and Statue of Liberty views. The mole is complex and refined. The enchiladas are comforting. The mezcal cocktails are smoky and well-crafted. The rooftop in summer — Lady Liberty in the distance, a margarita in hand — is one of Brooklyn's best dining moments. Resy. Brunch on weekends is excellent.

name: The Odeon
description: Tribeca bistro icon since 1980 — a fixture of downtown NYC history that anchored the neighborhood before it was cool. The steak frites and the tuna tartare are timeless. The bar scene is still relevant. The late-night kitchen keeps serving until midnight. The room hasn't changed much in four decades, and that's the point. Walk-in friendly. A living landmark.

name: Beauty & Essex
description: The restaurant hidden behind a pawn shop facade on the LES. Push through the vintage display cases and find a multi-room restaurant with shareable dishes, craft cocktails, and a sexy, dimly lit atmosphere. The tuna poke wonton tacos are the Instagram hit. The grilled cheese dumplings are unexpectedly excellent. Resy. The most theatrical entrance in downtown dining.

name: Emilio's Ballato
description: Old-school Nolita Italian red-sauce joint beloved by celebrities, with simple pastas, veal parm, and zero pretension. The spaghetti pomodoro is honest and perfectly sauced. The chicken scarpariello is lemony and bright. The veal parm is massive. No website, no Instagram, no reservations — just show up and eat like it's 1975. The most genuine Italian in Nolita.
address: 55 E Houston St, New York, NY 10012

name: ABC Kitchen
description: Jean-Georges Vongerichten farm-to-table restaurant inside ABC Carpet and Home was one of the defining restaurants of the 2010s NYC dining scene, championing local and organic ingredients before it was trendy. The space is ethereally beautiful and the cooking is clean, seasonal, and elegant, proving that restraint can be just as exciting as excess.

name: The Dutch
description: Andrew Carmellini's bustling SoHo corner restaurant with an American menu that hits every note — great oysters, an excellent burger, and a brunch that draws SoHo crowds every weekend. The fried chicken is crispy and juicy. The lobster roll is buttery. The cocktails are polished. Resy. The most reliable all-purpose restaurant in SoHo.

name: Wildair
description: Jeremiah Stone and Fabian von Hauske LES wine bar is the cooler, more casual sibling to their restaurant Contra -- a place where natural wine flows freely and the small plates are way more ambitious than they need to be for a bar. The menu changes constantly, but everything is executed with precision. This is where chefs eat on their nights off.

name: Contra
description: LES tasting menu restaurant with a creative, affordable multicourse dinner and natural wines in a minimalist space. The five-course menu is one of the best values in fine dining — creative, seasonal, and genuinely surprising. The sister wine bar Wildair is next door. Together they form downtown's most exciting dining duo. Resy.

name: Cervo's
description: LES Mediterranean seafood bar with Portuguese-inspired tinned fish, grilled sardines, and piri piri chicken in a lively sidewalk scene. The tinned fish is curated and excellent — this is where NYC learned to love conservas. The grilled sardines are smoky and simple. The piri piri chicken is fiery. Walk-in. The most fun casual seafood on the Lower East Side.

name: Bar Pisellino
description: Rita Sodi and Jody Williams's West Village Italian cafe-bar with spritzes, negronis, and Italian pastries served in one of the most charming spaces in the city. The negroni is textbook. The cornetto is flaky. The afternoon aperitivo hour — standing at the bar with a spritz — is peak West Village living. Walk-in. No reservations needed for a piece of Florence on Grove Street.

name: Corner Bistro
description: The West Village dive bar with one of NYC's most legendary burgers — the Bistro Burger with bacon, cheese, and a cold beer is a combination that transcends trend. Cash only, no nonsense, and open until 4 AM. The fries are fine. The atmosphere is wood-paneled and unpretentious. There are fancier burgers in the city. There are very few better ones.

name: Junior's Restaurant
description: Brooklyn institution since 1950 famous for towering New York cheesecake and mile-high deli sandwiches. The cheesecake is dense, creamy, and enormous — the strawberry version is the classic. The pastrami sandwich is piled impossibly high. The fried chicken is surprisingly good. Multiple locations, but the Downtown Brooklyn original near BAM has the soul.

name: Nathan's Famous
description: The original 1916 Coney Island hot dog stand that spawned an empire and an annual Fourth of July eating contest that has become as American as the hot dogs themselves. The snap of that natural casing dog with mustard and sauerkraut on the boardwalk is a quintessential NYC experience. Come for the nostalgia, the crinkle-cut fries, and the chaos of Coney Island.

name: Peter Pan Donut & Pastry Shop
description: Greenpoint institution since 1953 making old-school doughnuts — glazed, jelly, cruller, red velvet — behind a retro counter that time forgot. The glazed doughnut is simple perfection. The cruller is light and crispy. The line forms at dawn and the doughnuts sell out by afternoon. Cash only. The most beloved bakery in Greenpoint and arguably in all of Brooklyn.

name: P.J. Clarke's
description: Midtown tavern since 1884 with a classic burger, raw bar, and the kind of old-New York atmosphere that makes you feel like you're in a black-and-white photograph. The Cadillac Burger is the standard order. The raw bar is fresh and well-priced for Midtown. The Irish coffee is warming. Open late. The most authentic Midtown pub that isn't trying to be authentic.

name: Old Town Bar
description: Flatiron bar since 1892 with original mahogany bar, tin ceilings, and a solid burger in a setting that feels like a museum of Old New York. The Old Town Burger is thick and juicy. The onion rings are properly battered. The draft beer selection is curated. Walk-in. The bar itself is the main attraction — 130+ years of history in every worn groove.

name: Speedy Romeo
description: Clinton Hill wood-fired pizza with St. Louis-style pies, house-cured meats, and a serious wine program. The pies are thin-crusted and charred with creative seasonal toppings. The house charcuterie is excellent. The wine list leans natural and goes deep. The space is cozy and candlelit. Resy. One of the most interesting pizza experiences in Brooklyn.

name: Momofuku Ssäm Bar
description: David Chang's flagship East Village restaurant that launched a culinary empire. The bo ssam — slow-roasted pork shoulder with oysters, kimchi, and rice — is legendary and feeds the table. The rice cakes are excellent. The pork buns are the original. Resy. The restaurant that changed what Asian food in America could look like.

name: Ivan Ramen
description: Ivan Orkin's LES ramen shop — the story of a Jewish kid from Long Island who conquered Tokyo ramen and came home to do it again. The triple garlic mazemen is the signature — rich, garlicky, and unlike any other ramen in the city. The shio ramen is clean and elegant. Walk-in. One of the most personal ramen shops in New York.

name: Grand Army
description: Boerum Hill craft cocktail bar with an impeccable marble bar, serious drinks, and half-price oyster happy hour that's the best deal in Brooklyn. The classic cocktails are textbook — the martini, the daiquiri, the old fashioned are all perfect. The oysters are fresh and well-sourced. Walk-in. The most sophisticated cocktail bar in south Brooklyn.

name: Fanelli's Cafe
description: SoHo corner bar since 1847 — one of NYC's oldest continuously operating establishments, with a classic burger and the kind of weathered charm that new bars spend millions trying to fake. The draft beer is cold. The burger is honest. The walls have more history than most museums. Walk-in. No cocktail menu needed when the atmosphere does all the work.

name: The Smith
description: New York quintessential neighborhood brasserie -- reliable, buzzy, and always packed with a good-looking crowd eating elevated comfort food. The East Village location has great energy without the Midtown corporate feel of some other outposts. The place you go when you want a solid burger, a well-made cocktail, and a lively atmosphere without overthinking it.

name: Robataya
description: Theatrical East Village Japanese where chefs grill robata skewers over binchōtan charcoal and pass dishes to diners on long wooden paddles across the counter. The wagyu skewer is buttery. The grilled mochi is sticky and sweet. The experience of watching master grillers work over live fire is mesmerizing. Resy. The most visually stunning Japanese dining experience in the East Village.

name: Westville
description: Neighborhood American with a market-driven veggie-forward menu, great brunch, and a loyal following across multiple locations. The market plate — choose your protein and three sides — is the signature format. The kale Caesar is excellent. The sweet potato fries are mandatory. Walk-in friendly. The most reliable healthy-ish lunch in the city.

name: Golden Ratio
description: Sleek West Village cocktail bar with precise, balanced drinks and an intimate vibe that attracts serious drinkers without the pretension. The martini is textbook. The old fashioned is spirit-forward and flawless. The space is small enough that you feel like you're in on a secret. Resy. The kind of cocktail bar where you let the bartender choose and never regret it.

name: Katana Kitten
description: Masahiro Urushido West Village cocktail bar brilliantly bridges Japanese precision with American cocktail culture, creating drinks that are technically flawless and impossibly fun. The highball machine churns out the crispest, most perfectly carbonated whisky highballs in the city. The space has a warm izakaya energy that makes it dangerously easy to stay for three more rounds.

name: Angel's Share
description: The speakeasy that existed before speakeasies were trendy. Hidden above a Japanese restaurant on Stuyvesant Street since 1993, Angel's Share pioneered Japanese precision in NYC cocktails. The bartenders are artists. The rule is whispered: no standing, no loud talking, no parties over four. Walk-in only and the wait is part of the ritual. Sacred ground for cocktail lovers.

name: Dutch Kills
description: Dimly lit Long Island City cocktail bar with hand-carved ice, serious spirit-forward drinks, and an old-fashioned atmosphere in a neighborhood that's mostly wine bars and pizza joints. The whiskey sour is expertly balanced. The house punch is a gateway for non-cocktail people. Walk-in. One of the best bars in Queens, hiding on Jackson Avenue.

name: Amor y Amargo
description: The most intense bar in the East Village — dedicated entirely to bitters, amaro, and bitter cocktails. Tiny space, a few seats, and bartenders who can tell you more about Fernet-Branca than most Italians. The amaro flights are an education. The negroni variations are outstanding. Not for sweet-drink people. Walk-in. A pilgrimage for bitter enthusiasts.

name: Bar Goto
description: Kenta Goto's intimate LES cocktail bar where Japanese flavors meet classic cocktail technique in ways that feel effortless. The Sakura Martini — cherry blossom-infused — is iconic. The yuzu whisky sour is bright and balanced. The miso wings are the best bar snack downtown. Walk-in. The most personal cocktail bar on the Lower East Side.

name: Sugar Monk
description: Intimate Harlem speakeasy with elaborate, beautifully presented cocktails served in a plush, dimly lit space. The rum cocktails are the specialty — complex, layered, and served with ceremony. Live jazz sets on select nights add to the atmosphere. Resy. The most romantic bar in Harlem, hiding on Frederick Douglass Boulevard.
address: 2292 Frederick Douglass Blvd, New York, NY 10027

name: Martiny's
description: All-martini bar in NoMad where the entire menu is variations on a single cocktail — dirty, dry, espresso, reverse, frozen, and a dozen more. The concept sounds gimmicky but the execution is dead serious. The Art Deco space is gorgeous. Walk-in. The most fun single-concept bar in the city. Come as a martini skeptic, leave as a convert.

name: Schmuck
description: Barcelona import cocktail bar now in the East Village with theatrical, boundary-pushing drinks and an attitude that's equal parts cheeky and sophisticated. The cocktails are visual spectacles — smoking, bubbling, color-changing. The bar snacks are Spanish-influenced. Resy. The most Instagram-worthy cocktail experience downtown. The drinks actually taste as good as they look.

name: Tigre
description: Greenwich Village cocktail bar with a Latin American spirit focus — mezcal, pisco, cachaca — and a moody jungle atmosphere. The mezcal cocktails are smoky and complex. The small plates are Latin-influenced and shareable. The vibe is dark, leafy, and romantic. Resy. The best mezcal bar in Manhattan for people who want more than just a margarita.

name: The Portrait Bar
description: Cocktail lounge inside the Frick Madison where art-inspired drinks are served amid world-class paintings. Each cocktail is inspired by a work in the collection — Vermeer, Rembrandt, Bellini. The concept is elegant and the execution is thoughtful. Open Thursday through Sunday only. Walk-in. The most cultured drinking experience in New York.

name: Midnight Blue
description: Moody subterranean Tribeca cocktail bar with an intimate speakeasy energy, creative drinks, and late-night DJ sets. The cocktails are dark-spirited and inventive. The space is dimly lit and designed for lingering. Walk-in friendly early, packed late. Resy for groups. The kind of bar where conversations go deeper than you planned.

name: Oddball
description: New West Village cocktail bar making waves with inventive drinks, a welcoming atmosphere, and a short but excellent food menu. Featured on both the Infatuation and Eater Heatmap lists simultaneously — a rare double. The cocktails are playful but precise. Resy. The newest essential cocktail bar in the Village.

name: Seed Library
description: Botanical-themed West Village cocktail bar with garden-inspired drinks using house-made syrups, tinctures, and fresh herbs. Each drink tastes like a greenhouse distilled into a glass. The Garden Spritz is refreshing. The herbal highball is herbaceous and complex. Resy. The prettiest cocktails in the Village, and they taste even better than they look.

name: Bar Maeda
description: New Williamsburg Japanese cocktail bar with Kyoto-inspired drinks, Japanese whisky flights, and refined bar snacks. The highball is crisp and precise. The whisky selection goes deep into Japanese regions. The izakaya snacks pair perfectly. Resy. The most sophisticated Japanese bar in Brooklyn, with an atmosphere that transports you to a Kyoto side street.

name: The Django
description: Subterranean jazz club inside the Roxy Hotel in Tribeca with nightly live performances and craft cocktails. The room is dark, intimate, and designed for the music. The cocktails are strong. The late-night jam sessions are where the real magic happens — musicians sitting in, improvising, and creating something unrepeatable. Resy. Tribeca's jazz heartbeat.

name: Baby's All Right
description: Williamsburg music venue and bar with indie bands, DJ nights, and a taco kitchen in a converted warehouse. The booking is consistently excellent — catch bands on the rise before they sell out larger rooms. The tacos are surprisingly good for a music venue. The back room is intimate. Walk-in. The most important small music venue in Brooklyn.

name: Skinny Dennis
description: Williamsburg honky-tonk dive bar with nightly live country, bluegrass, and Americana — all free, all genuine. Cheap whiskey, cold beer, and sawdust energy. The vibe is a Texas roadhouse transported to Metropolitan Avenue. No cover, no pretension, and a crowd that actually knows the lyrics. Walk-in. The most authentic live music bar in Brooklyn.

name: The Sultan Room
description: Bushwick music venue with a state-of-the-art sound system, sunken dance floor, and a rooftop that opens for warm-weather events. The programming spans electronic, indie, hip-hop, and everything between. The sound quality is excellent for a room this size. Walk-in. The most versatile music venue in Bushwick.

name: Union Pool
description: Legendary Williamsburg dive bar and music venue with a huge backyard patio, taco truck, fire pit, and free shows. The backyard in summer is one of the best outdoor drinking experiences in Brooklyn. The taco truck is better than it needs to be. The frozen drinks are dangerously easy. Walk-in. The most fun patio bar in Williamsburg.

name: Ornithology Jazz Club
description: Intimate Crown Heights jazz club with nightly sets, creative cocktails, and a warmth that makes every show feel like a private performance. The musicians are excellent — a mix of established players and rising stars. The cocktails are crafted with care. Walk-in. The kind of jazz club that makes you fall in love with live music all over again.

name: Silvana
description: Harlem dual-purpose spot — Middle Eastern restaurant upstairs, free live music venue downstairs with eclectic programming spanning jazz, soul, Afrobeat, and DJ sets. The falafel and shawarma upstairs are solid. The music downstairs is the real draw — free entry, diverse lineups, and a Harlem community energy. Walk-in. Open late.

name: LunÀtico
description: Crown Heights musician-owned restaurant and jazz venue with Mediterranean food, natural wine, and nightly live performances. The food is genuinely good — not just bar snacks — with seasonal small plates and a wine list that goes deep. The jazz is intimate and curated. Walk-in. The most holistic jazz dining experience in Brooklyn.

name: Saint Vitus Bar
description: Greenpoint metal and punk venue with a dark, gritty atmosphere and touring bands that draw devotees from across the city. The sound system is excellent. The booking is underground and genuine. The beer is cold and cheap. Walk-in. For people who like their music loud, their rooms dark, and their scenes authentic.

name: Harlem Nights
description: Harlem jazz lounge with nightly live music, craft cocktails, and a sophisticated atmosphere that celebrates Harlem's deep musical heritage. The drinks are well-crafted. The lineups span traditional jazz, soul, and R&B. The crowd is dressed up and respectful. Walk-in. Jazz in the neighborhood that invented it.

name: Basement
description: This underground Chinatown club is one of NYC grittiest, most no-frills dance spots -- a dark, sweaty basement literally where the music is the point and nothing else matters. It draws a young, adventurous crowd with eclectic DJ bookings spanning techno, house, hip-hop, and everything in between. Do not expect bottle service; do expect to dance until your legs give out.

name: Elsewhere
description: Massive multi-room Bushwick venue with a rooftop, indoor stages, and DJ residencies that bring world-class electronic and live acts to East Williamsburg. The 700-capacity main room has serious sound. The rooftop is a bonus. The programming is consistently excellent. Walk-in. The most important club in Brooklyn.

name: Good Room
description: Greenpoint two-room club with an intimate front bar and a large dance floor in back hosting world-class DJs. The vinyl selection in the front room is curated. The sound system in the back room competes with any club in the city. Walk-in. The most DJ-focused club in Brooklyn — music first, always.

name: Mood Ring
description: Eclectic Bushwick queer bar with themed nights, DJ sets, a plant-filled backyard, and a welcoming energy that makes everyone feel like a regular. The cocktails are creative and affordable. The themed nights range from disco to drag to experimental. Walk-in. The most inclusive bar in Bushwick.

name: Le Dive
description: Williamsburg natural wine bar with a French dive bar aesthetic — cheap natural wines by the glass, tinned fish, and cheese plates in a tiny space with sidewalk seating. The wine changes constantly. The vibe is unpretentious and fun. Walk-in. The best cheap natural wine experience in Brooklyn.

name: Jimmy
description: Perched atop the modernist James Hotel in SoHo, Jimmy is a rooftop pool bar that feels like a Miami import dropped into lower Manhattan. The outdoor pool, skyline views, and retro-chic design make it one of the more photogenic rooftops in the city. It leans more scene-y than serious, but that is exactly the point -- a place to see and be seen.

name: The Roof at The Rockaway Hotel
description: Beachside rooftop at The Rockaway Hotel with ocean views, frozen cocktails, and a relaxed surf-town energy. The fish tacos and oysters are solid. The vibe is the opposite of Manhattan rooftops — barefoot, salty, and genuinely chill. Seasonal. Worth the trek to Rockaway for a sunset that feels like vacation.
address: 108-10 Rockaway Beach Dr, Rockaway Park, NY 11694

name: The Ides at Wythe Hotel
description: The rooftop bar at the Wythe Hotel is Williamsburg original cool-kid rooftop, offering unobstructed views of the Manhattan skyline that never get old. The cocktail program is more thoughtful than your average hotel rooftop, and the indoor-outdoor setup means it is actually a year-round destination. Go on a weekday evening for the full magic without the crowd crush.

name: Harriet's Rooftop
description: Chic rooftop atop 1 Hotel NoMad with Empire State Building views, DJ sets, and tropical cocktails. The cocktails lean creative and fruity. The small plates are elevated bar snacks. The Empire State view is front and center. Resy. The most glamorous rooftop in the NoMad neighborhood.

name: Lobby Bar at The Bowery Hotel
description: Moody, fireplace-lit lobby bar at The Bowery Hotel that's become NYC's ultimate see-and-be-seen lounge. The drinks are strong. The leather sofas are deep. The celebrity sightings are reliable. Walk-in, first-come-first-served, and the wait can be long on weekends. The most coveted non-reservation bar in downtown Manhattan.

name: Welcome to the Johnsons
description: LES dive bar designed to look like a suburban basement rec room — wood paneling, cheap beer, pool table, and a crowd that ranges from hipsters to neighborhood lifers. The $3 PBR is the currency. The vibe is intentionally trashy and completely fun. Cash only. Open until 4 AM. The most committed dive bar concept in the city.

name: 169 Bar
description: Grimy Chinatown dive with insane happy hour prices, a surprisingly good raw bar, and a loyal crowd of downtown regulars who've been drinking here since before the LES was trendy. The cheap oysters are the draw. The ambiance is fluorescent and unapologetic. Cash only. Open until 4 AM. The dive that dives aspire to be.

name: Sunny's Bar
description: Legendary Red Hook waterfront dive in an old longshoreman's hall — sawdust floors, Christmas lights, and live bluegrass on Saturdays that feels like a documentary about Old Brooklyn. Sunny Balzano tended this bar until he passed, and the community kept it alive. The whiskey is cheap. The music is genuine. The soul of Red Hook lives here.

name: Spring Lounge
description: A beloved Nolita dive bar with a neon shark on the wall and a crowd that ranges from neighborhood old-timers to off-duty service industry folks. The drinks are cold and fairly priced, the jukebox is solid, and nobody is trying to impress anyone. The ideal bar for when you just want to be a person drinking a beer.

name: Arlene's Grocery
description: No-cover LES rock venue in a former bodega with nightly live bands and Monday night karaoke that's achieved legendary status. The booking spans punk, rock, indie, and country. The room is tiny and sweaty. The drinks are cheap. Walk-in. Arlene's has launched more careers than most record labels. The most democratic music venue on the Lower East Side.

name: Terra Blues
description: Greenwich Village blues club with nightly live blues, R&B, and soul music in an intimate basement since 1990. The musicians are consistently excellent — some of the best blues players in the city. The room is dark and atmospheric. Walk-in. Cover charge is reasonable. The last real blues club in Greenwich Village.

name: Cafe Wha?
description: Historic Village basement where Hendrix, Dylan, and Springsteen played early gigs — and the room still hosts nightly rock and funk shows with that same raw energy. The history is on every wall. The music is loud and live. Walk-in. Cover charge varies. If these walls could play guitar, they'd be better than you.

name: Nublu
description: East Village jazz and world music club with an eclectic mix of Brazilian, Afrobeat, jazz, and electronic live sets. The programming is global and genuinely diverse. The room is intimate. The crowd is musically curious. Walk-in. Cover varies. One of the most interesting live music spaces downtown for people who want their ears challenged.

name: Apothéke
description: Hidden Chinatown speakeasy on Doyers Street where bartenders in lab coats mix apothecary-themed cocktails behind an unmarked door. The drinks are elaborate and herbal — tinctures, elixirs, and absinthe. The space is moody and mysterious. Walk-in only. The most atmospheric bar in Chinatown and one of the original NYC speakeasies.

name: The Back Room
description: Real Prohibition-era speakeasy on the LES where drinks are served in teacups and the entrance is through an unmarked alley door. This isn't speakeasy cosplay — The Back Room actually operated during Prohibition and hosted the likes of Lucky Luciano. The cocktails are classic. The history is real. Walk-in. The most authentic speakeasy in New York.

name: Bathtub Gin
description: Hidden Chelsea speakeasy entered through the Stone Street Coffee Company with a copper bathtub centerpiece and Prohibition-era cocktails. The drinks are strong and classic. The space is intimate and candlelit. Walk-in. The novelty wears off after the entrance — and then the cocktails keep you there because they're genuinely excellent.

name: Raines Law Room
description: Unmarked Flatiron speakeasy with a buzzer entry, plush velvet couches, and meticulously crafted cocktails in a candlelit parlor. The experience starts with pressing a buzzer and hoping the door opens. Inside, the cocktails are serious and spirit-forward. Resy. The most elegant speakeasy experience in the city — no gimmicks, just gorgeous drinks in a gorgeous room.

name: The Flatiron Room
description: Speakeasy-style whiskey bar with 1,000+ bottles, live jazz nightly, and an intimate Flatiron loft that feels like a private library for whiskey nerds. The flights are curated by region and style. The live jazz sets the mood without overwhelming conversation. Resy. The most civilized way to spend an evening in Flatiron if you love whiskey.

name: Existing Conditions
description: Dave Arnold's boundary-pushing cocktail lab in the Village — World's 50 Best Bars list and a menu that reads like a science paper. Clarified cocktails, centrifuge-spun spirits, and drinks that use techniques most bartenders haven't heard of. The cocktails are innovative and genuinely delicious. Resy. The most intellectually stimulating bar in the world.

name: The Campbell
description: Grand cocktail bar inside Grand Central Terminal in a restored 1920s office with painted ceilings, a massive fireplace, and classic cocktails served with the gravitas of a bygone era. The room is jaw-dropping — one of the most beautiful spaces in Midtown. Walk-in. The champagne cocktail is the right way to start a train trip.

name: King Cole Bar
description: The St. Regis hotel bar where the Bloody Mary was invented in the 1930s. The Maxfield Parrish mural behind the bar is worth the price of a drink alone. The cocktails are impeccable and old-school — this is not a craft cocktail bar, it's a monument to classic drinking. Walk-in. Jackets preferred. The most legendary hotel bar in America.
address: Two E 55th St, New York, NY 10022

name: Decoy
description: Secret underground Peking duck bar hidden beneath RedFarm in the West Village. Ring the doorbell, descend the stairs, and find yourself at an intimate counter where one of NYC's best Peking ducks is carved tableside. The duck buns are pillowy. The scallion pancakes are crispy. Resy. The most delicious secret in the West Village.

name: Ear Inn
description: One of NYC's oldest bars — operating since 1817 in SoHo. The story goes: the B in the neon BAR sign burned out, leaving EAR, and the name stuck. The burger is solid. The whiskey is cheap. The history is unmatched. Walk-in. Cash only. The kind of bar that makes you realize New York has been drinking for over 200 years.

name: Bohemian National Hall
description: Hidden Czech beer hall on the Upper East Side inside a stunning 1896 Beaux-Arts building that most New Yorkers walk right past. Czech beer, schnitzel, and goulash in a gorgeous space. Walk-in. The most architecturally beautiful bar that nobody knows about. If you told someone there was a Czech beer hall in a Beaux-Arts mansion on the UES, they wouldn't believe you.

name: Spritzenhaus33
description: Massive Greenpoint beer garden with 60+ craft beers, communal tables, a huge outdoor space, and a no-phone-at-the-bar policy that forces actual conversation. The bratwurst is snappy. The pretzel is warm and salted. Dog-friendly. Walk-in. Happy hour 4-7 PM. The most social drinking experience in Brooklyn.

name: Barcade
description: The original retro arcade bar with 50+ classic arcade games from the 80s and 90s plus rotating craft beers on tap. Pac-Man, Galaga, Space Invaders — every game costs a quarter. The beer selection is curated and local. Walk-in. The most fun you'll have in Williamsburg without spending more than $20.

name: Clover Club
description: Julie Reiner's elegant Cobble Hill cocktail bar with a Victorian parlor vibe and inventive drinks. The Clover Club cocktail — gin, raspberry, lemon, egg white — is the namesake and the star. The brunch cocktails on weekends are outstanding. Resy. The most sophisticated cocktail bar in South Brooklyn, with a warmth that makes craft cocktails feel welcoming.

name: The Immigrant
description: Tiny candlelit East Village wine bar seating 24 with an expertly curated natural wine list and simple, perfect snacks. The cheese plate is well-chosen. The charcuterie is imported. The wines change constantly. Walk-in. The most intimate wine bar in the East Village — so small that strangers become friends by the second glass.

name: Kashkaval Garden
description: Hidden Hell's Kitchen wine garden tucked behind a cheese shop with fondue, Mediterranean plates, and cheap wine in a secret courtyard. The fondue is rich and shareable. The hummus plate is generous. The wine is affordable by the glass. Walk-in. The coziest hidden garden in Midtown — most locals don't even know it exists.

name: Freemans
description: Hidden at the end of Freeman Alley off Rivington Street, this taxidermy-filled colonial lodge has been serving rustic American fare since 2004. The artichoke dip is legendary. The Devils on Horseback (dates wrapped in bacon) are addictive. The fried chicken is excellent. Resy. The restaurant that proved the LES could do cozy Americana — and made a dead-end alley famous.

name: La Lanterna di Vittorio
description: Cozy Village Italian with a hidden underground jazz bar, fondue, and fireside seating. The fondue is rich and indulgent. The tiramisu is excellent. The espresso is strong. But the real secret is the downstairs jazz room — live music in a candlelit basement that transforms dinner into an experience. Walk-in. The most romantic hidden spot in the Village.

name: Sakagura
description: Hidden subterranean sake bar in a Midtown office building basement with 200+ sakes and excellent izakaya dishes. The entrance is unremarkable — a lobby, an elevator, a basement door — but inside is a warm, wood-lined Japanese bar with one of the deepest sake lists in the country. The yakitori is excellent. Resy. The best-kept secret in Midtown East.
address: 211 E 43rd St B1, New York, NY 10017

name: Tokyo Record Bar
description: Twelve seats. A DJ spinning vinyl. An omakase dinner paired with sake. Tokyo Record Bar is the most unique dining experience in Greenwich Village — a listening bar where the music and the food are curated in harmony. Reservations required. The evening unfolds like a playlist made just for you. Resy. Nothing else like it in New York.

name: Death & Co
awards: James Beard Award, Tales of the Cocktail
description: The bar that launched the modern cocktail renaissance. Death & Co opened in 2006 in the East Village and changed how America drinks. The cocktails are inventive, the bartenders are scholars, and the dimly lit room has hosted more important drinking moments than any bar in the city. James Beard Award. World's Best Bars list. Resy. No standing — every seat is the best seat.

name: Lullaby
description: Secret LES cocktail bar behind an unmarked door with Asian-inspired cocktails, dim sum snacks, and a Shanghai lounge vibe. The drinks are creative and beautifully garnished. The dim sum pairings are playful. The entrance is the adventure — find the door, step inside, and let the bartenders surprise you. Walk-in. The most immersive cocktail experience on the Lower East Side.

name: Patent Pending
description: Nikola Tesla-themed speakeasy inside the Radio Wave Building where Tesla once lived. The cocktails are named after his inventions and experiments — Edison vs. Tesla, The Coil, Alternating Current. The vintage decor is meticulous. Resy. The nerdiest bar in New York, and proudly so. Bring a date who appreciates electromagnetic fields and good gin.

name: Mace
description: Single-ingredient cocktail bar in the East Village where each drink is built around one hero ingredient — cardamom, turmeric, cinnamon, banana, whatever inspires the menu. The concept sounds constraining but the creativity is staggering. World's 50 Best Bars list. Walk-in. The most intellectually creative cocktail bar in the East Village.

name: Darling
description: Lush rooftop garden bar atop the Park South Hotel in Gramercy with string lights, greenery, and craft cocktails. The space is intimate compared to mega-rooftops — perfect for a date that doesn't want to shout over DJ music. The cocktails are well-crafted. The crowd is Gramercy professional. Walk-in. Seasonal. One of the most charming rooftops in Midtown.

name: Panorama Room
description: Sitting atop the Graduate Roosevelt Island hotel, the Panorama Room offers genuinely jaw-dropping 360-degree views that most NYC rooftops can only dream about -- the East Side skyline, the Queensboro Bridge, and both rivers. The tram ride over is half the fun. The cocktails are tropical-leaning and well-executed, making this one of the city most underrated rooftop experiences.

name: Gansevoort Rooftop
description: Meatpacking rooftop at the Gansevoort Hotel with a heated pool, panoramic views, and DJ sets that turn cocktail hour into a full-blown party. The frozen drinks are the move in summer. The crowd is beautiful and knows it. Late-night energy on weekends. Walk-in. The original Meatpacking rooftop that started the trend.

name: Highlight Room
description: Elevated lounge and rooftop at the Moxy SoHo with downtown views, craft cocktails, and a hip crowd that actually lives in the neighborhood. The space is more intimate than the Midtown mega-rooftops. The cocktails are well-made. The energy is downtown cool without the downtown attitude. Walk-in.

name: Electric Lemon
description: 24th-floor bar at Equinox Hotel Hudson Yards with Hudson River views and a wellness-meets-luxury vibe. The cocktails are creative and health-adjacent (don't let that scare you — they're delicious). The space is sleek and modern. The sunset over the Hudson is spectacular. Walk-in. The most grown-up rooftop at Hudson Yards.

name: Berry Park
description: This Williamsburg beer garden and rooftop bar is a chill, no-pretense neighborhood spot that has been a local favorite for years. The rooftop has solid Manhattan skyline views without the velvet-rope nonsense, and the beer selection leans European with German and Belgian brews. Show up in a T-shirt, grab a cold one, and watch the sun set over the city.

name: Broken Shaker
description: Miami import cocktail bar at the Freehand Hotel with tropical drinks, a plant-filled rooftop, and a laid-back party energy. The frozen cocktails are the specialty. The space is lush and green. The crowd is young and fun. Walk-in. The most Miami thing in Gramercy — which is somehow exactly what Gramercy needed.

name: Elsie Rooftop
description: Colorful Midtown rooftop with a retractable glass roof, Empire State Building views, and tropical cocktails served year-round. The frozen drinks are the crowd-pleasers. The Empire State views are front-and-center. Walk-in. The most accessible year-round rooftop in Midtown — the retractable roof means rain doesn't cancel the plans.

name: St. Cloud
description: Rooftop bar atop the Knickerbocker Hotel overlooking Times Square — which sounds terrible but is actually magical. The chaos of Times Square viewed from above with a cocktail in hand transforms from nightmare to spectacle. The drinks are craft-level. Walk-in. The only reason to be in Times Square on purpose.

name: The Crown
description: Rooftop at Hotel 50 Bowery in Chinatown with panoramic downtown views and Asian-inspired cocktails. The space is chic and the crowd is downtown. The lychee cocktail is refreshing. The view south toward the bridges is stunning at night. Walk-in. The best rooftop below Canal Street.

name: Pacha New York
description: The legendary Ibiza nightclub brand takes over the Brooklyn Mirage complex for summer 2026. FIVE Holdings transformed the massive outdoor venue into Pacha New York, promising international headliners, Grammy winners, and the signature Pacha energy. The Great Hall operates year-round while the outdoor space runs June through October. A new era for Brooklyn nightlife.

name: Public Records
description: Gowanus three-room venue with a vinyl lounge, vegan cafe, and custom-built sound system. Public Records takes club culture seriously — the sound is world-class, the vegan food is genuinely good, and the programming spans jazz to techno. The most thoughtful club experience in NYC. Walk-in or tickets depending on the night.

name: 3 Dollar Bill
description: Massive double-venue queer nightlife complex in East Williamsburg with drag shows, DJ nights, live music, and a warehouse dance floor. The energy is inclusive, the performances are spectacular, and the space is enormous. Walk-in or tickets. The most important queer nightlife venue in Brooklyn.

name: The Stranger
description: Secret Midtown club disguised as a newsstand entrance — push through what looks like a bodega and step into themed rooms with burlesque, karaoke, and immersive experiences. The cocktails are theatrical. The concept is wild. Walk-in if you can find it. The most surprising nightlife experience in Midtown.

name: C'mon Everybody
description: Bed-Stuy 1970s-themed lounge with live music, DJ sets, comedy, and community events. The space is warm and intimate. The programming is eclectic. The crowd is neighborhood. Walk-in. The kind of small venue where every genre feels at home and every night is different.

name: Desert 5 Spot
description: Western-themed Williamsburg bar with live country music, a mechanical bull, and frozen margaritas. Yes, there's a mechanical bull in Williamsburg. The live country and honky-tonk dancing are genuine. The margaritas are strong. Walk-in. The wildest theme bar in Brooklyn, and it doesn't take itself too seriously.

name: Irving Plaza
description: Legendary 1,000-capacity Gramercy music venue hosting touring acts since 1978. The room is intimate enough to feel close to the stage but big enough for real shows. The sound is excellent. The booking spans rock, indie, hip-hop, and electronic. Walk-in with ticket. The sweet spot between small club and arena.

name: Webster Hall
description: East Village music landmark since 1886 — beautifully renovated with a 1,500-capacity ballroom, mezzanine, and intimate studio space. The sound system is state-of-the-art. The ornate interior is stunning. Webster Hall has hosted everyone from the Grateful Dead to Billie Eilish. Walk-in with ticket. The most historic music venue in the East Village.

name: Le Poisson Rouge
description: Greenwich Village multimedia venue in the former Village Gate space with eclectic programming — classical one night, electronic the next, comedy the next. The room is intimate and versatile. The bookings are adventurous. Walk-in with ticket. The most unpredictable music venue in the Village, and that's the point.

name: Joe's Pub
description: Intimate cabaret and music venue inside the Public Theater with world-class performances, craft cocktails, and dinner service. The talent level is staggering — Broadway stars, Grammy winners, and emerging artists in a 200-seat room. Resy for dinner shows. One of the most special live music experiences in New York. Every show feels like a private concert.

name: National Sawdust
description: Williamsburg new music venue with world-class acoustics and avant-garde programming spanning chamber music to experimental electronic. The SANAA-designed space is architecturally stunning. The sound is pristine. Walk-in with ticket. For people who want their live music experience to match the artistic ambition of the performers.

name: Dizzy's Club
description: Jazz at Lincoln Center's intimate club on the 5th floor with nightly sets, Central Park views, and a Southern-inspired dinner menu. The late-night sets have no cover and feature emerging artists. The room is elegant. The cocktails are strong. Resy. The most prestigious jazz club in America with the views to match.

name: Zinc Bar
