description: Underground Greenwich Village jazz club with nightly sets spanning jazz, Brazilian, Latin, and Afrobeat. The room is dark and intimate. The musicians are consistently excellent. The cover is reasonable. Walk-in. The most globally diverse jazz programming in the Village — you might hear bossa nova one night and Afrobeat the next.

name: Mezzrow
description: Underground West Village jazz piano bar from the Smalls team with a 60-seat room, craft cocktails, and nightly piano-focused sets. Intimate doesn't begin to describe it — you can hear the pianist breathe. Walk-in. The most refined jazz listening experience in the Village. Perfect for a date that wants to feel cultured.

name: The Stand
description: Gramercy comedy club and restaurant with nightly stand-up and surprise drop-ins from NYC's biggest comedians. The full dinner menu is genuinely good — not just bar food with a show attached. The room is intimate enough that eye contact with the comedian is a real possibility. Walk-in with ticket. The most complete comedy dining experience in the city.

name: New York Comedy Club
description: Gramercy comedy club with nightly shows, two stages, and regular appearances by touring headliners and late-night TV regulars. The ticket prices are fair. The room is energetic. The chance of seeing someone famous trying new material is real. Walk-in with ticket. A solid comedy night without the Comedy Cellar line.

name: Leyenda
description: Latin American cocktail bar in Cobble Hill from Ivy Mix with mezcal, pisco, and rum-forward drinks plus empanadas and arepas. The mezcal negroni is outstanding. The empanadas are crispy and well-filled. The space is colorful and warm. Walk-in. The best Latin cocktail bar in Brooklyn — every drink tells a story from a different country.

name: Weather Up
description: Intimate Tribeca cocktail bar with a copper bar top, serious spirit-forward drinks, and a neighborhood warmth that balances the craft precision. The whiskey sour is expertly made. The bartenders are knowledgeable without being preachy. Walk-in. The kind of cocktail bar where you can geek out or just have a great drink — it works either way.

name: Donna
description: Williamsburg tropical cocktail bar with frozen rum drinks, a backyard patio, and DJ sets. The frozen cocktails are the specialty — creative, boozy, and perfect for summer. The patio is the draw. The energy is casual and fun. Walk-in. The best place to drink something frozen and pretend you're in the Caribbean while staring at a Williamsburg warehouse.

name: BlackTail
description: Havana-inspired cocktail bar in Pier A Harbor House with pre-revolution Cuban aesthetic, rum cocktails, and sweeping harbor views. The daiquiri is the hero — simple, balanced, and made with excellent rum. The Cuban-inspired snacks are a nice touch. Walk-in. The most romantic waterfront bar in lower Manhattan.
address: 301 S End Ave, New York, NY 10280

name: Meow Wolf
description: Immersive art experience where you walk through surreal portals, climb inside sculptures, and interact with a mind-bending alternate universe. Each room is more disorienting and beautiful than the last. The cafe and bar inside are decent. Allow 2-3 hours. Timed tickets. The most otherworldly experience in Brooklyn — not a museum, not a theme park, something entirely new.

name: DUMBO Boulders
description: Brooklyn's premier bouldering gym in DUMBO with world-class climbing walls, yoga classes, and a community cafe. No ropes needed — just shoes, chalk, and courage. The space has Manhattan Bridge views. Day passes available. The perfect active date or rainy-day adventure. All skill levels welcome.

name: Batsu!
description: Japanese game show-inspired interactive comedy in the East Village where audience members compete in hilarious physical challenges while the crowd cheers (and drinks). BYOB. The show is 90 minutes of pure chaos and laughter. Tickets on their website. The most unexpectedly fun night out in the East Village — you'll be sore from laughing.

name: SPIN New York
description: Susan Sarandon's ping pong lounge in Flatiron with 17 Olympic-quality tables, craft cocktails, and a fun date energy that turns a casual idea into a full evening. The cocktails are surprisingly good. The food menu is above average. Reservations help on weekends. Resy. The only place in Manhattan where you can smash a forehand and a martini in the same hour.

name: Royal Palms Shuffleboard Club
description: Massive Gowanus shuffleboard club with 10 courts, tropical drinks, food pop-ups, and a retro-Florida vibe. The drinks are frozen and fruity. The shuffleboard is surprisingly competitive. Groups love it. Dog-friendly. Walk-in. The most fun group outing in Brooklyn that doesn't involve bowling.

name: Marquee New York
description: Legendary Chelsea megaclub from Tao Group, freshly renovated with world-class DJs, bottle service, and one of NYC's most iconic dance floors. The sound system is thunderous. The crowd is dressed to impress. Bottle service is the fast track. Walk-in with ticket. The nightclub that defined Chelsea nightlife and keeps reinventing itself.

name: Marquee Skydeck at Edge
description: Open-air rooftop club experience 100 floors up at Edge Hudson Yards — the highest outdoor nightclub in the Western Hemisphere. DJs, bottle service, and Manhattan glittering below your feet. Seasonal. The vertigo-inducing glass floor adds an edge (pun intended). Tickets on SevenRooms. The most extreme nightlife experience in the city.

name: The Box
description: Notorious LES burlesque and variety theater with wild live performances, acrobats, and a late-night party atmosphere in a converted theater. The shows are provocative, artistic, and unlike anything else in the city. The crowd is dressed up and ready. Walk-in or ticket. The most debauched and artistic night out on the Lower East Side.

name: Knockdown Center
description: Massive converted factory in Queens hosting underground dance parties, art exhibitions, concerts, and immersive events in a raw industrial space. The events range from intimate DJ sets to massive raves. The building itself is dramatic. Walk-in with ticket. The most adventurous event space in Queens.

name: Nebula
description: Ridgewood warehouse club with a powerful sound system and DJ-focused programming spanning techno, house, and experimental electronic. The crowd comes for the music, not the scene. The sound is excellent. The space is raw. Walk-in with ticket. For people who want their clubs to feel like clubs, not restaurants.

name: Avant Gardner
description: Brooklyn mega-venue complex that turned an industrial wasteland into the city most ambitious outdoor and indoor event space. The Great Hall and Kings Hall host world-class DJs and live acts with production values that rival European festivals. If you are into electronic music and do not mind the trek to East Williamsburg, this is where the magic happens.

name: Lavo Party Brunch
description: Saturday day-party brunch at Lavo where lunch turns into a full-blown dance party with DJs, confetti, and bottle service by 3 PM. The brunch menu is Italian and solid. The transition from restaurant to club is seamless and chaotic. Book ahead on Resy. The most fun daytime party in Midtown — business casual enters, dancing leaves.

name: PHD Terrace at Dream Downtown
description: Chelsea rooftop lounge atop the Dream Downtown with penthouse vibes, DJ sets, and Empire State Building views. The cocktails are strong. The crowd is attractive. The energy after 11 PM is full nightclub. Walk-in or table reservations. The most intimate rooftop club experience in Chelsea.

name: Up&Down
description: Two-floor West Village club with an upstairs lounge and a bigger downstairs dance floor. Celebrity sightings are reliable. A-list DJ sets rotate. The cocktails are premium. Walk-in with guest list or ticket. The most exclusive nightclub in the Village — dress accordingly.

name: Cielo
description: Legendary Meatpacking deep house and techno club with a Funktion-One sound system, sunken dance floor, and programming that's remained underground and authentic for years. No bottle service. No VIP culture. Just music. Walk-in with ticket. The last true music-first club in Manhattan.

name: Garage
description: SoHo underground dance venue focused on house, disco, and funk in a stripped-back basement. No bottle service, no velvet rope — just a good sound system and people who came to dance. Walk-in with ticket. The kind of club that New York used to have on every block and now barely has at all.

name: Music Hall of Williamsburg
description: Williamsburg's premier mid-size concert venue with a 550-capacity room from Bowery Presents. The sound is excellent, the sightlines are great from every angle, and the booking consistently features the best indie, rock, and electronic acts touring. Walk-in with ticket. The sweet spot between Brooklyn Bowl and Barclays.

name: Rough Trade NYC
description: Massive indie record store and live music venue in Williamsburg with in-store performances, album releases, and a 250-capacity stage. Browse vinyl, catch a free show, and buy the album on the way out. The coffee is decent. Walk-in. The most complete music retail and performance space in Brooklyn.

name: SOB's
description: Legendary SoHo world music venue — Sounds of Brazil — hosting Latin, Caribbean, Afrobeat, hip-hop, and R&B since 1982. The programming is global and the dance floor is joyful. The dinner menu is Caribbean-inspired. Walk-in with ticket. The most internationally diverse music venue in Manhattan.

name: Beacon Theatre
description: Majestic 2,900-seat UWS concert hall with stunning Art Deco interior and major headliner shows — rock, comedy, spoken word. The Allman Brothers played 238 consecutive shows here. The acoustics are outstanding. The ornate ceiling alone is worth the ticket price. Walk-in with ticket. The most beautiful concert hall in New York.

name: Radio City Music Hall
description: The most iconic entertainment venue in NYC — 6,000 seats of Art Deco grandeur at Rockefeller Center. Home of the Rockettes, host of the biggest concerts and comedy specials in the world. The room is breathtaking. Walk-in with ticket. If you see one show in New York, see it at Radio City.

name: Brooklyn Steel
description: 1,800-capacity East Williamsburg venue from Bowery Presents in a converted steel mill. The sound is outstanding, the sightlines are excellent, and the booking features mid-major touring acts at their peak. Walk-in with ticket. The best large-format concert venue in Brooklyn.

name: Brooklyn Storehouse
description: Massive event and nightlife venue in the Brooklyn Navy Yard hosting DJ nights, live performances, immersive parties, and private events in an industrial warehouse space. The programming is diverse and ambitious. The space is dramatic and raw. Walk-in with ticket. Brooklyn's newest major event space.
address: Navy Yard, Building 293, Assembly Rd, Brooklyn, NY 11205

name: Somewhere Nowhere
description: Multi-level Chelsea venue at the Renaissance Hotel with a rooftop pool lounge, underground basement club, and DJ programming spanning hip-hop, house, and R&B. The rooftop pool in summer is the draw. The basement club after midnight is the payoff. Walk-in with ticket. The most versatile nightlife experience in Chelsea.

name: Superior Ingredients
description: Underground Chinatown dance party and event space hosting some of NYC's best underground DJ nights, raves, and community events in a raw warehouse setting. No frills, no VIP, just music and dancing. Walk-in with ticket. The most authentic underground party space in lower Manhattan.

name: Under the K Bridge Park
description: Outdoor event and community space under the Kosciuszko Bridge in Greenpoint hosting seasonal DJ parties, markets, art installations, and neighborhood gatherings. The setting is industrial and beautiful. The events are community-driven. Walk-in. Brooklyn DIY culture at its best — under a bridge, under the stars.
address: Under the Kosciuszko Bridge, Greenpoint, Brooklyn, NY 11222

name: Ruins
description: Bushwick backyard bar built from salvaged architectural ruins with a magical garden, tiki drinks, and one of Brooklyn's best outdoor spaces. The cocktails are tropical and strong. The garden feels like a secret courtyard from another century. Walk-in. The most beautiful backyard bar in Brooklyn — every corner is a discovery.

name: SummerStage in Central Park
description: NYC's beloved free outdoor concert series in Central Park with major headliners spanning rock, hip-hop, Latin, world music, and dance. Rumsey Playfield holds thousands, and the energy of live music under the trees is unmatched. Free entry for most shows. The best free event in New York City, running every summer since 1986.
address: Rumsey Playfield, Central Park, New York, NY 10021

name: The Vessel at Hudson Yards
description: The honeycomb-like climbable structure at Hudson Yards with 154 interconnecting staircases. Love it or mock it, the experience of climbing through a massive copper vessel is uniquely New York. Free timed tickets. The views from the top are excellent. The photo opportunities are endless. The most Instagrammed structure in the city.

name: Top of the Rock
description: Three-tiered observation deck atop Rockefeller Center with unobstructed views of the Empire State Building and Central Park stretching north. The vantage point is arguably better than the Empire State itself — because you can actually see the Empire State. The sunset timing is worth planning. Book online to skip lines.

name: Empire State Building Observatory
description: The most iconic observation deck in the world — 86th and 102nd floors with 360-degree open-air views that have appeared in a hundred movies. The renovated Art Deco lobby is stunning. The 86th-floor outdoor terrace is the essential experience. Go at sunset or after dark when the city sparkles. Book online for express access.

name: Statue of Liberty & Ellis Island
description: America's most iconic landmark — take the ferry from Battery Park to Liberty Island for crown access (book months ahead), then Ellis Island for the Immigration Museum. The ferry ride alone is worth it. The crown climb is 354 steps and unforgettable. The Ellis Island museum will make you cry. Reserve crown tickets well in advance.

name: One World Observatory
description: Perched atop the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, this observation deck delivers genuinely jaw-dropping 360-degree views from the 100th, 101st, and 102nd floors. The SkyPod elevators alone are worth the price of admission with their immersive time-lapse video walls. Even jaded New Yorkers get a little breathless up here.

name: Chelsea Piers
description: Massive waterfront sports complex with bowling, rock climbing, ice skating, golf driving range, gymnastics, and fitness facilities — all on the Hudson River. The bowling is upscale. The rock climbing wall is serious. The driving range has river views. Chelsea Piers has something for every energy level and every age group.

name: St. George Theatre
description: Restored 1929 Spanish Baroque theater on Staten Island with 2,800 seats hosting major concerts, comedy, and Broadway shows. The architecture is jaw-dropping — ornate ceilings, gilded details, and the kind of grandeur that makes every performance feel important. Worth the ferry ride for the building alone. A hidden architectural gem.

name: Forest Hills Stadium
description: Historic 14,000-seat outdoor stadium in Queens that hosted the US Open until 1977, now reborn as an intimate outdoor concert venue for summer shows. The setting — a tennis stadium surrounded by Forest Hills Tudor homes — is surreal and beautiful. The sound is excellent. Summer concert season brings major acts to Queens.

name: Coney Island
description: NYC's legendary beachfront amusement district with Luna Park rides, the 1927 Cyclone roller coaster, Nathan's hot dogs, the boardwalk, and the New York Aquarium. The Cyclone is a wooden coaster that's survived a century of Brooklyn weather and still thrills. Nathan's on the boardwalk is mandatory. The Mermaid Parade in June is gloriously weird.

name: Fotografiska New York
description: Stockholm photography museum's NYC outpost in a stunning Gramercy townhouse with rotating exhibitions, a rooftop bar, and a plant-based restaurant by Veronica. The exhibitions are world-class. The rooftop views are excellent. The restaurant is genuinely good. Open late. The most elegant museum-bar-restaurant combination in the city.
address: 281 Park Ave S, New York, NY 10010

name: Artechouse NYC
description: Immersive digital art experience in Chelsea with large-scale projections, interactive installations, and an XR cocktail bar where drinks come to life through augmented reality. The exhibitions rotate seasonally. The cocktail bar is the highlight — point your phone at your drink and watch it transform. Timed tickets. The most technologically creative bar in NYC.

name: Color Factory NYC
description: Interactive art experience in SoHo with colorful installations, ball pits, and rooms designed for social media content. The spaces are vibrant and fun. The ball pit is genuinely joyful regardless of age. Timed tickets. Best for groups who want photos and laughs. Not high art — but high energy and unabashedly fun.

name: The Pool
description: Major Food Group's stunning seafood restaurant in the Seagram Building with a centerpiece pool and seasonal fish. The Dover sole is buttery, the lobster is pristine, and the room is one of the most glamorous in Midtown. A gentler sibling to The Grill next door. Resy. The most beautiful seafood restaurant in Manhattan.

name: Quality Italian
description: Quality Branded's theatrical Midtown Italian near Columbus Circle with a giant chicken parm for two, shareable plates, and a lively scene. The chicken parm is the showpiece — enormous and satisfying. The tuna tartare tower is a visual spectacle. Walk-in for the bar. The most entertaining Italian restaurant near Central Park.

name: Zona de Cuba
description: A rooftop escape in the heart of SoHo that channels Old Havana with tropical cocktails, Latin beats, and a scene that gets progressively livelier as the night goes on. The mojitos are legit and the Cuban-inspired bites are solid enough to soak them up. The kind of place where you go for one drink and suddenly it is midnight.

name: The Heights Bar & Grill
description: A neighborhood rooftop bar on the Upper West Side that gives you solid pub grub with surprisingly good views without the Midtown markup. Where the UWS locals go when they want burgers and beers with a breeze and do not feel like dealing with the madness downtown. Unpretentious, reliable, and a legit neighborhood gem.

name: Cantina Rooftop
description: Hell's Kitchen rooftop Mexican with frozen margaritas, tacos, and Times Square-adjacent skyline views. The frozen margs are the star — strong, slushy, and perfect for summer. The guacamole is fresh. Happy hour 4-7 PM daily makes it one of the better deals near the Theater District. Walk-in. Pre-show margaritas on a rooftop.

name: Rooftop Reds
description: The only rooftop vineyard in NYC — a working winery on the Brooklyn Navy Yard roof with wine tastings, skyline views, and seasonal events. They actually grow grapes and make wine on a Brooklyn rooftop. The tastings are fun and educational. The views are industrial-beautiful. Dog-friendly. Seasonal. The most unique drinking experience in Brooklyn.
address: Brooklyn Navy Yard, Building 275, Brooklyn, NY 11205

name: Mr. Purple
description: Rooftop bar and pool at Hotel Indigo LES with downtown skyline views, DJ sets, and a hip crowd. The pool is seasonal but the views are year-round. One of the best rooftops downtown for people who actually live downtown.

name: Refinery Rooftop
description: Retractable glass-roofed bar atop the Refinery Hotel in the Garment District with Empire State views and year-round cocktails. The glass roof means weather doesn't matter. The views are excellent. The cocktails are solid. Walk-in. The most weather-proof rooftop in Midtown.

name: Roof at Park South
description: Intimate NoMad rooftop with string lights, greenery, and craft cocktails at the Park South Hotel. Small and charming compared to the mega-rooftops — perfect for a date or a quiet drink without the scene-y pressure.

name: Azul on the Rooftop
description: Colorful Mexican rooftop at the Hotel Hugo with frozen margaritas, street tacos, and a festive party atmosphere. The margaritas are the draw — frozen, strong, and served in a sun-drenched space. The tacos are solid accompaniments. Walk-in. The most colorful rooftop in lower Manhattan.

name: The Roof at PUBLIC
description: Ian Schrager's rooftop at the PUBLIC hotel on the LES with panoramic downtown views and a hip crowd. The cocktails are crafted. The DJ sets on weekends add energy. The views of the Manhattan and Brooklyn bridges are stunning. Walk-in. The coolest rooftop on the Lower East Side.

name: Vida Verde
description: Mexican rooftop bar in NoMad with mezcal cocktails, tacos al pastor, and a lively after-work crowd. The frozen margaritas hit different on a warm evening. Happy hour 4-6 PM weekdays makes it one of the best deals in the neighborhood.

name: Salon de Ning
description: Sophisticated rooftop terrace at The Peninsula hotel with two outdoor terraces, panoramic 5th Ave views, and refined Asian-inspired cocktails. The space is elegant and intimate. The views are some of the best in Midtown. Walk-in. Hotel bar energy at its most refined — jackets recommended, views guaranteed.

name: Castell Rooftop Lounge
description: AC Hotel rooftop near Times Square with a retractable glass roof and year-round service. The European lounge vibe is a nice contrast to the Times Square chaos below. The cocktails and tapas are solid. Walk-in. A calm oasis above the most chaotic neighborhood in the city.

name: Circle Line Sightseeing Cruises
description: NYC's classic harbor cruise since 1945 — full island circumnavigation, sunset cruises, and holiday specials with Statue of Liberty and skyline views from the water. The full-island cruise (2.5 hours) is the most comprehensive way to see NYC from the water. Sunset cruises are the most romantic. Book online. The original NYC boat experience.
address: Pier 83, W 42nd St, New York, NY 10036
hours: Cruises daily, times vary by season

name: Classic Harbor Line
description: Elegant yacht cruises on vintage 1920s-style schooners with wine, cheese, and craft cocktails. The most romantic way to see the NYC skyline — sailing past the Statue of Liberty on a wooden boat with a glass of rose. Sunset sails sell out. Book ahead. The classiest way to experience the harbor.
address: 62 Chelsea Piers Pier 62, New York, NY 10011

name: Manhattan by Sail
description: Sail the harbor on a historic 1885 schooner with BYOB sunset sails, craft beer sails, and daytime Statue of Liberty cruises from Pier 6 in Brooklyn Bridge Park. The BYOB sunset sail is the best deal — bring your own wine and watch the skyline turn gold. Book online. The most affordable sailing experience in the harbor.
address: Pier 6, Brooklyn Bridge Park, Brooklyn, NY 11201

name: NYC Ferry
description: Forget the subway -- NYC Ferry lets you cruise between Manhattan, Brooklyn, Queens, and the Bronx on the water for the price of a MetroCard swipe. The outdoor decks offer unbeatable skyline views, and routes like the Soundview and South Brooklyn lines are practically scenic tours. One of the most underrated ways to experience the city.
address: Multiple stops (Wall St, DUMBO, Williamsburg, Astoria, etc)

name: Bateaux New York Dinner Cruise
description: A glass-enclosed luxury dinner cruise that floats you around Manhattan with surprisingly competent prix-fixe dining and live music. Unabashedly romantic and touristy, but the all-glass vessel means the views are spectacular from every seat. Perfect for anniversaries, proposals, or impressing out-of-towners who want that cinematic NYC experience.
address: Chelsea Piers, Pier 61, New York, NY 10011

name: Hornblower Cruises
description: Harbor cruises from Pier 15 with brunch, lunch, dinner, and cocktail options. Live DJs on evening cruises. Statue of Liberty views from every deck. The brunch cruise is the best value. The cocktail cruise is the most fun. Book online. Multiple departure times. The most versatile cruise operator in the harbor.
address: Pier 15, 78 South St, New York, NY 10038

name: Pilot Brooklyn
description: Floating oyster bar on a vintage 1924 pilot boat docked in Brooklyn Bridge Park. Craft cocktails, fresh oysters, and the Manhattan skyline from the deck of a wooden boat. Seasonal and magical — the rocking of the boat, the salt air, the bridge above. Walk-in. The most unique bar in Brooklyn.
address: Pier 6, Brooklyn Bridge Park, Brooklyn, NY 11201

name: Grand Banks
description: Oyster bar and cocktail lounge on a 1942 wooden schooner at Pier 25 in Tribeca. The oysters are pristine. The rose flows freely. The sunset from the deck of a wooden boat on the Hudson is one of the most romantic experiences in NYC. Seasonal May through October. Walk-in. The most beautiful bar in lower Manhattan.
address: Park, Pier 25 Hudson River, New York, NY 10013

name: Manhattan Kayak + SUP
description: Kayak and paddleboard rentals on the Hudson River at Pier 84. Paddle past the Intrepid with the Midtown skyline as your backdrop. Sunset paddles are the most dramatic. No experience needed. Seasonal. The most active way to experience the Manhattan waterfront.

name: Free Kayaking at Pier 26
description: FREE walk-up kayaking on the Hudson at Pier 26 — no experience needed, life jackets provided, 20-minute sessions. Operated by the Downtown Boathouse, a volunteer organization that believes the waterfront should be for everyone. Saturday and Sunday mornings plus select weekdays. The best free activity in New York, period.
address: Hudson River Greenway, New York, NY 10013

name: Yankee Stadium
description: Home of the New York Yankees since 2009 — 54,000 seats, Monument Park, and the most storied franchise in sports history. The garlic fries are essential. The Lobel's steak sandwich is the premium order. Take the 4 train to 161st Street. The roar of the Stadium on a playoff night is one of the great sounds in American sports.
hours: Game days, gates open 2hrs before first pitch

name: Taqueria Tlaxcalli
description: Beloved Bronx taqueria with handmade tortillas, tlacoyos, and authentic Pueblan cuisine in a no-frills storefront. The tortillas are hand-pressed and warm. The tlacoyos are stuffed with beans and properly charred. The cemitas are enormous. Cash only. The kind of spot that makes the Bronx one of the most exciting food boroughs.

name: Feeding Tree
description: Bronx Jamaican institution with jerk chicken, oxtail, and curry goat that draws crowds from across the city. The jerk chicken is smoky and properly spiced. The oxtail is braised until falling apart. The curry goat is rich and fragrant. Cash only. The portions are enormous. One of the most beloved Caribbean restaurants in the Bronx.

name: Bronx Night Market
description: Seasonal outdoor food market at Fordham Plaza with 30+ vendors celebrating the Bronx's incredible cultural diversity — Dominican, Albanian, Ghanaian, Mexican, and more. Live music, community energy, and food that represents the real Bronx. Select Saturdays 4-9 PM. The most authentic food market outside of Manhattan or Brooklyn.

name: Mike's Deli at Arthur Avenue
description: The undisputed king of Arthur Avenue, the Bronx real Little Italy that makes Manhattan tourist-trap version look embarrassing. The Migliucci family has been slinging heroic Italian sandwiches and imported goods for decades. The combo heroes are piled absurdly high with top-tier imported meats and cheeses -- this is destination-worthy eating.

name: Casa Della Mozzarella
description: Arthur Avenue cheese shop making fresh mozzarella by hand daily since 1989. Watch them pull it in the window, then eat it warm — still stretchy, still milky, still the best mozzarella in the Bronx. The burrata is equally extraordinary. Cash only. The most delicious 30-second show on Arthur Avenue.

name: Full Moon Pizzeria
description: Arthur Avenue coal-fired pizzeria making crispy, charred Neapolitan pies in the heart of the Bronx's real Little Italy. The margherita is beautifully blistered. The white pie is garlicky and good. Walk-in. Cash friendly. The pizza that Arthur Avenue regulars eat when they don't want to share their secret with Manhattan tourists.

name: Havana Cafe
description: Lively Bronx Cuban with live Latin music on weekends, mojitos, ropa vieja, and a festive atmosphere. The ropa vieja is tender and flavorful. The mojitos are strong and minty. The fried plantains are perfectly sweet and crispy. The weekend live music transforms dinner into a party. Walk-in. The most festive restaurant in the East Bronx.

name: Liebman's Deli
description: One of the last old-school Jewish delis in NYC, serving pastrami, corned beef, and matzo ball soup in Riverdale since 1953. The pastrami is hand-carved and piled high. The matzo ball soup is grandma-approved. The rye bread is fresh. Closed Saturdays. Cash only. A vanishing breed of deli that makes every visit feel like preserving history.

name: Madison Square Garden
description: The world's most famous arena — home of the Knicks, Rangers, and the biggest concerts in the world. The energy of 20,000 people at a sold-out Knicks game is unlike anything in sports. Penn Station is directly below for easy access. Walk-in with ticket. Madison Square Garden isn't just a venue — it's where New York goes to feel something together.

name: Barclays Center
description: Brooklyn's premier arena at Atlantic Terminal — home of the Nets and Liberty with major concerts, boxing, and events. The location at the intersection of nine subway lines makes it the most accessible arena in the city. The concessions are above average for an arena. Walk-in with ticket. Brooklyn's answer to MSG.

name: Lombardi's Pizza
description: America's first pizzeria, opened in 1905 on Spring Street. Coal-fired oven, thin crust, and a claim to history that no other pizza shop in the country can match. The original Margherita is still the move -- simple, charred, and perfect. Tourist-heavy but legitimately good.

name: 2nd Ave Deli
description: A New York Jewish deli institution that survived a move from its original Second Avenue location and came back stronger than ever. The pastrami is hand-carved, piled sky-high, and absolutely competes with Katz for the best in the city -- without the insane tourist lines. The chopped liver is arguably the gold standard.

name: Tom's Restaurant
description: The diner from Seinfeld's exterior shots -- Suzanne Vega wrote Tom's Diner about it. The food is standard greasy-spoon fare, but the history and Columbia University neighborhood energy make it a genuine NYC landmark. Breakfast is the move.

name: Denino's Pizzeria
description: Staten Island pizza legend since 1937. Thin crust tavern-style pies with a crispy edge and sauce that punches above everything in Manhattan. Worth the ferry ride. The clam pie rivals Pepe's in New Haven -- yes, that is a bold claim.
address: 524 Port Richmond Ave, Staten Island, NY 10302

name: Hwaro
description: Chef Sungchul Shim's most intimate project — a 22-seat marble counter inside his Times Square steakhouse Gui. 13-course Korean tasting menu built around a custom charcoal braiser. Foie gras with duck terrine, scallops with truffle makgeolli bearnaise. At $295, this is serious Korean fine dining.

name: Double Knot
description: Philly's beloved Double Knot arrives at Rockefeller Center in a sprawling 12,000-sq-ft, bi-level space. Tuna tartare, robatayaki skewers, sushi, and sashimi with fishbowl views and private nooks. Michael Schulson's biggest NYC bet.

name: Kjun
description: Korean Cajun restaurant that just expanded into a larger Murray Hill two-floor space. Chef Jae Jung's festive cooking — kimchi (try the okra and green tomato), sweet-savory blue crab curry, and galbi and grits. The most joyful collision of Korean and Southern cooking in NYC.

name: Ambassadors Clubhouse
description: London's Sethi siblings bring their celebrated Indian restaurant group to NoMad in a stunning 8,000-sq-ft space modeled after their grandfather's summer house. Papads, kebabs, biryani, and karahi dishes under executive chef Karan Mittal. The most anticipated Indian opening in NYC in years.

name: Seahorse
description: Splashy seafood brasserie from Mercer Street Hospitality inside the revamped W Union Square. Rockwell Group design with scallop tiles and midnight-blue banquettes. Crudo, Dover sole, spicy lobster cavatelli, and a tableside dessert cart. Far better than a hotel restaurant has any right to be.
address: 201 Park Ave S, New York, NY 10003

name: Rulin
description: Union Square expansion from Brooklyn's Noodle Lane team, specializing in pan-Chinese hand-pulled noodles. Lanzhou-style beef noodle soup, dan dan noodles, grilled skewers, and smashed cucumbers. Simple, soulful, and exactly what Union Square needed.

name: The Eighty Six
description: A refined West Village steakhouse that combines classic chophouse vibes with a modern sensibility and genuinely excellent dry-aged beef. The room is dark, handsome, and exactly what you want from a New York steakhouse without the corporate expense-account stuffiness. A serious neighborhood contender in a city drowning in steak options.

name: Golden Steer
description: Legendary Vegas steakhouse's NYC debut in the One Fifth building. Old-school glam meets Greenwich Village with premium steaks, raw bar, bloody mary oyster shooters, and cherries jubilee lit tableside. The kind of restaurant where you dress up and feel like you're in a Scorsese film.

name: Wild Cherry
description: A buzzy West Village newcomer serving creative New American plates in an intimate, moody setting that feels like a dinner party at your coolest friend apartment. The seasonal menu changes frequently, but everything is thoughtfully composed with bold flavors and beautiful plating. The wine list is genuinely interesting.

name: Or'esh
description: A gorgeous SoHo Mediterranean spot that treats seasonal vegetables and Middle Eastern flavors with fine-dining finesse. The wood-fired dishes are the stars, and the hummus alone is worth the trip -- silky, warm, and absolutely perfect. The whole roasted cauliflower is one of the most Instagrammed dishes in the neighborhood.

name: Odo East Village
description: The casual sibling to 2-Michelin-starred Odo, offering what they call 'kaiseki izakaya' — an a la carte menu of Japanese raw, grilled, simmered, steamed, and fried dishes at an elegant counter. Uni donburi, rice-cracker fried chicken, and Japanese crab. Fine dining technique at izakaya prices.

name: Bufón
description: Swanky LES follow-up to West Village wine bar Demo. Steak, seafood, and a raw bar with bluefin tuna carpaccio, grilled mackerel, and a robust natural wine selection from Jacob Nass. The kind of place where you order another bottle and stay until midnight.

name: Bistrot Ha
description: A sultry Lower East Side gem where French bistro classics collide with Vietnamese flavors in ways that feel completely natural and deeply delicious. The pho French onion soup is the kind of genius mashup that makes you wonder why nobody did it sooner. The room is intimate and atmospheric, and the cocktail program punches well above its weight.

name: Blue Bottle Coffee
description: Oakland import that helped define third-wave coffee in NYC. The single-origin pour-overs are clean and bright, the New Orleans iced coffee is sweet and addictive, and every location is architecturally beautiful. Multiple spots across the city. The Williamsburg flagship feels like a Japanese teahouse.
address: 76 N 4th St Store A, Brooklyn, NY 11249

name: Stumptown Coffee Roasters
description: Portland coffee legend with a Ace Hotel lobby location that became the de facto living room for downtown creatives. The hair bender espresso blend is the gateway drug. The cold brew on nitro is creamy and dangerous. The Greenwich Village location has the best atmosphere.

name: Birch Coffee
description: NYC-born coffee roaster with a neighborhood cafe warmth that the bigger chains can't replicate. The latte art is consistently beautiful, the pastries are fresh, and the baristas remember your name. Multiple Manhattan locations. The Flatiron shop has the most regulars.

name: Milk Bar
description: Christina Tosi's dessert empire that turned cereal milk soft serve and compost cookies into a national obsession. The Birthday Cake Truffles are absurdly good. The Crack Pie lives up to its name. Multiple locations, but the East Village flagship is where the magic started. Everything tastes like a nostalgic sugar bomb engineered by a genius.

name: Magnolia Bakery
description: The Sex and the City cupcake shop that launched a billion Instagram posts. The banana pudding is actually the star — creamy, vanilla-wafer-studded, and more addictive than any cupcake. The West Village original on Bleecker has the most charm. Multiple locations. The red velvet cupcake is the safe bet.

name: Ample Hills Creamery
description: Brooklyn-born ice cream with flavors that read like a stoner's wish list and taste like a dream. The Ooey Gooey Butter Cake is the signature — rich, buttery, and addictive. The Salted Crack Caramel is the cult favorite. Multiple locations. The Prospect Heights original is the coziest. Worth the line.

name: Lady M
description: Japanese-French patisserie famous for the Mille Crepe — 20 paper-thin crepes layered with pastry cream into a cake that defies physics. The green tea version is equally stunning. Each slice is $10 and worth every penny. The Midtown location is elegant and quiet. The signature cake box makes a perfect gift.

name: OddFellows Ice Cream
description: Brooklyn ice cream shop with flavors that walk the line between creative and insane — miso cherry, olive oil ash, cornbread — and somehow every single one works. The sundaes are architectural. The seasonal specials are worth following on Instagram. Multiple locations. The Williamsburg shop is the original.

name: Sugar Hill Creamery
description: Harlem's own Black-owned ice cream shop with flavors rooted in the neighborhood — Harlem Shake (peanut butter cup), sweet potato, and a rotating lineup inspired by the African diaspora. The waffles are excellent. The community energy is genuine. The kind of shop that makes Harlem feel like home.
address: 184 Malcolm X Blvd, New York, NY 10026

name: Williamsburg Pizza
description: The late-night Williamsburg slice shop that has saved a thousand stumbling bar-hoppers. Thin crust, proper char, and a cheese pull that photographs well at 2 AM. The grandma slice is the sleeper. Multiple locations across Brooklyn. Open late, which is the entire point.

name: Best Pizza
description: Frank Pinello's Williamsburg slice shop serves one of the most honest plain slices in Brooklyn — thin, crispy, and properly oily. The white slice with ricotta is the insider order. Featured on Vice's The Pizza Show, which makes sense because it is exactly the kind of pizza a media company would worship.

name: NY Pizza Suprema
description: Directly across from Penn Station and widely considered the best slice near MSG. The plain slice has the kind of chew and char that tourists expect from NYC pizza and rarely get this close to a transit hub. The upside-down Sicilian is excellent. Pre-game essential for Knicks and Rangers fans.

name: Corner Slice
description: Detroit-style square slices with thick, crispy edges and cheese that caramelizes against the pan. The pepperoni square is the move — those little cups of grease are a feature, not a bug. Walk-in, late-night hours, and the kind of pizza that makes you question why anyone eats thin crust.

name: Skylark
description: Hidden Midtown rooftop on the 30th floor of a Garment District building with unobstructed Empire State Building views and a sophisticated cocktail program. Less scene-y than most Midtown rooftops, which is its superpower. The cocktails are well-crafted. The view at sunset is extraordinary. Walk-in.

name: The Press Lounge
description: The rooftop at the Ink48 hotel in Hell's Kitchen with sweeping Hudson River views and one of the most photogenic infinity edges in the city. The cocktails are excellent. The sunset over the Hudson is spectacular. Less crowded than the Meatpacking rooftops. Walk-in. The most underrated rooftop in Midtown West.

name: Variety Coffee Roasters
description: Brooklyn-born roaster with a no-nonsense approach to specialty coffee. The espresso is bold, the drip is clean, and the shops are designed for people who want to drink coffee, not perform it. Multiple locations across Brooklyn and Manhattan. The Williamsburg original is the flagship.

name: Think Coffee
description: Fair-trade focused coffee chain born in the Village with a social mission and genuinely good coffee. The cold brew is smooth, the pastries are fresh, and every cup supports sustainable farming. Multiple Manhattan locations. Great study/work spot with free wifi. The Mercer Street location is the original.

name: Toby's Estate Coffee
description: Australian coffee culture meets Brooklyn industrial chic. The flat white is one of the best in the city — velvety, perfectly textured, and served by baristas who actually know what a proper flat white tastes like. The pastries are sourced and solid. The Williamsburg space is huge and airy.

name: Dailies
description: Tiny Williamsburg coffee shop with some of the most carefully pulled espresso in north Brooklyn. The baristas are serious about extraction without being pretentious about it. The cortado is the standard order. The space is minimal — a few seats, a counter, and nothing to distract from the coffee.

name: Felix Roasting Co.
description: The most Instagrammable coffee shop in NYC — a gorgeous SoHo space with hand-painted tile floors, Moroccan-inspired design, and lattes topped with intricate foam art. The oat milk latte is the go-to. The space photographs like a dream. Multiple locations, but the Lafayette Street original is the most beautiful.

name: Blank Street Coffee
description: The VC-funded micro-cafe chain that conquered NYC with tiny storefronts and $3 lattes. Love it or resent it, the matcha latte is solid and the prices are significantly cheaper than the competition. 40+ locations and counting. The business model is fascinating. The coffee is surprisingly decent for the price.

name: Remi Flower & Coffee
description: Half flower shop, half coffee bar — the most charming combination in the West Village. The lattes are genuinely good, the flower arrangements are stunning, and the tiny space smells incredible. The perfect pre-date stop: buy flowers and a coffee in one move. Instagram catnip.

name: Devocion
description: The greenhouse-like Williamsburg roastery is one of the most beautiful coffee spaces in the world. Floor-to-ceiling plants, natural light, and single-origin Colombian beans roasted within weeks of harvest. The cortado is excellent, the cold brew is exceptional, and the space alone is worth the trip.

name: Ground Support
description: SoHo corner coffee shop that's been quietly excellent for over a decade while flashier spots come and go. The drip coffee is well-sourced, the sandwiches at lunch are above average, and the regulars are fiercely loyal. No gimmicks, no Instagram bait — just really good coffee in a neighborhood that charges $7 for bad lattes.

name: Insomnia Cookies
description: Late-night warm cookie delivery that has saved a thousand midnight cravings. The chocolate chunk is the standard, the snickerdoodle is the sleeper, and the cookie cake is for celebrations. Open until 3 AM. Delivery is the move, but walking in at midnight for a warm cookie is its own kind of therapy.

name: Bibble & Sip
description: Midtown dessert cafe famous for cream puffs in flavors like matcha, Earl Grey, and strawberry. The Earl Grey cream puff is the signature — crispy shell, creamy filling, and a flavor that hits every note. The space is tiny and always packed. The coffee is also good, which is a bonus at a pastry shop.

name: Davey's Ice Cream
description: East Village ice cream shop with nostalgic flavors done right — the vanilla burnt sugar is the signature, the Brooklyn blackout is rich and dark, and the strong coffee is dangerously caffeinated. The cones are dipped in chocolate. The space is old-school ice cream parlor. Cash friendly.

name: Chip City
description: NYC-born cookie shop with thick, gooey cookies that went viral for good reason. The chocolate chip is obscenely buttery, the cookies and cream is loaded with Oreo chunks, and the seasonal flavors rotate constantly. Multiple locations. The cookies are baked fresh throughout the day — time your visit for a warm batch.

name: Ferrara Bakery
description: Little Italy's oldest bakery since 1892 with cannoli, sfogliatelle, and Italian cookies that taste like a direct flight to Naples. The cannoli are filled to order — crispy shell, sweet ricotta — and the espresso is strong enough to restart your heart. The cafe upstairs is a time capsule. Over 130 years of sugar.

name: Mochidoki
description: Mochi ice cream shop with flavors that span traditional Japanese (matcha, black sesame) to NYC creative (cookie butter, birthday cake). Each ball is perfectly formed — thin mochi shell, creamy ice cream inside. The tasting flights let you try six flavors. The Village location is cute and colorful.

name: Cafe Panna
description: The gelato shop that serious dessert people whisper about. Hallie Meyer makes small-batch panna cotta and gelato in a tiny Gramercy storefront with flavors that rotate daily and taste like pure concentrated joy. The olive oil gelato is extraordinary. The seasonal fruit panna cotta is why regulars check Instagram before visiting. One of the most personal dessert shops in the city.

name: Ichiran Ramen
description: The Tokyo ramen chain that went mega-viral on TikTok for its solo dining booths — individual cubicles where you eat alone, separated from other diners by wooden partitions. The tonkotsu broth is rich and customizable by spice, richness, and noodle firmness. The experience is as much the point as the ramen. Multiple NYC locations.

name: Sushi by Bou
description: The omakase that went viral for being hidden inside a Bodegas Negras taco joint — and later expanded to speakeasy-style counters across the city. Twelve pieces of nigiri in 30 minutes for around $150. It is not the most refined omakase in the city, but the concept, the speed, and the TikTok content it generates are undeniable. Multiple locations.

name: T Swirl Crepe
description: Japanese crepe shop that conquered NYC TikTok with its cone-shaped crepes filled with ice cream, Nutella, matcha cream, and fresh fruit. The crepe is thin and crispy. The fillings are generous. The photos are inevitable. Multiple locations — the East Village original has the best vibes.
address: 19 St Marks Pl, New York, NY 10003

name: Tiger Sugar
description: The Taiwanese boba brand that started the brown sugar tiger stripe trend — thick brown sugar syrup streaked against the glass with creamy milk and chewy tapioca pearls. Every cup photographs like art. The original brown sugar boba milk is the essential order. Multiple locations. The Chinatown shop has the most foot traffic.

name: Baekjeong
description: The Korean BBQ spot that TikTok food influencers put on the map. Thick cuts of prime galbi grilled tableside, corn cheese bubbling in a cast iron, and soju flowing freely. The brisket is outstanding. Multiple locations, but the Koreatown flagship is the scene. Go with a group — KBBQ is a communal experience.

name: OLIO e PIU
description: West Village Italian with a gorgeous heated patio that food Instagrammers cannot stop photographing. The truffle pasta went viral for a reason — rich, aromatic, and photogenic. The burrata is creamy and perfect. The weekend brunch is one of the most popular in the Village. The patio seating is the prize — request it specifically.

name: Bar Pitti
description: The Greenwich Village Italian sidewalk cafe where every fashion person in New York eats during Fashion Week. No reservations, cash only, and a sidewalk patio that is one of the most coveted people-watching spots in the city. The pastas are simple and excellent. The vibe is effortlessly European. Celebrities eat here regularly and nobody makes a fuss.

name: Sant Ambroeus
description: Milanese cafe and restaurant that is the unofficial canteen of the fashion industry. The cappuccino is perfect. The vitello tonnato is a classic. The gelato is made in-house. Every location is gorgeous — marble counters, leather banquettes, and a clientele that looks like it stepped out of Italian Vogue. Multiple NYC locations.

name: Fish Cheeks
description: Thai seafood restaurant in NoHo that Instagram food accounts adore. The whole fried fish is the showstopper — crispy, spicy, and served on a dramatic platter. The crab fried rice is loaded with lump crab. The green curry mussels are rich and coconutty. The space is tiny, which makes getting in feel like an accomplishment.

name: The Butcher's Daughter
description: The plant-based cafe that made vegetable juices and avocado toast feel like a lifestyle brand before that was a thing. The acai bowls are gorgeous, the kale Caesar is legitimately good, and the brunch scene on weekend mornings is pure wellness-Instagram energy. Multiple locations. The Nolita original is the most charming.

name: Claw Daddy
description: Cajun seafood boil spot on the LES that blew up on TikTok for massive bags of garlic butter crab legs, shrimp, and corn served on brown paper. The garlic butter sauce is obscenely good. The bib is mandatory. Go with friends who do not care about getting messy. Open late. The most fun seafood experience downtown.

name: Jongro BBQ
description: Midtown premier Korean BBQ destination where you grill premium meats over real charcoal at your table, giving everything that unmistakable smoky depth. Open until the wee hours, it has become the go-to for post-work K-BBQ sessions and late-night meat feasts. The banchan spread is generous and the galbi is absolutely top-tier.

name: Naya
description: Lebanese fast-casual that Midtown office workers swear by — fresh pita, crispy falafel, creamy hummus, and shawarma that puts most food carts to shame. The platters are generous and under $15. Multiple Midtown locations. Halal. The kind of quick lunch that makes you feel good about what you ate.

name: Faicco's Italian Specialties
description: West Village Italian pork store and sandwich shop since 1900. The mozzarella is pulled fresh daily, the Italian combo hero is stacked with house-cured meats, and the rice balls are crispy spheres of heaven. Cash only, tiny space, and a line that wraps around Bleecker Street on Saturdays. The most authentic Italian deli left in Manhattan.

name: Defonte's of Brooklyn
description: Red Hook sandwich institution since 1922 — three generations making massive Italian heroes with house-roasted meats, fresh mozzarella, and bread that could double as a weapon. The roast beef with mozzarella and peppers is the power order. The potatoes and egg hero is the blue-collar classic. Cash only. Closed weekends.

name: Sal Kris & Charlie's Deli
description: Old-school Midtown Italian deli making heroes the way your grandfather remembers them — fresh mozzarella, prosciutto, roasted peppers, and olive oil on crusty bread. The construction workers and theater techs who pack this tiny space at lunch know something tourists on 8th Avenue never will. Cash preferred.

name: Lioni Italian Heroes
description: Bensonhurst Italian hero shop that ships nationwide because people who move away from Brooklyn literally cannot live without these sandwiches. The house-made mozzarella is the foundation — milky, fresh, and applied with zero restraint. The Lioni Special with prosciutto and roasted peppers is legendary. Worth the trek to Bensonhurst.

name: Pisillo Italian Panini
description: Tiny FiDi Italian sandwich shop making panini with imported Italian meats, fresh mozzarella, and ingredients sourced directly from Italy. The Godfather — prosciutto, sopressata, fresh mozz, roasted peppers, arugula — is the cult favorite. Every sandwich is pressed on a panini grill to crispy, melty perfection. The most beloved lunch in the Financial District.

name: All'Antico Vinaio
description: Florence's legendary schiacciata sandwich shop opened in NYC and immediately drew lines around the block. The focaccia-like bread is crispy, olive-oiled, and stuffed with imported Italian meats and cheeses. The La Favolosa with porchetta and truffle cream is the must-order. Yes, it is worth the wait. No, the line does not move fast.

name: Sunny & Annie's
description: East Village bodega-style deli that Eater loves for its breakfast sandwiches and chopped cheese. The BEC on a roll is perfectly greasy and exactly what a New York morning demands. The chopped cheese is the NYC street sandwich that went viral — seasoned ground beef, melted American cheese, lettuce, tomato on a hero. Authentic New York.

name: Eataly Panini Counter
description: The panini counter inside Eataly Flatiron makes pressed Italian sandwiches with imported meats and cheeses that taste like a Roman train station bar — and that is a sincere compliment. The porchetta panini is rich and herby. The prosciutto with burrata melts into the bread. Quick counter service, no seats, eat standing up like an Italian.

name: Parisi Bakery
description: Mott Street Italian bakery and sandwich shop since 1903 — one of the last authentic delis on what used to be a fully Italian block. The bread is baked fresh daily and it is the foundation of everything. The Italian combo on their own semolina bread is one of the best sandwiches in Manhattan. Cash only. The old-timer behind the counter has seen everything.

name: Torrisi Sandwich Shop
description: The legendary Nolita spot from Rich Torrisi and Mario Carbone that launched the Major Food Group empire. Known for Italian-American sandwiches and dishes elevated to fine-art status with house-roasted meats and perfect bread. The chicken parm hero is absurdly good -- crispy cutlet, tangy sauce, melted mozz on fresh bread.

name: No. 7 Sub
description: Chef-driven sub shop in Chelsea with creative combinations that elevate the sandwich to an art form. The fried broccoli sub with sesame and mayo is the cult favorite — a vegetable sub that converts carnivores. The tofu with smoked chili and peanut is equally creative. Inside the Ace Hotel lobby. The most interesting sandwich shop in Manhattan.

name: High Street on Hudson
description: Philly import that brought its obsessive bread program to the West Village and immediately became one of the best sandwich shops in the city. The forager sandwich with roasted mushrooms is extraordinary. The pastrami on rye rivals the delis. The bread — baked in-house — is the star of everything. Brunch is outstanding.

name: Empire State Building
description: The most iconic skyscraper in the world. 86th floor observation deck has 360-degree open-air views. Go at sunset for magic. Express pass worth it to skip the line. Go between 8-10 AM or after 10 PM to avoid crowds.

name: Central Park
description: 843 acres of green in the middle of Manhattan. Bethesda Fountain, Bow Bridge, The Mall, Belvedere Castle, and Strawberry Fields. Enter at 72nd St west side for the best first impression. Rent Citi Bikes for $4.49 per ride.

name: Times Square
description: Love it or hate it, you have to see it once. 15 blocks of neon sensory overload and Broadway theaters. Best at night. See it for 10 minutes, take a photo, then leave. TKTS booth sells same-day Broadway tickets at 20-50% off.

name: The High Line
description: Elevated park built on a former railway running 1.45 miles through Chelsea and Meatpacking. Art installations, gardens, and incredible architecture views. Enter at Gansevoort St south and walk north. Stop at Chelsea Market for food.
address: The High Line, New York, NY 10011

name: MET Museum
description: One of the greatest art museums on Earth with 2 million works spanning 5,000 years. Egyptian Temple of Dendur, European paintings, and seasonal rooftop bar are highlights. Friday and Saturday open until 9 PM and much less crowded.

name: Grand Central Terminal
description: The most beautiful train station in the world. Main concourse ceiling painted with zodiac constellations. The Whispering Gallery lets you whisper into one corner and hear it across the room. Oyster Bar downstairs since 1913. Campbell Bar is hidden inside.

name: DUMBO & Brooklyn Bridge Park
description: The most Instagrammed spot in NYC -- Manhattan Bridge framed by Washington Street cobblestones. Brooklyn Bridge Park has jaw-dropping skyline views, Jane Carousel, and great pizza nearby. Photo spot is between Water and Front Streets on Washington St.

name: Wall Street & Charging Bull
description: The financial capital of the world. Walk past the NYSE, Federal Hall where Washington was inaugurated, and the iconic Charging Bull. Free to walk around. Trinity Church cemetery has Alexander Hamilton grave.

name: Scarr's Pizza
description: Scarr Pimentel mills his own organic flour in the basement, which makes his plain slice one of the most talked-about in New York. The shop on Orchard feels like a time warp — wood paneling, old booths, and a crowd that runs from LES locals to visiting chefs. The pepperoni slice with a glossy curl of grease is the move. Cash-friendly, late-night friendly, and genuinely better than the hype.

name: Grimaldi's
description: The coal-fired Brooklyn pie institution under the Brooklyn Bridge that helped put DUMBO on the map. Lines snake down Old Fulton most weekends for the thin-crust margheritas with fresh mozzarella and San Marzano tomatoes. It's touristy, sure, but the pizza is still very good. No slices — whole pies only.

name: Totonno's
description: Open since 1924 on Neptune Avenue in Coney Island, Totonno's is one of the oldest continuously operating pizzerias in the country. The coal-fired pies have blistered crusts and charred cheese that tastes like no chain will ever replicate. Hours are erratic — they close when they run out of dough, which can be early. Worth the trek.

name: Artichoke Basille's Pizza
description: Famous for a gloriously rich artichoke slice that tastes like spinach-artichoke dip on a crust — an East Village late-night ritual. The original 14th Street shop stays open into the small hours, feeding drunk kids, line cooks, and anyone who needs dairy right now. Also excellent: crab slice, Sicilian, and the Margherita. Multiple locations but the 14th Street mothership has the vibe.

name: Song' E Napule
description: A Greenwich Village Neapolitan pizzeria run by brothers from Naples whose DOC-certified pies have a cloud-soft cornicione and a wet-middle bite that's deeply authentic. Beyond pizza, the fritti and pastas are sleepers — try the Montanara, a flash-fried then wood-oven-finished pie. The second room is wine-focused and surprisingly romantic.
address: 132 W Houston St, New York, NY 10012

name: Upside Pizza
description: Noam Grossman's new-school NYC slice shop in the Garment District uses a 48-hour cold-fermented dough that gives the plain slice a superior structure. The pepperoni with honey, the upside-down Sicilian, and the vodka slice all land. Quick counter service and a line that moves fast.

name: F&F Pizzeria
description: From the Frank's/Prime Meats team, F&F is a tiny Carroll Gardens slice shop that churns out a superb New York plain with blistered undercarriage and balanced sauce. The square Sicilian is among the best in the city. Cash-free, counter-only, line forms early.

name: Sauce Pizzeria
description: Frank Prisinzano's Lower East Side red-sauce joint serves thin, square, crispy Sicilian-style pies plus huge portions of lasagna and meatballs. The Spicy Spring — vodka sauce and Calabrian chile — became famous for a reason. Warm, loud, and very NYC.
address: 147 E Houston St, New York, NY 10002

name: Kesté
description: Roberto Caporuscio's West Village Neapolitan temple — he literally chairs the US branch of the Naples pizza association. The Pizza del Papa (with butternut squash cream and smoked mozzarella) is the house signature. A pillar of NYC's Neapolitan scene.

name: Motorino
description: Mathieu Palombino's East Village Neapolitan that has quietly been one of NYC's best pizzas for over a decade. The brussels sprout pie with garlic, pecorino, and smoked pancetta is the sleeper classic. Crusts are thin, wet, and properly charred.

name: Tompkins Square Bagels
description: An East Village mainstay with outrageous flavored cream cheeses (jalapeño cheddar, French toast, cookie dough) slathered on hand-rolled bagels. The egg sandwiches and whitefish platters are legit. Always a line on weekends.
dishes: Everything Bagel with Scallion Cream Cheese

name: Utopia Bagels
description: Widely considered one of the single best bagels in New York City, Utopia in Whitestone has been kettle-boiling and hand-rolling for over 40 years. The crust is crackly, the crumb is dense and chewy, and the plain is almost spiritual. A Queens cult favorite that locals drive in from miles away for.
dishes: Bacon Egg & Cheese on a Bagel

name: Bagel Hole
description: A Park Slope purist's bagel: small, dense, properly chewy, and with zero interest in the oversized doughy trend. Cash-preferred, tiny storefront, and the plain bagel toasted with butter is all you really need. Brooklyn institution since 1985.

name: PopUp Bagels
description: A viral newcomer that shook up NYC's bagel orthodoxy by serving hot bagels with cream cheese on the side, by the tub, for dipping. Pre-order model and limited drops create permanent lines. The crust is thin and shattery, the inside steamy — unlike any traditional NYC bagel, for better or worse.
dishes: Cinnamon Raisin Bagel with Honey Butter

name: Leo's Bagels
description: A FiDi bagel shop that's become the default choice for downtown workers looking for a proper hand-rolled bagel and a smoked fish platter. Beauty is its consistency — thick schmears, nova from Acme, and a surprisingly good egg sandwich.

name: Hungry Ghost Coffee
description: A small Brooklyn chain pouring Stumptown coffee with genuinely good baked goods, a reliable everyday option across Park Slope and Fort Greene. Bright, plant-filled interiors and strong wifi make it a favorite remote-work perch.

name: Joe Coffee Company
description: A homegrown NYC specialty roaster that started on Waverly Place in 2003 and remains a local default for a dependable cortado or drip. The West Village flagship is a tiny throwback with marble counters and regulars.

name: Bluestone Lane
description: The Aussie-style cafe mini-chain that introduced New York to flat whites and avocado toast at scale. Lamingtons, banana bread, and a menu of tidy brunch plates make it a reliable daytime option across the city.

name: Fabrique Bakery
description: The Stockholm stone-oven bakery chain whose cardamom buns became instantly famous when it landed in NYC. Flaky, generously spiced, and worth the line. Also excellent: cinnamon buns, sourdough loaves, and open-faced Scandi sandwiches.

name: Balthazar Bakery
description: The grab-and-go counter next door to Keith McNally's Balthazar — the croissants, pain au chocolat, and baguettes are among the best in Manhattan, full stop. Morning lines are predictable. Also stocks whole loaves, cookies, and small pastries to go.

name: L'Appartement 4F
description: Ashley Coiffard and Gautier Coiffard's Brooklyn Heights bakery that became an overnight line-out-the-door phenomenon for laminated pastries of genuine Parisian quality. The kouign-amann and the ham-and-cheese croissant regularly sell out. Tiny, lovely, and worth the wait.

name: Radio Bakery
description: From the team behind She Wolf, a Greenpoint bakery that pumps out hearth breads and laminated pastries with a devoted following. The morning buns, seasonal fruit galettes, and focaccia sandwiches are the move. Counter line moves fast but stock dwindles early.

name: Librae Bakery
description: Amanda Bechara's Middle Eastern-inflected East Village bakery making pastries spiced with cardamom, orange blossom, and sumac. The labneh cheesecake and baklava croissant rotate in and out, but everything is distinctive. A fresh voice in NYC's bakery landscape.

name: She Wolf Bakery
description: A naturally leavened Greenpoint bakery that supplies some of the best restaurants in the city with their bread. The miche, seeded rye, and baguettes are pillars of the NYC sourdough scene, and the cafe sells pastries and loaves directly.

name: Win Son Bakery
description: The bakery counterpart to Win Son across the street, serving Taiwanese-American pastries and breakfast sandwiches that redefined what a Brooklyn bakery could be. The scallion milk bread and the fried chicken sandwich are essential. Sit-down coffee and counter service both work.

name: Fan-Fan Doughnuts
description: Fany Gerson's Bed-Stuy doughnut shop where yeast-raised rings get filled with seasonal jams and dulce de leche. Regularly sold out before noon on weekends. A small but mighty contender for NYC's best doughnut.

name: Cote
description: Simon Kim's Michelin-starred Korean steakhouse mash-up where smokeless tabletop grills meet dry-aged USDA Prime. The Butcher's Feast — four cuts plus banchan, stews, and soft-serve — is the order. The wine list is one of the strongest in the city. Loud, sceney, and very good.

name: Danji
description: Hooni Kim's pioneering modern Korean tapas bar — the first Korean restaurant in the US to receive a Michelin star. The KFB (Korean fried-chicken wings) and the bulgogi sliders remain legendary. Small, warm, and still essential after more than a decade.

name: Hanjan
description: Hooni Kim's second spot, a Flatiron gastropub focused on Korean drinking food and soju. The pa jun (scallion pancake) and the sujebi (hand-torn noodle soup) are high points. Lower-key and earthier than Danji — a chef's favorite.

name: Nowon
description: Jae Lee's East Village Korean-American bar known for the Nowon burger — a dry-aged smash patty with American cheese and bulgogi on top. The KFC (Korean fried chicken) is excellent and the cocktails pour strong. A rowdy, fun, late-night spot.

name: Soogil
description: Chef Soogil Lim's intimate East Village spot blending his French training with Korean flavors — he was formerly at Daniel. The short rib with gochujang jus and the oxtail pappardelle are signatures. A sleeper pick for quiet, refined dining.

name: Kimika
description: An Italian-Japanese mash-up in Nolita from chef Christine Lau, where uni-topped crudos meet handmade pastas. The lasagna bolognese and the cold mazemen are standouts. The tiny room is dim and date-night appropriate.

name: Kunjip
description: A 24-hour Koreatown institution on 32nd Street that's been serving stone-pot bibimbap, soft tofu stew, and galbi to late-night crowds for decades. Not the trendiest room in K-Town, but one of the most beloved and reliable.

name: Bonchon
description: The Korean fried chicken chain that helped popularize the double-fried, soy-garlic wing in America. The 32nd Street Koreatown location is the NYC flagship — loud, beer-soaked, and reliably good. Soy-garlic wings are the default order.

name: Miss Korea BBQ
description: A Koreatown BBQ stalwart spanning multiple floors on 32nd Street, serving marinated galbi and prime cuts over tabletop grills late into the night. The premium beef set is the big-night order; the kimchi stew on its own is worth the visit.

name: Babu Ji
description: Jessi and Jennifer Singh's East Village Indian with a fun, loud vibe and an excellent self-serve beer fridge. The Colonel Tso's Cauliflower and the unreal gol gappa are signatures. Thali options run surprisingly deep.

name: Junoon
description: Vikas Khanna's fine-dining Flatiron Indian, one of the longest-running Michelin-starred Indian restaurants in the US. Dedicated tandoor, sigri, tawa, and patthar stations turn out regional dishes with modern polish. The tasting menu moves from Kerala to Kashmir and back.

name: Indian Accent
description: The New York outpost of Manish Mehrotra's acclaimed New Delhi flagship, serving inventive modern Indian tasting menus in a polished Midtown hotel room. The signature meetha achar pork ribs and blue-cheese naan are icons. A special-occasion pillar for Indian food in NYC.

name: Bungalow
description: Vikas Khanna's East Village follow-up that became one of the hottest tables in NYC on arrival. The lamb shank nihari, tandoori broccoli, and coastal curries showcase India's regional range. Bright, airy, and very hard to book.

name: Masalawala & Sons
description: Roni Mazumdar and Chintan Pandya's Park Slope follow-up to Adda and Dhamaka, focused on Calcutta-style Bengali cooking. The kosha mangsho and Parsi-style berry pulao land perfectly. Loud, warm, and one of the best new Brooklyn restaurants of the decade.

name: Pippali
description: A modern NoMad Indian restaurant spotlighting regional dishes from Chettinad to Hyderabad, with a tandoor going all night. The lamb biryani and Chettinad chicken are the signature orders. A refined alternative to Curry Hill staples.

name: Jackson Diner
description: A Jackson Heights institution serving northern Indian classics and a cult-favorite lunch buffet since 1980. Unfussy, generous, and one of the reasons Little India became Little India. The goat curry and lamb biryani remain the anchors.

name: Pig and Khao
description: Leah Cohen's LES spot blends Thai and Filipino flavors with equal fluency. The whole fried branzino, sizzling sisig, and khao soi regularly appear on best-of lists. A pioneering Southeast Asian restaurant in NYC.

name: Ugly Baby
description: Sirichai Sreparplarn’s ferociously spicy Thai restaurant is reopening in a bigger Williamsburg space at 364 Grand Street after shuttering its beloved Carroll Gardens original. A “new, wild take” on the menu is promised; the rare Southern Thai dishes that made Ugly Baby one of NYC’s most exciting kitchens remain the draw.

name: Wayla
description: An LES Thai restaurant focused on the grilled and charcoal dishes of Bangkok street cuisine. The crispy pork belly, drunken noodles, and whole fish are standouts; the backyard garden is a summer destination.

name: Up Thai
description: An Upper East Side Thai that's become one of the neighborhood's go-tos for dependable, well-spiced curries and noodles. The crispy rice salad and the Crying Tiger beef are hits.

name: Madame Vo
description: Yen Vo and Jimmy Ly's East Village Vietnamese dedicated to the cooking of Saigon. The pho dac biet and the bo kho (beef stew) are pillars; the beef seven ways is a whole-night event. Warm room, big flavors.

name: An Choi
description: A dive-y LES Vietnamese sandwich shop and banh mi counter with a broader menu of pho, broken rice, and cocktails. The BBQ pork banh mi and the oxtail pho are sleeper classics. Dim, rock-heavy, very NYC.

name: Van Da
description: Hannah Wong and Yen Ngo's East Village Vietnamese with a focus on regional specialties and Vietnamese wine pairings. The shaking beef and crab noodles are especially strong. A quieter alternative to the neighborhood's louder spots.

name: Saigon Shack
description: A tiny Greenwich Village Vietnamese shop beloved by NYU students for its generous bowls of pho and banh mi under $15. Long lines, tight tables, very worth it.

name: Purple Yam
description: Amy Besa and Romy Dorotan's pan-Southeast Asian restaurant in Ditmas Park with a focus on Filipino cooking. The kare-kare, sisig, and ube desserts all shine. A pioneering Filipino restaurant in Brooklyn.

name: Jeepney
description: A loud, fun East Village gastropub serving Filipino comfort food and creative cocktails. The Kamayan feast — eaten with bare hands on banana leaves — is the party move. Sisig, lumpia, and halo-halo are all on point.

name: Hwa Yuan
description: The modern revival of a legendary East Broadway Sichuan restaurant that originally introduced NYC to cold sesame noodles in the 1970s. The multi-level space serves elegant Sichuan classics — the whole crispy sea bass in spicy sauce is the move for a group.

name: Shu Jiao Fu Zhou
description: A cash-only Fuzhou noodle shop on Eldridge where a plate of dumplings and peanut noodles costs almost nothing and tastes like a dream. No atmosphere, no frills, one of the best cheap-eats bargains in NYC.

name: Szechuan Mountain House
description: An East Village Sichuan restaurant serving boundary-pushing regional dishes in a theatrical rustic room. The Grandma's Braised Pork, the cold spicy beef tendon, and the mapo tofu all hit high notes.
address: 23 St Marks Pl, New York, NY 10003

name: Hao Noodle
description: A pretty West Village Chinese restaurant known for noodle soups, vegetable-heavy cold plates, and beautiful tea service. The spicy dan dan and the tea-smoked duck are signatures; the room is warm, green, and date-night friendly.

name: Little Tong Noodle Shop
description: Simone Tong's East Village noodle shop focused on Yunnanese mixian rice noodles in bracing, herbaceous broths. The Grandma Chicken and the Little Pot Mixian are the defaults. Smart, small, and one of the most original Chinese restaurants in the city.
address: 28 St Marks Pl, New York, NY 10003

name: RedFarm
description: Joe Ng and Ed Schoenfeld's West Village modern Chinese with famously whimsical dim sum — Pac-Man shrimp dumplings, the Katz's pastrami egg roll, and the crispy beef. Loud, lively, and consistently excellent for nearly 15 years.

name: Shun Lee Palace
description: Michael Tong's Midtown East Chinese banquet hall that has been a special-occasion destination for Manhattan diners since 1971. The Peking duck, the Grand Marnier shrimp, and the Beggar's Chicken remain old-school signatures. Jackets encouraged.

name: Dim Sum Palace
description: An always-busy Chinatown dim sum house with carts still rolling around the dining room on weekend mornings. Har gow, shu mai, turnip cakes, and a steady stream of roasted meats on rice make this a classic NYC Sunday.

name: Sushi Ishikawa
description: A Michelin-starred Upper East Side omakase counter where chef Don Pham serves an intimate 18-course nigiri progression. The fish is flown from Toyosu and the rice is warmed to body temperature. Quiet, controlled, and genuinely among the best sushi in NYC.

name: Sushi Azabu
description: A hidden Tribeca omakase basement beneath Greenwich Grill, holding a Michelin star since its early years. Traditional Edomae style, calm and confident. A consistent top-five sushi experience in the city.

name: Jin Ramen
description: Harlem's neighborhood ramen shop, serving rich tonkotsu and shoyu bowls that became the uptown go-to when it opened near Columbia. A second Morningside Heights location keeps the student crowd fed.

name: Shuko
description: Nick Kim and Jimmy Lau's modern Greenwich Village omakase counter that broke from the traditional hushed sushi mold — music plays, chefs joke, and the fish is pristine. 20-course nigiri and a kaiseki option are both excellent.

name: Sushi Yasuda
description: A blond-wood Midtown sushi temple that has been an NYC benchmark since 1999, famous for its pristine fish and the warm rice ethos championed by its original chef. The a la carte counter seats are still the best way to experience it.

name: Hasaki
description: An East Village Japanese mainstay since 1984, serving dependable sushi and cooked izakaya dishes in a warm, low-lit room. Less flashy than the new wave of omakase spots, but a proven neighborhood favorite.

name: Takashi
description: A West Village yakiniku restaurant specializing in rare Japanese offal cuts you grill at your own table. The beef tongue, niku-uni (raw beef with uni), and the late-night garlic rice are all cult favorites.

name: Bar Masa
description: Masa Takayama's more accessible a la carte room adjacent to his iconic tasting counter Masa, serving sushi, sashimi, and cooked dishes at a much lower price than its legendary neighbor. Still stratospheric — just only by regular fine-dining standards.

name: Aburiya Kinnosuke
description: A serious Midtown East izakaya with tatami rooms and a menu deeply focused on robata-grilled skewers and traditional Japanese small plates. The salt-grilled whole fish and the kamameshi rice pots are highlights. Popular with Japanese expats for a reason.

name: La Contenta
description: A stylish LES Mexican restaurant from Luis Arce Mota, serving regional dishes with well-chosen mezcals. The huitlacoche quesadilla, the birria tacos, and the tamale trio all deliver. A great mid-week date-night pick.

name: Empellón
description: Alex Stupak's upscale Midtown Mexican, from the former WD-50 pastry chef turned modern Mexican pioneer. The duck carnitas, the queso fundido, and the avocado taco with pistachio salsa all land. A full bar of mezcal and an unusually strong wine list round it out.

name: Atla
description: Enrique Olvera's all-day NoHo Mexican, the casual sister to Cosme. Sun-drenched room, tostadas, chilaquiles, and creative salads draw a celebrity-heavy brunch crowd. Consistently one of the best brunches in Manhattan.

name: Claro
description: TJ Steele's Michelin-starred Gowanus Oaxacan restaurant with a mezcal menu that's among the deepest in the city. The mole negro, suckling pig, and handmade tortillas reflect Oaxacan regional cooking at its finest. A quiet standout in Brooklyn dining.

name: La Esquina
description: The legendary Nolita corner taqueria with a hidden speakeasy-style brasserie down the stairs — one of the founding icons of NYC's 2000s downtown scene. The tacos al pastor and elote still bring people in.

name: Casa Adela
description: A Loisaida Puerto Rican institution that's been spinning rotisserie chickens on Avenue C since the 1970s. The pernil, the rotisserie chicken, and the mofongo are unchanging anchors. A James Beard America's Classics honoree and a pillar of the neighborhood.

name: Forsythia
description: Jacob Siwak's LES Roman trattoria where the focus is handmade pastas and bright seasonal antipasti. The cacio e pepe and the rigatoni all'amatriciana are textbook, and the wine list leans natural. Warm, tight, and always full.

name: Osteria Morini
description: Michael White's SoHo osteria focused on the Emilia-Romagna region — handmade tortellini, tagliatelle bolognese, and lots of prosciutto. The wood-paneled dining room is warm and clubby. A reliable Italian special-occasion pick.

name: Al Di La Trattoria
description: Anna Klinger's long-running Park Slope Venetian trattoria — a no-reservations Brooklyn pillar for over two decades. The malfatti (chard-and-ricotta dumplings), the braised rabbit, and the hanger steak are menu anchors. Still one of the best Italians in the borough.

name: Scarpetta
description: Scott Conant's Meatpacking Italian, famous for a spaghetti with tomato sauce that is taken almost absurdly seriously (and deserves it). Now located in The James New York - NoMad. The duck and foie ravioli and the polenta with truffle mushrooms round out a special-occasion menu.

name: Il Mulino New York
