description: A Greenwich Village Italian institution since 1981, famous for parades of complimentary antipasti and enormous portions of classic red-sauce and Abruzzese dishes. Clubby, dimly lit, and unapologetically old-school. Reservations are essential.

name: Lupa
description: Mario Batali and Joe Bastianich's originally-opened Roman trattoria (now part of the Batali-less Tarry Lodge/B&B group), still serving the Roman classics that made it famous — gnocchi alla romana, bucatini all'amatriciana, and saltimbocca.

name: Long Island Bar
description: Toby Cecchini and Joel Tompkins's restored 1950s diner in Cobble Hill reborn as one of the best cocktail bars in the country. The perfect gimlet and the martini have a cult following; the burger is shockingly good. A pilgrimage spot.

name: Apotheke
description: A speakeasy-style cocktail bar hidden on Doyers Street in a former opium den, famous for theatrically prepared apothecary-inspired drinks. Dim, velvet-curtained, and surprisingly romantic. A long-running standard for the date-night cocktail bar category.

name: Tatiana
description: Kwame Onwuachi's Lincoln Center restaurant that earned a rare four-star New York Times review and immediately became the hardest reservation in NYC. Afro-Caribbean flavors filtered through Onwuachi's life — jerk-spiced pastrami short rib, curry goat patties, and egusi dumplings. A generational debut.
address: 10 Lincoln Center Plaza, New York, NY 10023

name: Jupiter
description: An all-day Italian cafe and pasta bar from the King team inside Rockefeller Center, serving beautiful focaccia sandwiches, crudo, and handmade pasta in a bright, European-feeling room. One of the best things to happen to Midtown dining in years.
address: Rink Level, 20 W 50th St, New York, NY 10020

name: Foul Witch
description: The Roberta's team's moody East Village Italian, a dark-walled counterpart to their sunnier spots. Wood-fired pastas, whole fish, and a tightly edited natural wine list. Sceney and warm at the same time.

name: Libertine
description: A cozy West Village bistro that opened to immediate acclaim for its bouillabaisse, steak frites, and generous wine list. A modern version of a classic French neighborhood restaurant.

name: Cafe Chelsea
description: The restaurant inside the restored Hotel Chelsea, from the team behind Eight Row Flint in Houston. A classic hotel bistro serving onion soup, trout amandine, and a stacked cocktail menu — a worthy addition to the iconic landmark.

name: Corner Bar
description: Ignacio Mattos's LES bistro inside the Nine Orchard hotel, serving a tight menu of steak frites, steak tartare, and classic bar food with impeccable execution. The burger is one of the most sought-after in the city.

name: Foxface Natural
description: Ori Menashe and Genevieve Gergis-trained chef David Santos's East Village offshoot of the Foxface sandwich shop, turned into an ambitious tasting-menu experience focused on wild game and foraged ingredients. Small, intense, and one of the most interesting small restaurants in NYC.

name: Penny
description: A petite East Village seafood bar from the Claud team, devoted to oysters, caviar, and impeccable raw-bar work. The stocked bar list makes it a great pre- or post-dinner drop-in too.

name: Clover Hill
description: Chef Charlie Mitchell's Michelin-starred Brooklyn Heights tasting-menu counter — Mitchell was the first Black chef in NYC to earn a Michelin star. The 10-course menu is ingredient-forward and quietly modern. Small, serene, and essential for the NYC fine-dining map.

name: Hav & Mar
description: Marcus Samuelsson's Chelsea seafood restaurant blending Scandinavian and African influences — the name means 'Sea & Ocean' in Norwegian. Gravlax, jollof paella, and whole roasted fish anchor the menu. Beautiful room, strong wine list.

name: Naks
description: The Unapologetic Foods team (Semma, Dhamaka) opened this East Village Filipino kamayan restaurant to immediate critical acclaim. Chef Eric Valdez's tasting menu turns out dishes like kinilaw, whole lechon, and kare-kare that feel uncompromising and thrilling.

name: Noksu
description: A hidden Koreatown omakase-style Korean tasting counter that earned a Michelin star. Chef Dae Kim's Korean-inspired progression runs from caviar bites to grilled short rib. One of the more interesting new fine-dining entries in NYC.

name: Café Altro Paradiso
description: Ignacio Mattos's bright, airy SoHo Italian companion to Estela. Handmade pastas, a great vegetable program, and one of the strongest brunches in Manhattan. The bucatini and the whole branzino are anchors.

name: Cafe Luxembourg
description: A Keith McNally-designed Upper West Side bistro that's been an institution since 1983. The steak frites, the roast chicken, and the Lincoln Center pre-theater crowd are the whole story. Time-tested and still genuinely good.

name: Peter Luger
description: The 1887 Williamsburg steakhouse that is arguably the most famous in America. Dry-aged porterhouse for two, bacon slab, and sliced tomato & onion — the menu has barely changed in a century. Gruff service, cash preferred, and a cultural institution.

name: Gallaghers Steakhouse
description: A Theater District steakhouse that's been dry-aging prime beef since 1927, with the meat locker on display to the sidewalk. Old-school to the bone — plush booths, red sauce, and waiters in white jackets. The porterhouse is the move.

name: Union Square Cafe
description: Danny Meyer's founding Union Square Hospitality Group restaurant, now in a Park Avenue South location. The restaurant that defined the warm, service-first NYC hospitality template. Seasonal menu rooted in the Greenmarket just around the corner.

name: Blue Hill
description: Dan Barber's downtown restaurant (sibling to the upstate Stone Barns flagship) that helped define American farm-to-table cooking. Underground Washington Place dining room, seasonal menu, and a tasting option that's become a pilgrimage for serious eaters.

name: Craft
description: Tom Colicchio's long-running Flatiron restaurant where diners build plates from simple seasonal components — the original hyper-ingredient-driven NYC format. Still polished and still relevant decades later.

name: Aldo Sohm Wine Bar
description: Le Bernardin's legendary sommelier Aldo Sohm's companion wine bar next door to the main restaurant, with an adventurous wine-by-the-glass list and elegant small plates. One of the best wine experiences in Midtown.

name: Museum of Modern Art (MoMA)
description: The most influential modern art museum in the world, home to Van Gogh's Starry Night, Monet's Water Lilies, Picasso's Demoiselles d'Avignon, and Warhol's Campbell's Soup Cans. Six floors of masterpieces. The sculpture garden is a mid-Manhattan oasis. Free Friday evenings via UNIQLO sponsorship.

name: Whitney Museum of American Art
description: Renzo Piano-designed museum dedicated exclusively to 20th and 21st-century American art. Edward Hopper, Georgia O'Keeffe, Jasper Johns, Basquiat. The outdoor terraces offer Hudson River and High Line views. The Biennial remains the most influential contemporary art exhibition in America.

name: Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum
description: Frank Lloyd Wright's spiraling white rotunda — one of the 20th century's most important architectural landmarks — housing modern and contemporary art. Walking down the continuous ramp past Kandinsky, Chagall, and Picasso is itself the experience. A masterpiece building full of masterpieces.

name: American Museum of Natural History
description: 34 million specimens across 26 interconnected buildings. The iconic dinosaur halls, the blue whale in the Hall of Ocean Life, the Rose Center for Earth and Space (Hayden Planetarium), and the Gilder Center's immersive science experiences. A full day — easily. Central Park's west neighbor.
address: 200 Central Prk W, New York, NY 10024

name: 9/11 Memorial & Museum
description: The twin reflecting pools mark the footprints of the original towers; the names of every victim are inscribed in bronze around their edges. Below, the museum tells the full story with artifacts including the Last Column and the Survivor Staircase. The most emotionally powerful museum experience in New York.

name: Intrepid Sea, Air & Space Museum
description: The aircraft carrier USS Intrepid docked on the Hudson, now a military and maritime museum. Board the space shuttle Enterprise, walk through the Concorde, tour the submarine Growler. Exhibits cover the Cold War, space exploration, and naval aviation. Great for families and history buffs.
address: Pier 86, W 46th St, New York, NY 10036

name: Summit One Vanderbilt
description: The newest and most immersive observation experience in NYC, rising 1,401 feet above Grand Central. Floor-to-ceiling mirrored rooms (Air), glass skyboxes extending over 42nd Street (Ledge), and the Ascent glass elevator on the exterior. The most Instagrammed observatory in the city.

name: Rockefeller Center
description: The Art Deco complex anchoring midtown: Christmas tree lighting, the Rink at Rockefeller Center in winter, Channel Gardens, Radio City next door, Prometheus statue, and 30 Rock. Home to NBC Studios and the Today Show plaza. The beating heart of Manhattan's holiday season.

name: Bryant Park
description: 9.6-acre park behind the NY Public Library, the epicenter of midtown's social life. Free summer movies, fall lawn games, winter Ice Skating + European-style Holiday Shops. Chess, ping-pong, petanque, reading room. One of the most programmed public spaces in the world.

name: Washington Square Park
description: Greenwich Village's living room, anchored by the iconic Washington Arch. Street musicians, chess players, NYU students, and dog-walkers orbit the central fountain. Best people-watching in NYC. The Halloween parade passes through every year. Free, open 24/7.
address: Washington Square N, New York, NY 10012

name: San Sabino
description: West Village Italian-American seafood restaurant from the Don Angie team (Angie Rito and Scott Tacinelli). Warm-brass dining room, caviar service, and sardine-grandma sandwiches that took over food-media feeds shortly after opening.

name: Gjelina
description: NYC outpost of the iconic Venice, LA original. Wood-fired pizzas, vegetable-forward plates, and an all-day menu that set the template for California-NYC cross-pollination. NoHo setting with industrial-chic warmth.

name: The Musket Room
description: Kiwi-owned Nolita restaurant with New Zealand-inspired New American cooking and a beloved garden patio. Multi-course tasting menu showcases venison, fish, and stonefruit in season. Earned a MICHELIN Star before its 2023 reinvention and remains a NYC destination.

name: Momofuku Noodle Bar
description: David Chang's original 2004 restaurant — the one that effectively launched the modern New York ramen scene. Still serving constantly-changing seasonal menus alongside signature pork buns and the namesake Momofuku ramen bowl. East Village flagship of the global Momofuku group.

name: Strange Delight
description: Fort Greene Brooklyn favorite named one of Infatuation's and Resy's ten best new NYC restaurants of 2024. Seasonal New American cooking in a tight, thoughtful space, with a killer cocktail list and a morning cafe spinoff (Amanda's Good Morning Cafe).

name: Cafe Mars
description: An "unusual" Italian restaurant from the family behind Leroy's Place. Core to Cafe Mars is investigating and expanding what it means to be an Italian restaurant — rotating pastas, unexpected flavor pairings, and a wine list skewed toward Italian biodynamic producers. Park Slope locals' pick.

name: Caviar Russe
description: MICHELIN-starred caviar specialist seven years running. A tasting-menu Dining Room upstairs and a more relaxed Bar & Lounge downstairs, both anchored by same-day delivery-fresh caviar. 538 Madison Avenue flagship with a Miami sister.

name: Le Jardinier
description: MICHELIN-starred French restaurant from chef Alain Verzeroli (ex-Joël Robuchon). Vegetable-forward cooking refracted through classical French technique, served in a verdant, garden-like Midtown dining room. Upstairs sibling Sereine extends the program for special menus.

name: Stonewall Inn National Monument
description: Birthplace of the modern LGBTQ+ rights movement. The 1969 Stonewall Uprising transformed this Greenwich Village bar into a pilgrimage site; designated a National Monument in 2016. The adjacent Christopher Park forms the monument’s core.

name: Cherry Lane Theatre
description: New York City’s oldest continuously running off-Broadway theater, founded 1924 on a quiet West Village mews. Tucked on Commerce Street, the 179-seat space has premiered work by Sam Shepard, Samuel Beckett, and Edward Albee.

name: Tenement Museum
description: Preserved tenement buildings at 97 and 103 Orchard Street telling the stories of the immigrant families who lived there between the 1860s and 1980s. Guided tours walk through recreated apartments and the working-class neighborhoods they shaped.

name: New Museum
description: Manhattan’s only museum dedicated exclusively to contemporary art, founded 1977. The Sejima + Ryue Nishizawa / SANAA-designed Bowery building is itself a landmark of 21st-century architecture. Rotating shows highlight emerging international artists.

name: Essex Market
description: Historic public market founded 1940 under Mayor La Guardia, reborn in 2019 inside Essex Crossing. A vendor hall of specialty grocers, butchers, and prepared-food stalls reflecting the Lower East Side’s immigrant food traditions.

name: Museum of Chinese in America
description: Maya Lin-designed museum documenting the history of the Chinese diaspora in the United States. Rotating exhibitions and an extensive archive; the main Centre Street location is the public face of the institution.

name: Columbus Park
description: Historic public park carved out of the 19th-century Five Points slum. By day, the playground of choice for Chinatown grandparents — dominoes, tai chi, and the occasional pickup erhu concert.

name: Museum at Eldridge Street
description: Restored 1887 synagogue that served Eastern European Jewish immigrants on the Lower East Side’s Chinatown border. A National Historic Landmark with an east rose window by Kiki Smith and Deborah Gans.

name: Apollo Theater
description: Harlem’s crown jewel and the cradle of Black American music — Ella Fitzgerald, James Brown, and countless others launched careers here at Amateur Night. The neon marquee on 125th Street is one of NYC’s most photographed landmarks.

name: Studio Museum in Harlem
description: Premier institution dedicated to artists of African descent. Reopened 2025 in a new David Adjaye-designed building on West 125th Street; the Artist-in-Residence program has launched the careers of Kehinde Wiley, Julie Mehretu, and many others.

name: Morris-Jumel Mansion
description: Manhattan’s oldest remaining house (1765), where George Washington headquartered during the Battle of Harlem Heights. A hidden gem on a hill at the edge of Washington Heights with panoramic views.

name: Basilica of St. Patrick’s Old Cathedral
description: The original St. Patrick’s Cathedral, completed 1815 — the seat of the Archdiocese of New York before the Fifth Avenue cathedral opened. Gothic Revival exterior, 200-year-old cemetery, and the catacomb tour that inspired scenes in The Godfather.

name: Elizabeth Street Garden
description: Volunteer-run sculpture and community garden tucked between Prince and Spring Streets. Classical statuary, greenery, and a beloved community gathering spot — fought to stay open through repeated development attempts.

name: Prospect Park
description: Brooklyn’s 526-acre Olmsted and Vaux masterpiece — the team behind Central Park. The Long Meadow, the Ravine, Prospect Park Lake, and the LeFrak Center skating rink anchor Brooklyn’s largest park.

name: Brooklyn Museum
description: Second-largest museum in New York City, with 1.5 million works across ancient Egyptian antiquities, Judy Chicago’s Dinner Party, and a celebrated African art collection. First Saturdays are a free, late-night neighborhood institution.

name: Brooklyn Botanic Garden
description: 52-acre botanical garden wedged between Prospect Park and the Brooklyn Museum. Cherry Esplanade Sakura Matsuri (spring), Shakespeare Garden, and the glass Steinhardt Conservatory — one of the city’s great year-round escapes.

name: Brooklyn Academy of Music (BAM)
description: America’s oldest continuously operating performing-arts center (1861), anchored by the landmark 1908 Peter Jay Sharp Building. The annual Next Wave Festival is a global contemporary-performance showcase.

name: BRIC House
description: Nonprofit performing-arts and media center in Fort Greene — the producer of the Celebrate Brooklyn! summer concert series at Prospect Park Bandshell. Free contemporary-art exhibitions in the Stoop gallery year-round.

name: STK Steakhouse
description: The Meatpacking District steakhouse where a DJ spins through dinner service and the Lil' BRGs arrive on a tiny cutting board. STK turned the steakhouse into nightlife — USDA prime, truffle Parmesan fries, and a dining room that turns into a bar as the night thickens. Not where your grandfather had his 80th birthday. Where his granddaughter had hers.
address: 26 Little W 12th St, New York, NY 10014

name: Cafe Carmellini
awards: Eater NY Best New 2024, Michelin-listed 2025, NY Times 3-star
description: Andrew Carmellini's 2024 fine-dining flagship inside the Fifth Avenue Hotel — a French-Italian tasting-menu room with a hand-painted ceiling, a serious cellar, and one of the most-reviewed new openings in the NYC fine-dining class since it hit. The room NoHo Hospitality built to cement Carmellini's modern-master status.

name: Carne Mare
awards: Eater NY Hit List — Seaport Italian chophouse
description: Andrew Carmellini's Seaport Italian chophouse inside the Tin Building — a dry-aged beef program, handmade pasta, and a Fulton Street room that revived the Seaport dining scene when it opened. The polished NoHo Hospitality answer to Peter Luger for the downtown set.

name: Leuca
awards: NoHo Hospitality — Williamsburg Italian at William Vale
description: NoHo Hospitality's Southern Italian restaurant at the William Vale hotel in Williamsburg — wood-fired pizzas, a strong handmade-pasta program, and one of the more restaurant-focused hotel rooms in Brooklyn. A required Williamsburg date-night stop.

name: Jeffrey's Grocery
description: Gabe Stulman's Waverly Place corner — a compact raw bar, a reliable seafood-tower program, and one of the defining low-key West Village neighborhood date-night rooms. The shop that anchored the Stulman West Village takeover.

name: Joseph Leonard
description: Gabe Stulman's flagship corner bistro on Waverly Place — an all-day French-American menu, a steady crowd that fills at 9am for eggs and at 11pm for steak frites, and the template every polished West Village neighborhood restaurant that opened after 2009 traces back to.

name: Fairfax
awards: Gabriel Stulman — Happy Cooking all-day concept
description: Stulman's West 4th Street Jewish-deli-leaning all-day café — a matzo ball soup, a pastrami sandwich, and a built-for-neighborhood breakfast-to-late-night format. One of the Happy Cooking rooms that feels like a neighborhood fixture two years after opening.

name: Bar Sardine
awards: Gabriel Stulman — Happy Cooking wine-bar concept
description: Stulman's West 10th Street wine bar — a tight natural-leaning wine list, a burger that Eater has repeatedly ranked among the city's best, and a room small enough to feel like you caught a secret every time it isn't full.

name: La Grenouille
awards: Established 1962 — last of the great Midtown French palaces
description: Charles Masson's 1962 Midtown French institution — the last of the great 1960s French grande-dame rooms still operating in Manhattan. The floral program is legendary, the menu has barely moved in six decades (frog legs Provençale, Dover sole, quenelles), and a table here is the last experience of its kind in New York.

name: Patsy's Italian Restaurant
description: A 1944 red-sauce Italian institution on W 56th — the same family-run Southern Italian operation for three generations, famously Frank Sinatra's favorite New York restaurant. Veal chop Milanese, spaghetti pomodoro, a dining room preserved in mid-century amber.

name: Pietro's
awards: Established 1932 — one of NY's oldest continuously operating Italian steakhouses
description: A 1932 Midtown Italian steakhouse on E 43rd — dry-aged sirloins, old-school Italian service, and a dining room that exists exactly as it did 40 years ago. The kind of room where regulars are known and the waitstaff has been working the same station since Carter.

name: Sparks Steak House
awards: Established 1966 — legendary wine program, notorious 1985 Gotti history
description: The 1966 Midtown steakhouse on E 46th — dry-aged sirloins, a 500-bottle wine list that made it a destination for sommeliers, and the infamous 1985 sidewalk where Paul Castellano caught a Gotti contract. Steak-forward food history in one room.

name: The Palm Too
awards: Established 1973 — original Palm second location, 2nd Ave institution
description: The Palm's 1973 second location on Second Avenue — sawdust-floor era gone but the caricature-lined walls and New York-Italian steakhouse menu remain. One of the rare Midtown classics still held by the Bozzi-Ganzi founding families.

name: Rolf's German Restaurant
description: The 1968 Gramercy German restaurant that has kept Christmas decorations hanging year-round for decades — schnitzel, bratwurst, steins of imported lager, and a room so over-decorated with tinsel and ornaments it's a tourist destination unto itself.

name: Lexington Candy Shop Luncheonette
description: A 1925 UES luncheonette on Lexington Avenue — a third-generation family-run soda-fountain diner with the original Coca-Cola syrup dispenser, a classic American-diner menu, and one of the last remaining pre-WWII soda fountains in Manhattan.

name: Bar Six
description: A 6th Avenue French-Moroccan bistro — a steady 30-year neighborhood fixture, a late-night crowd that has included Carrie Bradshaw-era sightings, and a couscous program that's better than the guidebook references would suggest.

name: Chumley's
description: The 1922 Bedford Street speakeasy where Fitzgerald, Hemingway, and Faulkner kept regular tables — collapsed in 2007, reopened in 2016 by Sushi Nakazawa's Alessandro Borgognone, and restored to its literary-hideout glory. Book jackets of famous regulars still on the walls.

name: Waverly Inn
awards: Graydon Carter — Vanity Fair celebrity haunt since 2006
description: Graydon Carter's 1844 Bank Street tavern — the former Vanity Fair editor's relaunch of a 19th-century bar, a mural-wrapped dining room, a mac-and-cheese with shaved black truffle, and the most reliable celebrity-sighting room in the West Village for the past 20 years.

name: Cafe Habana
awards: Established 1998 — Nolita corner institution, famous Mexican street corn
description: A 1998 Prince Street Cuban-Mexican diner — the Mexican-street-corn that anchored half the menu rip-offs of the 2000s, Cuban sandwiches, and one of the defining small-format corner rooms of 21st-century Nolita.

name: Temple Bar
awards: Reopened 2022 by Sam Ross & Michael McIlroy (Attaboy/Diamond Reef)
description: The 1989 Lafayette Street cocktail lounge — closed in 2017, reopened in 2022 by the Attaboy team of Sam Ross and Michael McIlroy. A dim-lit, booth-seated classic cocktail room with a menu that respects the pre-Prohibition template. A reference for how a proper New York cocktail bar should look and feel.

name: The Smile
description: A Bond Street subterranean all-day café — Mediterranean-leaning breakfast-to-dinner menu, house-made granola, and a design-first room that has anchored the Bond Street block since 2009. Still a required NoHo morning stop.

name: BondSt
awards: NoHo Japanese since 1998 — Jonathan Morr concept
description: A Bond Street townhouse Japanese restaurant since 1998 — a Tokyo-modern dining program, a compact sushi bar, and a ground-floor lounge that made it one of the defining NoHo scene-and-sushi rooms of the early 2000s.

name: Kajitsu
awards: Michelin 1 Star — NY's only shojin-ryori temple cuisine
description: A Murray Hill temple-cuisine (shojin-ryori) Japanese restaurant — a seasonal vegan kaiseki tasting menu, a Buddhist-vegetarian Kyoto tradition rendered precisely in New York, and the only restaurant of its kind in the city.

name: Kyo Ya
awards: East Village kaiseki — NYT critics' pick
description: An East 7th Street basement kaiseki room — a compact seasonal tasting menu, a single chef-run operation, and one of the rare true kaiseki experiences in the city outside the high-end Midtown set. A reservation-only, word-of-mouth kind of room.

name: Tiki Tabu
awards: Moxy Hotel LES rooftop — Dante/Sother Teague team
description: The Moxy LES rooftop tiki bar from the Dante cocktail team — a tropical-drink-first menu, an Allen Street skyline, and one of the more committed genre-distinct rooftop cocktail rooms in the LES.
address: 120 Allen St Rooftop, New York NY 10002

name: Gallow Green
awards: McKittrick Hotel rooftop — Punchdrunk/Sleep No More team
description: The McKittrick Hotel's greenhouse-and-garden rooftop bar above Sleep No More — a seasonal menu, a retro-British aesthetic, and one of the most photographed rooftop terraces in Manhattan. Closes Nov-Apr for the winter.

name: Pebble Bar
awards: Rockefeller Center townhouse bar — 2022 opening by Justin Theroux/Jackie Onassis townhouse team
description: A 1860 Rockefeller Center brownstone turned three-floor cocktail bar in 2022 — a Jackie Onassis and Justin Theroux-backed room, a tight classics-first cocktail menu, and a townhouse layout that has made it one of the hottest new Midtown reservations since opening.

name: Le Bilboquet
awards: Upper East Side French — 30-year scene-and-dining institution
description: A 60th Street French bistro that moved 20 feet three decades ago and has never stopped humming — Cajun chicken, celebrity tables, and one of the most-cited scene rooms on the Upper East Side since it opened. Nobu-level hard to get in at peak, but still operational as a neighborhood room.

name: Peking Duck House
awards: Chinatown institution — NYC's most-cited Peking duck room
description: A Mott Street Chinatown institution — the whole-bird tableside-carved Peking duck that regularly tops best-duck-in-NY lists, crispy skin with scallion pancakes and hoisin, and a Cantonese menu that fills in around the flagship dish.

name: The Lobster Place
description: Chelsea Market seafood market with a raw bar, sushi counter, and prepared seafood — a Food Network favorite since 1974. Lobster rolls, oysters, whole fish, and take-home catch.

name: Friedman's
description: All-day American at Chelsea Market and other NYC outposts. Famously 100% gluten-free-friendly kitchen with a full non-GF menu — pancakes, eggs Benedict, burgers, and sandwiches.

name: Li-Lac Chocolates
description: NYC's oldest chocolate house, founded 1923. The Chelsea Market stall sells hand-dipped truffles, molded chocolate lollipops, and the 'Rapture' fruit-and-nut clusters. Manufacturing is still all by hand.

name: Doughnuttery
description: Made-to-order mini doughnuts tossed in house-made sugar blends — grapefruit cardamom, cocoa nib hazelnut, rosemary salt. A Chelsea Market cult favorite.

name: Yankee Tavern
description: Bronx sports-bar institution across from Yankee Stadium, open since 1923. Thin-crust pizza, burgers, and cheap beer on game days — a pilgrimage for baseball lore.

name: Bronx Drafthouse
description: Craft-beer sports bar steps from Yankee Stadium. Dozens of taps, bar food, and game-day crowds — the go-to pre-game spot after leaving the train at 161st.

name: Le Café Louis Vuitton
description: Luxury French café on the fourth floor of Louis Vuitton's 57th Street flagship. Pastry chef Maxime Frédéric (formerly of Cheval Blanc Paris) leads the kitchen; the menu spans brunch, afternoon tea, and French-flair snacking in a couture-styled dining room.
address: 6 E 57th St, Floor 4, New York, NY 10022
bookingInfo: Opens 28 days ahead at midnight ET on Resy.

name: Kisa
description: Minimalist Korean diner on Allen Street channeling the kisa sikdang tradition — the taxi-driver-focused canteens of Korea. Compact menu of daily banchan-anchored sets above the F train.

name: Jean's
description: Late-night New American bistro on Lafayette, open until 4am Thursday through Saturday. Scene-forward NoHo newcomer with cocktails that keep the room running past midnight.

name: Ramen By Ra
description: Black and female-owned ramen shop on East 1st Street. Asa-ramen tradition (Japanese morning ramen) with Southern culinary influences; two-week reservation blocks released twice monthly.
bookingInfo: Two-week blocks released twice monthly on Resy.

name: I Cavallini
description: Italian small-plates and natural wine bar from the team behind Michelin-starred, James Beard-winning Four Horsemen — located directly across Grand Street from the original. Cozy Williamsburg counterpoint to its wine-bar sibling.

name: Theodora
description: Mediterranean restaurant on Greene Avenue from Chef/Owner Tomer Blechman — same team as the beloved Miss Ada down the street. Seasonal mezze, wood-fire cooking, and a polished neighborhood-restaurant feel.

name: Clemente Bar
description: Daniel Humm's intimate cocktail lounge sits one flight above Eleven Madison Park, a collaboration with artist Francesco Clemente whose three large-scale murals anchor the walnut-paneled room. Beverage director Sebastian Tollius and bar manager Richard Millwater built the cocktail program around house-fermented miso, lending umami depth to drinks like Real Talk (pretzel miso, Kings County Coffee Whisky, amontillado) and Against the Grain (Glenfiddich 12, crème de banane, plantain-banana miso). The Lounge serves à la carte; the nine-seat chef's counter (The Studio) runs an omakase-style experience and hosts sushi master Eiji Ichimura for a multi-month residency in early 2026.
dishes: Chocolate espresso sundae with milk punch

name: Bar Madonna
description: Eric Madonna's Williamsburg cocktail bar — designed by Studio Guia with art direction from KidSuper (Colm Dillane) — runs a 14-drink program from beverage director Rob Crowe, including four drafts. Chef Rob Zwirz (alum of Carbone, Babbo, Lupa) reimagines Italian-American bar food: Calabrese wings, smashed meatball Parm, house salumi. Not a red-sauce joint — a bar with an Italian soul.
bookingInfo: Opens 30 days ahead on Resy.

name: Gabriela
description: An intimate Williamsburg dancefloor and cocktail bar on Wythe Avenue, opened in 2023 in the former Kinfolk storefront. State-of-the-art A/V, a custom-designed dance floor, and natural light define the room; DJ Eli Escobar curates the music program. Upstairs runs a quieter lounge. Capacity 250 standing, 90 seated. Early reservations start at a $2,000 minimum spend; full buyouts Friday/Saturday require $30,000.

name: PopUp Bagels - Upper West Side
description: PopUp Bagels' Upper West Side outpost on Columbus Ave between W 75th and W 76th — second Manhattan location after the original on Thompson St. Same model: five bagel flavors, two core schmears, salted butter, weekly rotating schmears. Hot bagels, cream cheese on the side by the tub for dipping.

name: PopUp Bagels - Midtown East
description: Late-March 2026 opening — PopUp Bagels' Midtown East outpost at 139 E 57th St near the Lenox Hill border. Same chain model: five flavors, two core schmears, weekly rotating specialties.

name: PopUp Bagels - Chelsea
description: Early-April 2026 opening — PopUp Bagels near Penn Station at 370 7th Ave. Same chain model: five flavors only, two core schmears, weekly rotating specialties, hot bagels with cream cheese on the side for dipping.

name: Deluxx Fluxx
description: The NYC outpost of the Detroit art-club concept from Brooklyn artist duo FAILE (Patrick McNeil and Patrick Miller), opened in 2022 in the former Studio at Webster Hall. Four thousand two hundred square feet of floor-to-ceiling blacklight interiors, day-glo graffiti, custom "artfully weird" arcade games (including Crown Heights King, where pigeons battle for borough supremacy), pinball, DJs, and costumed performers. Experimental lighting design by Andi Watson (Radiohead, Prince); beverage program from James Beard-nominated restaurateur Joe Robinson. Part interactive art installation, part nightclub.

name: Damballa
description: The slightly-more-grown-up sibling to Cafe Erzulie — same Haitian-rooted team, directly across Broadway. Damballa pays homage to Haitian culture and art deco design: a wavy brown-and-white floor, floor-to-ceiling record shelves, and velvet maroon banquettes. The cocktail list leans rhum-based (Summer Sorel, Spicy PB&J) and the kitchen fries yuca crisp with a cilantro-dill dipping sauce. Sets run late — Friday and Saturday DJs often through 4am.

name: Kind Regards
description: A two-story cocktail lounge in the former Cake Shop space on Ludlow Street — upstairs lounge-forward room with banquette seating and a long bar; downstairs a darker, late-night party with DJs running to close. Bottle service available, wine-focused table pairings, and a seasonal cocktail program. Open Tuesday through Sunday from 8pm.

name: the blond
description: The cocktail lounge inside 11 Howard, the Space Copenhagen-designed boutique hotel from Aby Rosen. Arched windows face Howard Street; dark woods, subdued colors, low lighting, and gold accents set a seductive tone. Bar-bites come courtesy of next-door Le Coucou, and rotating seasonal cocktail lists pair with the room's changing mood — open and workable by day, sensual and DJ-driven by night. After 10pm, entry is by reservation, and two disco balls glow red over the dance floor. A longtime fashion-set haunt.
bookingInfo: Walk-in before 10pm; after 10pm entry is reservation-only.

name: Amber Room
description: An upscale 55 West 21st lounge that has quickly become a NYFW and celebrity hotspot — host to a Met Gala after-party, the NYFW closing party, and Carlos Alcaraz's 2024 US Open closing celebration. Plush furniture and a wide-open floor plan seat up to 150 with 400 standing and a VIP PDR for 50. Four-am liquor license; table service for large events, DJs, and brand activations. J Balvin, Rosalía, Odell Beckham, Fat Joe, and Flo Rida are on the regulars list.

name: The Sampler
description: Bushwick's first bar devoted to craft beer, open since 2013 and revitalized in 2018 by co-owners Richard Mercado (Puerto Rican) and Leo Tineo (Dominican) with partner Joel Suarez. Twenty rotating taps and 300+ bottles and cans from US micro-brewers, plus wine, signature cocktails, and a pop-up kitchen that cycles through guest chefs. Table games, local art on the walls, and a DJ most nights. Latino-owned, Bushwick-native, late open — Friday and Saturday run to 4am.

name: Grand Bar & Salon
description: The lobby lounge of the Soho Grand Hotel, a throwback to old-New-York grand-bar tradition: gold-polished marble-top oak bar, embossed leather stools with nail-head detailing, New York Public Library-inspired chandeliers, terrazzo floors with nickel strips, and woven leather banquettes along the soaring windows. Fifty rare American whiskeys and a cocktail program from Natasha David and Jeremy Oertel. Breakfast through late-night food service — Eggs Benedict at 7am, Grand Burger at 2am. Live DJs Thursday through Saturday.

name: Club Room
description: A two-room cocktail bar and live-music supper club on the second floor of the Soho Grand — Parlor and Bar Room, both drenched in velvet seating and warm lighting, with oversized Terry O'Neill black-and-white portraits on every wall. The bartenders run a mixology-history program from the 19th-century old fashioned through modernized espresso martinis. Nightly performers take the stage 7:30–8:30pm and 9:00–10:00pm — electric pianists, sultry vocalists, jazz. Cover is $25 Wed/Thu, $35 Fri/Sat (waived for hotel guests); 10pm onward turns DJ-driven with dancing through late.
bookingInfo: Resy for dining + live music; SevenRooms for late-night tables after 10pm.

name: Gilligan's
description: The Soho Grand's summer-only outdoor bar, a slice of Long Island island-life on West Broadway. Picnic tables, deck chairs, kitschy tropical decor (mermaids, hanging bananas, beach fabric). Ingredient-driven menu runs signature pizzas alongside seafood from Long Island farms and fishermen. The Frozen Watermelon Margarita is the house anchor, joined by seasonal additions like a Gin Matcha Piña Colada and a fresh Jalapeño Mezcal. Typically open May through late September.
